2026-03-10 Regular Meeting ceremonial
51m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 3-10-26

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting featuring extensive employee recognition presentations for police, fire, and service department personnel. The board approved a resolution to collect 5% cable franchise fees from Alt Fiber in addition to existing Spectrum fees, increased general fund revenue by $463,623 from host community dispensary funds, approved the purchase of dais tablets, scheduled a zoning workshop, and expressed support for a Clermont County land use plan grant application.

Employee recognition presentations for service department (winter snow operations), police, and fire personnel2025 Officer of the Year award to Officer Wesley LyonsLife-Saving Award for cardiac arrest response on January 3, 2026Drug interdiction awards for Sergeant Collier and Officer McAllisterMeritorious service commendation for Detective Sergeant McMillan (homicide, child abuse, financial crimes investigations)Traffic crash reconstructionist recognition for Officer Tyler SmithCable franchise fee resolution to collect from Alt Fiber in addition to SpectrumHost community dispensary fund revenue increase of $463,623OPWC road paving grant for Smith Fay and Charles Snyder roads (tentative approval)USPS approval to recognize Goshen as preferred mailing city name for all township addressesNew fire station construction nearing completionClermont County land use plan grant support letterZoning workshop schedulingPurchase of dais tablets for board membersDiscussion of investing dispensary revenue in money market savings account

Decisions (9)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (Feb 24 - Mar 10, 2026) passed
  • Motion to recess for approximately 15 minutes for employee recognition refreshments passed
  • Motion to schedule Board of Trustees special meeting/zoning workshop for Tuesday March 31 at 6 PM passed
  • Motion to approve purchase of eight tablets with stands for the dais at $341.75 each passed
  • Motion to approve Resolution R-011-2026 to collect 5% cable franchise fees including advertising revenue from video service providers passed
  • Motion to increase general fund revenue (host community dispensary fund) by $463,623 passed
  • Motion to approve letter of support for Clermont County countywide land use plan grant application passed
  • Motion to adjourn at 7:18 PM passed

Action Items (6)

  • Evan Corday to send franchise fee resolution to Alt Fiber and resend to Spectrum
  • Evan Corday to perform annual audits of cable franchise fee collections from Spectrum and Alt Fiber
  • Fiscal Officer Laura to roll dispensary fund money into the savings/money market account
  • Administrator Rose and Donna Rosick to finalize OPWC grant details for road paving (Smith Fay and Charles Snyder roads)
  • Board to sign and send letter of support for Clermont County land use plan grant to fiscal officer
  • Schedule and prepare for zoning workshop special meeting on March 31, inviting Zoning Commission, BZA, and Mr. Mayoff

Financials

  • $495,000 OPWC grant for road paving (tentative)
  • $135,000 local match for road paving project
  • $631,000 total road paving project cost
  • $341.75 per tablet (8 tablets for dais)
  • $463,623 increase in general fund from host community dispensary fund
  • $283,000 in dispensary back pay and first two 2026 payments received
  • ~$18,000 per month average dispensary payments going forward
  • $94,000 collected from Spectrum cable franchise fees in 2025

2026-03-02 Regular Meeting contentious
17m ▶ Vimeo

ZC 3-2-26

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held its regular meeting and addressed the continued Zoning Case 2025-010 (Westbrook's Preliminary Development Plan). The commission voted to dismiss the case on the basis that the underlying 2005 zoning map amendment failed to receive the required statutory majority vote of the Board of Township Trustees, meaning no lawful rezoning ever occurred. The property reverts to R1 zoning. Property owner Marc Orlando spoke in opposition to the dismissal, expressing concerns about having purchased the property under the assumption the PUD zoning was valid.

Legality of the 2005 zoning map amendment and Board of Trustees vote (one affirmative, one abstention, one absent - failed to meet statutory majority)Dismissal of Zoning Case ZC 2025-010 (Westbrook's Preliminary Development Plan)Property reverts to R1 zoning classification due to improper 2005 rezoningProperty owner Marc Orlando's concerns about purchasing property based on PUD zoning that was shown on maps for 20+ yearsUpcoming land use plan community meeting with Clermont County Planning Commission

Decisions (3)

  • Approved video meeting minutes from February 3, 2026 (motion by Lewis, second by Curie; passed with one abstention)
  • Dismissed Zoning Case ZC 2025-010 on the basis that the 2005 zoning map amendment failed to receive the statutory required majority vote of the Board of Township Trustees (motion by Pegram, second by Dinwiddie; passed 4-0 with 2 abstentions)
  • Meeting adjourned (motion by Curie, second by Dinwiddie; passed unanimously)

Action Items (2)

  • Staff noted the land use plan community meeting with Drew DeMarsh from Clermont County Planning Commission is scheduled for the next day (March 3, 2026) in the training/community room
  • Marc Orlando was advised to file a public records request to obtain the opinion letter and memo regarding the legality of the 2005 zoning actions

Financials

  • $2,000 - cost of independent property review referenced by Marc Orlando

2026-02-24 Regular Meeting routine
59m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 2-24-26

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting covering road salt appropriations, equipment purchases, zoning resolution amendments, a strategic plan presentation, and several community-related items. Key actions included increasing salt appropriations by $20,000, purchasing a portable washer/steamer for $14,110.65, adopting three zoning text amendments (PDM mixed-use minimum, accessory structure cap removal, and PUD lot size increase), accepting donated AEDs, and presenting the township's first five-year strategic plan. Two part-time firefighter EMTs were hired following an executive session.

Road salt appropriations increase due to harsh winterIndustrial site cleanup on State Route 28 nearing completionMedic unit with locked motor and bent frame under evaluation at HortonPortable washer/steamer purchase shared between Service and Fire departmentsThree zoning text amendments: PDM mixed-use 20% non-residential minimum, removal of 2,000 sq ft accessory structure cap, PUD lot size increase from 5,000 to 10,800 sq ftGoshen Township Historical Society Anchorage House HVAC replacement funding requestLand Use Plan survey and public meeting scheduled March 3Five-year Strategic Plan presentation (2026-2030) covering flock cameras, levy reviews, SR28 improvements, cemetery expansion, fire station improvementsAmerican 250 flag presentation recognizing Revolutionary War veterans buried in Goshen TownshipDonation of three new AEDs from Project Heart Start and surplus of three old AEDs to American Regulators USACommunity center request from resident Judy BurnsNew fire station ceremony March 8 and open house March 9Executive session for employment matters resulting in two part-time firefighter EMT hires

Decisions (17)

  • Adopted the agenda
  • Approved payment of bills for February 11-24, 2026
  • Approved Chief Myers to attend NFFF CAST team meeting in Baltimore, MD March 8-10
  • Approved increasing appropriations for salt (MBL contracted services) by $20,000
  • Approved purchase of Enzo's 4000 PSI Portable Washer Steamer with undercarriage wash unit for $14,110.65 shared by Service and Fire departments
  • Adopted Resolution R007-2026 to amend Article 14 PDM District Section 1404 to establish minimum 20% non-residential gross floor area
  • Adopted Resolution R008-2026 (amended title) to amend Article 7 Section 7.01 to remove 2,000 sq ft maximum accessory structure cap
  • Adopted Resolution R009-2026 (amended title) to increase minimum PUD residential lot size from 5,000 to 10,800 sq ft with setback adjustments
  • Accepted donation of 91 $5 gift cards for staff from Snack Pack 4L Foundation
  • Approved sending letter of support to Rep. Schmidt for Historical Society's Anchorage House HVAC state funding request
  • Approved $250 sponsorship of Goshen Derby event for Goshen Township Historical Society
  • Accepted donation of three AEDs from Project Heart Start
  • Adopted Resolution R010-2026 declaring three Phillips Park Stop AED units as obsolete and donating them to American Regulators USA
  • Entered executive session at 6:57 PM per ORC 121.22G1
  • Hired part-time firefighter EMT Jesse Blevins at $18/hour effective March 8, 2026
  • Hired part-time firefighter EMT Ethan Geiger at $18/hour effective March 8, 2026
  • Adjourned meeting at 7:25 PM

Action Items (7)

  • Administrator Rose to finalize strategic plan for adoption at next meeting, incorporating Trustee Allen's feedback
  • Director Langenheim to coordinate Land Use Plan survey public session on March 3 at 6:30 PM downstairs
  • Administrator Rose to work with Congress marketing team to draft flyer for fire station ceremony (March 8) and open house (March 9)
  • Chief Rose and Administrator Rose to schedule meeting with Historical Society to discuss HVAC funding and grant collaboration for Anchorage
  • Medic unit assessment at Horton to be completed by end of next week regarding locked motor, bent frame, and cab evaluation
  • Trustee Hodges to sign letter of support to Rep. Schmidt on behalf of Board
  • Director Meadows to purchase 200 tons of salt with the $20,000 appropriation

Financials

  • $14,110.65 for Enzo's 4000 PSI Portable Washer Steamer with undercarriage wash unit

2026-02-17 Special Meeting routine
1h 13m ▶ Vimeo

BOT Special Meeting 2-17-2026

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting zoning workshop with the Zoning Commission, Board of Zoning Appeals, and invited guest Kevin Maloff (who did not attend). Key discussions included the land use plan update progress, a proposed 20% non-residential requirement for PDM (Planned Development Mixed-use) rezonings with a 60% residential build-out trigger, removal of the 2,000 square foot aggregate cap on accessory structures, revising accessory dwelling unit (ADU) square footage maximums, and potential adoption of the International Property Maintenance Code (IPMC).

Land use plan update progress and March 3rd public workshopPDM residential and non-residential requirements - 20% business minimum and 60% residential triggerPLK Communities Smith Road apartment application as example of PDM misuse for purely residential projectsRemoval of 2,000 square foot aggregate cap on accessory structuresAccessory dwelling unit (ADU) square footage maximums and 25% proportional rule inadequacyInternational Property Maintenance Code (IPMC) adoption discussionState Route 28 corridor and downtown overlay districtHousing development pressure and market trends toward high-density residentialFederal government passing rules on increasing housing densityTiny home communities and current zoning limitationsEagles Nest mixed-use development phasing requirementsDowntown Goshen revitalization challenges and dilapidated propertiesMoratorium expiration planned for AprilComparison with neighboring communities: Loveland, Milford, Mason, Westchester, Union Township, Hamilton Township

Decisions (2)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed unanimously
  • Motion to adjourn the special meeting at 7:13 PM passed unanimously

Action Items (7)

  • Director Langenheim to prepare draft resolution language for 20% non-residential PDM requirement with 60% residential trigger for further review
  • Director Langenheim to crunch numbers and prepare scenarios on ADU square footage percentages for a future workshop
  • Director Langenheim to purchase digital and physical copies of the International Property Maintenance Code (~$80) for distribution to BZA, Zoning Commission, and Trustees for review
  • Director Langenheim to get clarification from prosecutor's office on whether IPMC fine appeals go to court or BZA
  • Director Langenheim to coordinate with Will Menz on potential digital complaint submission system for the website
  • Drew DeMarsh to help facilitate public land use plan workshop on March 3rd
  • Staff to promote the land use plan survey on website and Facebook for maximum public input

Financials

  • $80 approximate cost for one physical and one digital redline copy of the International Property Maintenance Code
  • 20% proposed minimum non-residential requirement for PDM rezonings
  • 60% residential build-out threshold trigger before non-residential development must proceed in PDM projects
  • 40% current residential requirement on PDM developments
  • 2,000 square foot current aggregate maximum for accessory structures proposed for removal
  • 800 square foot current cap on accessory dwelling units proposed for removal
  • 25% current maximum ADU size relative to primary structure
  • 7% lot area maximum for accessory structures
  • 1,400 square foot minimum home size in Goshen Township
  • $25-$75 typical property maintenance code violation fees for smaller townships

2026-02-10 Regular Meeting routine
44m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 2-10-26

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting covering a wide range of topics including approval of a 4th of July parade organized by the Goshen Chamber of Commerce, three zoning enforcement resolutions authorizing the Clermont County prosecutor to pursue violations under House Bill 315, a rezoning of parcels from M1 light industrial to B1 local business district at the former Combs cleanup site, and discussion of the Eagle's Nest development's connector road being designated as private. The board also approved police training requests, accepted a firefighter resignation, and discussed engaging Bricker legal counsel for TIF-related work on the Eagle's Nest/State Route 28 corridor project.

4th of July parade approval for America's 250th celebrationThree zoning enforcement resolutions under House Bill 315 for nuisance propertiesRezoning of Combs cleanup site parcels from M1 industrial to B1 business districtEagle's Nest development connector road designated as private roadEagle's Nest TIF discussion and potential engagement of Bricker legal counselState Route 28 corridor widening from two to five lanesFire Station 18 construction nearing completionNew medic unit delivery moved up to last quarter of 2027Land use plan public session scheduled for March 3Snow removal and winter storm response commendationPolice officer training and SRO conference approvalsDirector Langenheim completed master's degree in public administration from EKU

  • Resolution R-004-2026 adopted authorizing prosecutor to pursue zoning enforcement at 1629 Woodville Pike passed unanimously
  • Resolution R-005-2026 adopted authorizing prosecutor to pursue zoning enforcement at 7000 Willow Ridge passed unanimously
  • Resolution to initiate rezoning of parcels from M1 to B1 at Combs cleanup site passed unanimously
  • Motion to approve Officer Maggie Weiderholt's crime scene reconstruction training ($725) passed unanimously
  • Motion to approve SRO training conference in Sandusky ($830 per officer) passed unanimously
  • Motion to increase fire department appropriations by $11,500 for Tender 18 repairs passed unanimously
  • Motion to accept resignation of part-time firefighter/paramedic Steven Fisher effective Jan 29, 2026 passed unanimously
  • Motion to enter agreement with Charles E. Harris and Associates for Hinkle report preparation passed
  • Motion to approve out-of-town travel for administrative assistant Jen to symposium in Erlanger, KY ($75 registration) passed unanimously
  • Motion to appoint Trustee Hauserman as voting representative to Clermont County General Health District Advisory Council on March 19 passed (Hauserman abstained)
  • Motion to approve Eagle's Nest development connector road as a private road passed unanimously
  • Motion to accept $50 Sonic Drive-In gift card donation passed unanimously
  • Motion to adjourn at 6:44 PM passed unanimously

ZC 2-3-26

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held its regular meeting on February 3, 2026. The commission continued Zoning Case 2025-010 (Westbrook's Preliminary Development Plan major modification) to the next meeting at the applicant's request. The commission unanimously approved a text amendment (ZC 2026-001) initiated by the Board of Trustees to increase minimum lot sizes and setback requirements for residential PUD developments. The commission also voted to reschedule the March meeting from Tuesday, March 3rd to Monday, March 2nd to avoid a conflict with a community land use plan workshop.

Continuance of Zoning Case 2025-010 Westbrook's Preliminary Development Plan major modificationText amendment ZC 2026-001 to increase minimum lot sizes and setback requirements for residential PUD developmentsRescheduling March Zoning Commission meeting to avoid conflict with community land use plan workshopDiscussion of PUD residential lot standards including lot area, lot width, and facade separation distances

Decisions (4)

  • Motion to continue Zoning Case 2025-010 (Westbrook's Preliminary Development Plan) to the March meeting passed (Curry-Yes, Dinwiddie-No, Lewis-Abstain, Pegram-Yes, Purcell-Yes)
  • Motion to recommend approval of text amendment ZC 2026-001 (residential PUD lot size and setback changes) passed unanimously (5-0)
  • Motion to reschedule March Zoning Commission meeting from Tuesday March 3rd to Monday March 2nd at 6:30 PM passed unanimously
  • Approval of transcribed meeting minutes from January 6, 2026 passed unanimously

Action Items (4)

  • Staff to publish notice of rescheduled March Zoning Commission meeting (Monday, March 2, 2026 at 6:30 PM)
  • Staff to prepare for continued hearing of Zoning Case 2025-010 at March 2nd meeting
  • Staff to forward recommendation of approval of text amendment ZC 2026-001 to the Board of Trustees
  • Mr. DeMarsh from planning commission to take steps on land use plan workshop scheduled for March 3rd

Financials

  • Minimum lot area for residential PUD use increased from 5,000 sq ft to 10,800 sq ft
  • Minimum lot width for residential PUD use: 50 feet
  • Minimum distance between rear facades of homes: 60 feet
  • Minimum distance between side facades of homes increased from 10 feet to 30 feet
  • Minimum side yard setback increased to 10 feet per property (15 feet standard)
  • Base zoning R-5 lot size is 12,000 sq ft; new PUD minimum of 10,800 sq ft is approximately 1,200 sq ft less

2026-01-27 Regular Meeting routine
1h 14m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 1-27-26

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their organizational and regular meeting for 2026, electing Trustee Hauserman as Chairperson and Trustee Hodges as Vice Chairperson. The State of the Township address highlighted public safety achievements, $1.3 million growth in unencumbered funds, and ongoing infrastructure projects including Station 18 construction. A significant zoning update revealed that a 2005 rezoning of the Kazaddale Road property was never legally approved, halting a pending development. The board also addressed organizational matters, approved zoning fee schedules, discussed a proposed Station 18 parking lot expansion, and accepted two firefighter resignations.

2026 organizational meeting - election of chair and vice chairState of the Township address recapping 2025 achievementsKazaddale Road property zoning determination - 2005 rezoning found legally invalidThree-day snow storm response and costs breakdownStation 18 construction update and parking lot expansion proposal2025 fiscal year true-up showing $1.3 million unencumbered fund growthProposed rezoning of State Route 28 Land Bank parcels from M-2 industrial to B-1 business2026 zoning fee schedule updates including variance and lot split fee restructuringLand use plan development progress targeting March-April completion before moratorium endsFourth of July parade proposal from Goshen Chamber of Commerce for America's 250th anniversaryRetired K-9 Six memorialStructure fire on State Route 28 with mutual aid from four departmentsMedic unit updates - replacement ambulance delivery moved up to Q1 2028Ohio Township Association meeting and property tax reform legislationState Route 28 illegal dump site cleanup progressZoning workshop scheduled for February 17Two part-time firefighter paramedic resignations acceptedExecutive session regarding employment and discipline of a public employee

BZA 1-12-26

The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals held its organizational meeting for 2026. The board approved previous meeting minutes, adopted the 2026 meeting schedule, elected officers for the year, and cancelled the February 9th meeting due to no pending applications.

  • Elected secretary for the board (name unclear from transcript)
  • Cancelled the February 9, 2026 meeting due to no pending applications
  • Motion to adjourn passed

3m ▶ Vimeo

BOT Special Meeting 12-17-25

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting to address a 2025 budget amendment correction and to consider hiring a service department employee. The board rescinded a prior budget resolution containing typos related to the Station 18 rebuilding fund and replaced it with a corrected version. After an executive session, the board approved the hiring of Aaron Pruitt as a part-time service worker.

2025 budget amendment correction (resolution R-035-2025 replacing R-030-2025)Station 18 rebuilding fund accounting correctionsExecutive session for employee appointment/compensationHiring of part-time service department workerAcknowledgment of recent storm response efforts

Decisions (5)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed unanimously
  • Motion to rescind resolution R-030-2025 and replace it with resolution R-035-2025 passed unanimously
  • Motion to adjourn into executive session at 8:32 a.m. passed unanimously
  • Motion to hire Aaron Pruitt as part-time service worker effective 12-18-2025 at $19.25/hour on a one-year probationary period passed unanimously
  • Motion to adjourn at 8:54 a.m. passed unanimously

Action Items (1)

  • Director Meadows to process hiring of Aaron Pruitt as part-time service worker effective 12-18-2025

Financials

  • $19.25 per hour salary for new part-time service worker Aaron Pruitt
  • $2.5 million received from the state of Ohio in 2024 for Station 18 rebuilding fund (incorrectly listed as 2025 revenue in prior resolution)
  • Over $2 million was erroneously expected as 2025 state revenue in the prior resolution
Trustee AllenTrustee HausermannTrustee HodgesDirector MeadowsChief Myers (mentioned but absent due to structure fire)

2025-12-09 Regular Meeting ceremonial
1h 21m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 12-9-25

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting that included swearing in Trustees Lisa Allen and Dan Hodges for new terms, swearing in four new firefighters, and presenting life-saving awards to three firefighters who resuscitated a cardiac arrest patient. A zoning case to rezone a property on State Route 28 from B2 to M2 heavy industrial was denied unanimously due to spot zoning concerns and incompatibility with the Town Center Overlay District, though the board expressed willingness to work with the applicant on alternatives. The board also approved a zoning text amendment increasing minimum lot sizes, approved purchase of a John Deere wheel loader for $115,000, and entered a collaborative resurfacing agreement with Clermont County.

Swearing in of Trustees Lisa Allen and Dan Hodges for new termsSwearing in of four new firefighters: Anthony Addison, Jameson Benoit, Mueller, and Tyler PilcherLife-saving awards presented to firefighters Anthony Addison, Nathan Denowden, and Tyler Harding for cardiac arrest save on September 26, 2025Patient Ms. Brock publicly thanked the firefighters who saved her lifeZoning case ZC-2025-009 denial of B2 to M2 rezoning for Haywood Utility Contractors property on State Route 28 - spot zoning and Town Center Overlay District conflict concernsStation 18 construction update - on pace for April grand opening, interior stairwells and exhaust system complete, sprinkler system passed final hydro testText amendment to increase PUDR minimum lot size from 5,000 to 10,800 square feet with 30-foot setback requirementsAmerica 250 Goshen mural dedication recognition and America 250-Ohio flag requestCollaborative resurfacing agreement with Clermont County for township road improvements contingent on OPWC grantFuture land use plan changes in progress with community surveys planned, target completion March 2026 to align with moratorium timelineBudget amendment for EMS billing revenue shortfall due to state Medicaid managed care payment delaysFirst snowstorm of season handled without problems by service departmentTrustee Hodges abstained from wheel loader and backhoe votes due to employment with Murphy Tractor

Decisions (16)

  • Motion to amend the agenda to include item N1 (resurfacing agreement resolution) passed unanimously
  • Motion to adopt the agenda as amended passed unanimously
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (Nov 18 - Dec 9, 2025) passed unanimously
  • Resolution denying map amendment from B2 to M2 heavy industrial for 1800 State Route 28 (PIDN 112-207-J207) passed unanimously (zoning case ZC-2025-009 denied)
  • Resolution R031-2025 to initiate text amendments to Section 6.02C3 increasing PUDR minimum lot size to 10,800 sq ft passed unanimously
  • Resolution R030-2025 amending 2025 budget for EMS billing revenue and Station 18 building fund passed unanimously
  • Motion to approve out-of-town travel for Chief Myers to FDIC in Indianapolis (April 21-25, 2026) passed unanimously
  • Motion to increase 2025 appropriations in gasoline tax motor vehicles fund by $115,000 passed (Allen yes, Hauserman yes, Hodges abstain)
  • Motion to approve purchase of 2024 John Deere 244P wheel loader from Murphy Tractor for $115,000 passed (Allen yes, Hauserman yes, Hodges abstain)
  • Motion to sell 2017 Case 580 Super M backhoe on GovDeals with $40,000 reserve passed (Allen yes, Hauserman yes, Hodges abstain)
  • Motion to accept $790 in food gift cards from anonymous donor for police, fire, and service passed unanimously
  • Motion to cancel January 13, 2026 meeting passed unanimously
  • Motion to set January 27, 2026 as organizational and regular meeting passed unanimously
  • Resolution R033-2025 to request America 250-Ohio flag and promotional materials passed unanimously
  • Resolution R034-2025 approving resurfacing agreement with Clermont County for township road improvements passed unanimously
  • Motion to adjourn at 7:22 PM passed unanimously

Action Items (7)

  • Zoning Director Langenheim to work with Haywood family applicant to explore B2-compatible alternatives including PDB/PUD options or use variance for their property at 1800 State Route 28
  • Zoning Director Langenheim to continue work on future land use plan changes with target completion around March 2026
  • Director Meadows to list 2017 Case 580 Super M backhoe on GovDeals with $40,000 reserve
  • Administrator Rose to request two America 250-Ohio flags (one for Historical Society, one for township display)
  • Chief Myers to attend FDIC conference in Indianapolis April 21-25, 2026
  • Zoning Director Langenheim to prepare and distribute community surveys related to future land use plan
  • Haywood family encouraged to develop building plans and explore alternative zoning approaches and return with detailed proposal

Financials

  • $115,000 for purchase of 2024 John Deere 244P wheel loader from Murphy Tractor
  • $140,000 retail value of the wheel loader being purchased for $115,000
  • $40,000 reserve minimum for sale of 2017 Case 580 Super M backhoe on GovDeals
  • $790 in food gift cards accepted from anonymous donor for police, fire, and service staff
  • $600,000 estimated paving costs contingent on OPWC grant approval
  • 10,800 square feet proposed new minimum lot size for PUDR (up from 5,000 sq ft)
  • 60-month warranty included with wheel loader purchase

2025-12-02 Regular Meeting routine
1h 12m ▶ Vimeo

ZC 12-2-25

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held a public hearing on two zoning cases. Case ZC 2025-009, a request to rezone a parcel from B-2 to M-2 heavy industrial for Haywood Utility Contractors' storage yard and garage, was unanimously denied as inconsistent with the State Route 28 corridor plan and Town Center overlay district. Case ZC 2024-006, a final development plan review for expansion of Lakefield Place subdivision (sections 6 and 7), was continued to January 6, 2026 due to unresolved questions about whether a separate out lot must be included in the PUD per Board of Trustees conditions. The commission also approved meeting minutes and the 2026 meeting schedule.

Denial of B-2 to M-2 rezoning request for Haywood Utility Contractors on State Route 28State Route 28 corridor plan and Town Center overlay district incompatibility with M-2 industrial zoningSpot zoning concerns for isolated M-2 industrial districtLakefield Place subdivision expansion final development plan (sections 6 and 7)Dispute over out lot inclusion in PUD and open space calculations for Lakefield Place2026 Zoning Commission meeting schedule approvalViolation notices issued to Haywood property for unpermitted business operations

Decisions (4)

  • Approved November 4, 2025 meeting minutes (5-0, one abstention from Pegram)
  • Approved 2026 Zoning Commission meeting schedule as presented (6-0)
  • Denied zone change from B-2 to M-2 heavy industrial for ZC 2025-009 per Article 6.02 standards (6-0)
  • Continued ZC 2024-006 Lakefield Place Final Development Plan review to January 6, 2026 (6-0)

Action Items (4)

  • Zoning Commission members to review previous meeting recordings on Vimeo to determine Board of Trustees stipulations regarding the out lot inclusion in the PUD for Lakefield Place
  • John (Zoning Director) to work with Haywood family on alternative zoning options or plans that might fit B-2 requirements
  • Zoning staff to coordinate with Lakefield Place developer (Joe Perugia/Steve from Choice Engineering) before January meeting to clarify out lot status
  • Haywood applicants encouraged to work with zoning staff and potentially return with a different plan

Financials

  • 40.85 acres - size of Lakefield Place expansion parcel
  • 8,550 sq ft - minimum lot area for Lakefield Place PUD
  • 57 feet - minimum lot width for Lakefield Place PUD
  • 60 by 80 building - proposed shop building size for Haywood Utility Contractors
  • 35% - minimum open space requirement for PDR
  • 30.4% - open space for new sections alone (below 35% minimum without original out lot)

2025-11-18 Regular Meeting contentious
2h 28m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 11-18-25

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting covering two zoning cases, department updates, and community announcements. Zoning case ZC-2025-007 (PLK Communities' request to rezone 28.77 acres from M1/B2 to PDM for a 230-unit multifamily development on Smith Road) was denied 2-1 based on staff and Zoning Commission recommendations citing inconsistency with the Growth Management Plan and State Route 28 Corridor Plan. Zoning case ZC-2025-008 (Victor Capitula's request to rezone ~16 acres from R3 to PDR for 34 ranch-style condominiums on Greenstone Trace) was discussed with staff recommending approval with stipulations, though some residents raised concerns about floodplain building, stormwater runoff, and lack of direct notification.

Denial of PLK Communities 230-unit multifamily rezoning on Smith Road (ZC-2025-007)Review of Greenstone Trace 34-unit condominium development (ZC-2025-008) with resident opposition regarding floodplain concerns and stormwater runoffState Route 28 Corridor Plan and Growth Management Plan applicability debateStation 18 construction progress - steps going in, exhaust system started, floors being polished, March move-in targetMedic 2-18 chassis likely totaled after being towed to Horton in ColumbusCompleted 2023-2024 audit with clean resultsStrategic planning work session held with township leadership teamsHolly Fair weekend events and Light Up Goshen paradeReturn of Army Corporal Kibbe's remains from KoreaPrayers for Casey McLean after serious accident

Decisions (3)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed unanimously (3-0)
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (October 28 - November 18, 2025) passed unanimously (3-0)
  • Motion to deny zoning case ZC-2025-007 (PLK Communities rezoning from M1/B2 to PDM for 230-unit multifamily development) passed 2-1 (Allen yes, Hauserman yes, Hodges no)

Action Items (4)

  • Staff to continue processing zoning case ZC-2025-008 (Greenstone Trace condominiums) with stipulations including minimum 5,000 sq ft lot configuration, sidewalks on both sides of streets, and HOA maintenance documentation
  • Fire department working on replacement/repair of Medic 2-18 (chassis likely totaled, 6-12 months for return)
  • Service department completing Station 18 construction - target move-in March, grand opening April after final paving
  • Address procedural management items from completed 2023-2024 audit

Financials

  • 230 residential units proposed in PLK Communities development (denied)
  • 489 parking spaces proposed in PLK Communities development
  • 28.77 acres for PLK Communities parcel
  • 34 residential units (17 paired ranch-style condominiums) proposed for Greenstone Trace
  • 16.166 acres for Greenstone Trace parcels
  • 52% open space in Greenstone Trace development
  • 65% open space in PLK Communities development
  • 7.99 dwelling units per acre proposed by PLK Communities (under 8 max)
  • 6-12 months estimated for Medic 2-18 return

2025-11-17 Other routine
1h 13m ▶ Vimeo

BZA-11-17-2025

The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals held a public hearing on three variance cases. Cases BZA 2025-020 and 2025-021, continued from October 20, involved Rachel and John Walker's request for variances to operate an agritourism/vendor market business on two R2-zoned parcels on Kerbet Road, including a 6,240 square foot barn for a farmer's market and allowance for secondary vendors and food trucks. Case BZA 2025-022 involved a request for a variance to build a 6,000 square foot aircraft hangar on a property adjacent to a private airstrip on Huntley Road. All three variances were approved unanimously 4-0.

Agritourism vs commercial use determination for farmer's market/vendor operationParking surface material debate - crushed limestone chips vs paved surface requirementsDust mitigation concerns for gravel driveway and parkingAmplified music concerns from neighborsTraffic control on Kerbet Road for vendor market eventsAircraft hangar variance for private airstrip property on Huntley RoadTree preservation along Huntley Road for hangar propertySchool bus turnaround and road vacation history on Huntley Road2021 zoning resolution change adding 2,000 sq ft aggregate limit for accessory structures

Decisions (4)

  • Approved minutes from October 20, 2025 meeting (4-0)
  • Approved BZA 2025-020 and 2025-021 variances for agritourism/farmer's market operation on Kerbet Road with no stipulation for paved entrance parking (4-0)
  • Approved BZA 2025-022 variance for 6,000 square foot aircraft hangar on Huntley Road (4-0)
  • Motion to adjourn approved (4-0)

Action Items (3)

  • Staff to publish findings of fact for BZA 2025-020/021 and begin permit process for the Walkers
  • Staff to publish findings of fact for BZA 2025-022 and begin permit process for the hangar
  • Staff to send email to solidify next year's BZA meeting dates if no new cases are filed by December 2nd deadline

Financials

  • 6,240 square foot barn proposed for farmer's market (BZA 2025-020/021)
  • 800 square foot maximum allowed for farmer's market use without variance
  • Maximum 200 vendors/food trucks combined for vendor market events
  • 5-acre parking area proposed for agritourism property
  • 4,000 square foot hangar to be built initially with expansion to 6,000 square feet (BZA 2025-022)
  • 2,000 square foot maximum for accessory structures per current zoning code
  • Events limited to no more than 2 times per month, May through November

2025-11-04 Other contentious
1h 10m ▶ Vimeo

ZC 11-4-2025

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held a public hearing on two zoning cases. Case ZC2025-007 involved a request to rezone 28.7 acres from M1/B2 to Planned Development Mixed Use (PDM) for a 230-unit apartment and townhome community proposed by PLK Communities; the commission voted unanimously to recommend denial based on inconsistency with the Growth Management Plan and zoning resolution requirements. Case ZC2025-008 involved a request to rezone 16.16 acres from R3 to Planned Development Residential (PDR) for 34 paired ranch-style condominiums by Capital Properties; the commission voted unanimously to recommend approval with stipulations including 5,000 sq ft lot assignments per unit and sidewalks on both sides of the road.

Rezoning request from M1/B2 to PDM for 230-unit apartment/townhome development (ZC2025-007)Dispute over whether State Route 28 PDO or Growth Management Plan governs ZC2025-007 propertySplit zoning issues on the ZC2025-007 parcel and 2018 rezoning historyGrowth Management Plan density limits (1.5 units/acre rural suburban vs 8 units/acre transitional residential)Clermont County Planning Commission recommended approval of ZC2025-007 but Township staff recommended denialRezoning request from R3 to PDR for 34-unit paired ranch condo extension (ZC2025-008)Recent minimum lot size changes from 10,000 sq ft to 25,000 sq ft necessitating PDR application for ZC2025-008Resident concerns about traffic congestion on Sandwood Drive and State Route 48 accessAdjacent property owner concerns about buffer zones and existing recreational use of neighboring land

Decisions (4)

  • ZC2025-007: Motion to recommend denial of rezoning from M1/B2 to PDM for 230-unit apartment/townhome development passed unanimously (Currie-Yes, Dinwiddie-Yes, Lewis-Yes, Otteke-Yes, Purcell-Yes)
  • ZC2025-008: Motion to recommend approval with stipulations of rezoning from R3 to PDR for 34-unit paired ranch condo development passed unanimously (Currie-Yes, Dinwiddie-Yes, Lewis-Yes, Otteke-Yes, Purcell-Yes)
  • Minutes from September 2, 2025 meeting approved unanimously
  • Motion to adjourn approved

Action Items (5)

  • ZC2025-007 to proceed to Board of Trustees for decision on November 18, 2025
  • ZC2025-008 to proceed to Board of Trustees for decision
  • Traffic impact study required by Clermont County Engineer for ZC2025-007
  • Traffic impact study required by Clermont County Engineer for ZC2025-008
  • ZC2025-008 developer to incorporate 5,000 sq ft lot assignments per unit and sidewalks on both sides of the road per staff stipulations

Financials

  • $45 million estimated project investment for ZC2025-007 (PLK Communities apartment development)
  • 230 apartments and townhomes proposed in ZC2025-007 across 28.7 acres
  • 489 parking spaces proposed for ZC2025-007
  • 7.99 units per acre density proposed for ZC2025-007 (max 8 allowed under PDM)
  • 34 residential units (17 paired ranch condos) proposed in ZC2025-008 across 16.16 acres
  • 52% open space proposed for ZC2025-008 (25% minimum required)
  • 6,750 sq ft per lot proposed for ZC2025-008 units (5,000 sq ft staff minimum recommendation)

2025-10-28 Regular Meeting routine
1h 42m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 10-28-25

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting covering a wide range of agenda items including approval of bills, donations for Fire Prevention Week and National First Responders Day, grant acceptances for traffic enforcement and paramedic training, and a comprehensive 2026 budget presentation by Administrator Rose. The budget presentation detailed revenue challenges across all departments, road paving funding shortfalls, restricted vs. unrestricted funds, levy structures, and capital equipment needs, with trustees providing feedback on deferring certain purchases like a dump truck, ALS vehicle, and grizzly struts while prioritizing safety-related equipment.

2026 comprehensive budget presentation across all departmentsRoad paving funding crisis - township lacks dedicated road levy unlike neighboring townshipsPotential second $5 PMVL registration fee to generate ~$100,000 for roadsCommunity host fund (cannabis dispensary revenue) still not received statewideStation 18 fire station construction progress - asphalt down, permanent power and gas connected, on budgetAmbulance involved in crash reducing fleet to three medic unitsSpeed study requested for Linton Road (40 mph zone)Capital equipment needs discussion - cruisers, dump truck, front loader, tractor, quint, cardiac monitorsNational Opioid Settlement opt-in for additional fundsInterest earnings from investing unencumbered funds significantly increasedProperty tax re-evaluation impacts and potential state rollbackFire department EMS billing lower than expected ($710K vs $825K estimate) due to Medicaid delaysJohn Becker announced candidacy for Clermont County AuditorSam Lever baseball history presentation by Jim PoeUpcoming community events: Light Up Goshen Parade, Lions Holly Fair, Trunk or TreatChief Myers upcoming wedding congratulations

Decisions (17)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (Sept 23 - Oct 28, 2025) passed
  • Motion to approve Goshen Chamber of Commerce Light Up Goshen Parade on Nov 22, 2025 passed
  • Motion to approve National Opioid Settlement with multiple companies (CL-1765935) passed
  • Motion to sell obsolete floor tiles and portable radios on GovDeals passed
  • Motion to approve staff attendance at OTA 2026 Annual Winter Conference (Feb 4-6, 2026) passed
  • Motion to approve ADP payroll provider renewal with 20% cost concession for three years passed
  • Motion to approve Ohio Plan insurance renewal with Highline (effective 10-23-25) with $494 premium decrease passed
  • Resolution R-024-2025 accepting tax amounts/rates and certifying to county auditor passed
  • Resolution R-025-2025 requesting speed study on Linton Road passed
  • Motion to accept $750 donation from CPAA for Fire Prevention Week passed
  • Motion to accept $751 donation from Lions Club for Fire Prevention Week passed
  • Motion to accept $250 in Kroger gift cards from CPAA for National First Responders Day passed
  • Motion to accept $250 in Chick-fil-A gift cards from CPAA for National First Responders Day passed
  • Motion to authorize purchase of 10-foot Norway spruce and run electric to it passed
  • Motion to accept $58,728.24 grant from Ohio Dept of Public Safety for overtime traffic enforcement passed
  • Motion to accept $28,571.42 AFG grant for paramedic training passed

Action Items (7)

  • Administrator Rose to meet individually with trustees to finalize 2026 budget decisions
  • Chief Rose to look into leasing options for police cruisers instead of purchasing
  • Chief Myers to explore trade-in options for older ALS vehicles toward a new Tahoe/Explorer-type ALS vehicle
  • Chief Myers to explore community donation opportunities for grizzly struts purchase
  • Chad Meadows to order 10-foot Norway spruce and coordinate electric installation before Christmas
  • Department heads to refine budget proposals based on trustee feedback on deferred purchases (dump truck, grizzly struts, second cruiser)
  • Budget discussion to continue at next meeting

BZA 10-20-25

The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals held a public hearing on five cases. Two variances (BZA 2025-016 and 017) were approved allowing creation of flag lots at 5830 Deerfield Road. A variance (BZA 2025-019) for the Goshen Public Library to install an electronic message board sign at seven feet height was approved pending ODOT approval. Cases BZA 2025-020 and 2025-021 regarding an agritourism/farmer's market operation at 1867 Kerbet Road were continued to the next meeting after the board entered deliberative session and determined additional questions needed to be addressed.

  • BZA 2025-016: Approved variance allowing creation of flag lots in R3 district at 5830 Deerfield Road
  • BZA 2025-017: Approved variance allowing creation of more than two flag lots plus interior lot at 5830 Deerfield Road
  • BZA 2025-019: Approved variance for Goshen Public Library electronic message board sign at seven feet height, pending ODOT approval
  • Motion to enter deliberative executive session approved
  • BZA 2025-020 and BZA 2025-021: Continued to next meeting (November 17, 2025)
  • Approval of September 22, 2025 meeting minutes
  • Motion to adjourn approved

2025-09-23 Regular Meeting routine
1h 32m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 9-23-25

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting featuring a major public hearing on zoning case ZC-2025-006 for the Eagles Nest Golf Course redevelopment into a mixed-use community with commercial, residential, apartments, and townhomes. The board approved the zoning change with conditions, accepted an ODOT safety sign grant of $58,543.81, scheduled a strategic planning workshop, accepted a heritage tree from the Clermont County Park District, and passed a resolution to pursue changing the township's postal address designation with USPS.

Eagles Nest Golf Course rezoning to mixed-use development (ZC-2025-006) - commercial, residential, apartments, townhomesODOT traffic study and road improvements for State Route 28 corridorSentencing conclusion in Evelyn Kammerer child death case (19-24 years and 5 years for defendants)ODOT Township Safety Sign Grant for multiple township roadsStrategic planning workshop scheduling for next 5-year planClermont County Park District heritage white oak tree for America 250USPS postal address designation change resolution for Goshen TownshipPolice policy revision for outside duty employment cruiser ratesTurn arrow installation at Route 28 and Goshen Road intersectionGrocery store anchor tenant negotiations (unnamed national chain)School capacity concerns related to new development growthFlock camera requests for development egress points

Decisions (10)

  • Adopted the meeting agenda
  • Approved payment of bills for September 10-23, 2025
  • Approved zoning case ZC-2025-006 (Eagles Nest Golf Course rezoning to PD Mixed Use) with conditions including traffic safety measures, speed limits, road design standards, ODOT recommendations compliance, phased development schedule, and matching township street signs
  • Scheduled special Board of Trustees strategic planning workshop for Thursday, November 13 from noon to 6 PM
  • Accepted ODOT Township Safety Sign Grant up to $58,543.81
  • Accepted Clermont County Park District heritage white oak tree for America 250 celebration
  • Accepted donation of $345 in meal gift cards from TQL for police officers
  • Approved revised Police Policy 504 (Outside and Extra Duty Employment) raising cruiser rate from $10 to $15 per hour
  • Approved Resolution R-023-2025 to pursue changing USPS postal address designation to Goshen
  • Adjourned meeting at 7:33 PM

Action Items (7)

  • Director Meadows to send list of roads included in ODOT sign grant to trustees
  • Director Meadows to prepare site at Jones House for heritage white oak tree planting (remove/grind existing stump)
  • Staff to coordinate with USPS on postal address/boundary change for Goshen Township
  • Director Langenheim/staff to work with Drees on development agreement incorporating approved stipulations
  • Administrator Rose to organize strategic planning workshop for November 13
  • Director Meadows to look into costs of flashing warning signs for stop sign intersections
  • Drees/developer to comply with ODOT traffic study recommendations including likely traffic light at main entrance

Financials

  • $58,543.81 - ODOT Township Safety Sign Grant accepted
  • $345 - TQL meal gift card donation for police officers ($15 per officer x 23 officers)
  • $150 million - referenced as scale of Drees' Montgomery Quarters mixed-use development experience
  • $1,200 to $1,900/month - expected apartment rents at Eagles Nest development
  • $325,000 to $419,000 - expected townhome prices at Eagles Nest development
  • $15/hour - new cruiser rate for outside/extra duty employment (increased from $10/hour)
  • 93 sixty-foot lots, 84 fifty-two-foot lots, ~252 apartments, 54 Type 1 townhomes, 72 Type 2 townhomes proposed
  • 163 acres total development, ~28 acres commercial, 46+ acres open space

BOT 9-9-25

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting on September 9, 2025 with Trustee Allen absent. Key topics included a public presentation by Rachel Walker about hosting vendor markets and festivals on her 40-acre property on Kerbet Road, a presentation from the Clermont County Mental Health and Recovery Board about their renewal levy on the November ballot, and several financial and administrative motions. The board also went into executive session to discuss public employee matters.

Rachel Walker's proposal for vintage artisan vendor markets and family-friendly festivals on 40-acre Kerbet Road propertyClermont County Mental Health and Recovery Board renewal levy presentation for November ballotStation 18 construction update – approximately two weeks ahead of scheduleZoning workshop discussion on increasing minimum lot size in PUD restrictions and distance between housesMoratorium on single-family home developmentHeavy rescue training class using donated semi trucks from City ServiceDrug fund appropriation transfer from equitable sharing/DEA fundsARPA Ambulance Retention Grant acceptance for audit complianceExecutive session regarding public employee appointment, employment, discipline, or compensation

Decisions (8)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda – passed
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (August 27, 2025 through September 9, 2025) – passed
  • Motion to increase appropriations in drug fund motor vehicles (22217607500000) in the amount of $17,904.93 – passed
  • Motion to accept donation of three semis and one semi trailer from City Service for heavy rescue training and approve disposal via scrap upon completion – passed
  • Motion to accept the 2024 OBM ARPA Ohio Ambulance Retention Grant in the amount of $41,761 retroactively to 7-16-2024 – passed
  • Motion to renew lease with Quadrant for postage machine – passed
  • Motion to adjourn into Executive Session at 6:21 PM – passed
  • Motion to adjourn the meeting at 7:11 PM – passed

Action Items (4)

  • Planning and Zoning Director to contact Rachel Walker regarding commercial conditional use permit process for vendor markets/festivals on Kerbet Road property
  • Rachel Walker to present project to BZA at the October meeting
  • Chief Myers to obtain titles for cement truck and finalize arrangements for heavy rescue training class at end of September
  • Chief Myers to take photos of heavy rescue training class

Financials

  • $17,904.93 – increase in appropriations in drug fund motor vehicles from equitable sharing (DEA) funds
  • $41,761 – 2024 OBM ARPA Ohio Ambulance Retention Grant accepted retroactively
  • $73.12/month – renewed postage machine lease rate (down from $73.26)
  • $13.04 (corrected from $35.04) – estimated annual increase in postage costs due to rate increase
  • $3.3 million/year – revenue generated by Clermont County Mental Health and Recovery Board renewal levy
  • $15 per $100,000 valuation – cost of Mental Health and Recovery Board renewal levy
  • 0.75 mills – Mental Health and Recovery Board renewal levy rate

2025-09-08 Special Meeting routine
1h 41m ▶ Vimeo

BOT Zoning Workshop 9-8-25

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special zoning workshop with the Zoning Commission, Board of Zoning Appeals, and Clermont County Planner Drew Demarche. Demarche presented on a proposed land use plan for the township, covering county development trends, community surveys, and corridor planning goals. The board discussed potential zoning resolution amendments including minimum lot sizes for planned developments (PDR/PDM), setback requirements, overlay districts vs. base zoning for PUDs, mixed-use density ratios, and garage/yard sale regulations. The board approved township staff working with county staff to create a land use plan.

Land use plan development with Clermont CountyCounty housing study and economic development planPDR/PDM minimum lot sizes and setback requirementsOverlay districts vs. base zoning layer for PUDsState Route 28 corridor planning and traffic concerns with ODOTMixed-use development density ratios (60/40 residential/commercial)Garage and yard sale regulation to prevent junk accumulationTransient vendor regulations for roadside sellersPreserving rural character and farmland while managing growthBuilding facade and material quality standards (brick construction)

Decisions (3)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda – passed unanimously
  • Motion to approve Township staff to work with Clermont County staff to create a land use plan – passed unanimously
  • Motion to adjourn at 7:42 p.m. – passed unanimously

Action Items (6)

  • Township staff (Director Langenheim) to work with Clermont County staff (Drew Demarche) to develop a land use plan
  • Drew Demarche to research community survey methods including EDDM (Every Door Direct Mail) for community input
  • Director Langenheim to research overlay district approach for PUDs and formulate potential language changes
  • Director Langenheim to research the 60/40 residential-to-commercial split for PDM districts to ensure it doesn't limit the township
  • Staff to draft garage/yard sale regulations including a three-consecutive-day limit and complaint-based enforcement
  • Staff to look into transient vendor regulations for roadside sellers

Financials

  • Current PDR minimum lot size: 5,000 square feet
  • Current most dense lot size (R5): 12,000 square feet
  • Miami Township minimum lot size: 8,400 square feet with 70-foot minimum lot width
  • Half-acre lot size reference: 21,780 square feet
  • Current PUD side setback: 5 feet from property line (10 feet between facades)
  • Proposed setback increase: 15 feet from property line (30 feet between facades)
  • Proposed PDM split: 60% max residential, 40% commercial
  • Proposed lot size increase to 5,800 square feet for PDR

2025-09-02 Special Meeting routine
1h 0m ▶ Vimeo

ZC 9-2-25

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held a public hearing on zoning case ZC 2025-006, a preliminary development plan for the Eagle's Nest Golf Course site submitted by Drees Homes. The proposal seeks to rezone 163.38 acres from PUD/PDBB to Planned Development Mixed Use (PDM), encompassing 177 single-family homes, 126 townhomes, 252 apartments, and approximately 28 acres of commercial space including a grocery store, gas station, and retail locations. After staff presentation, applicant testimony, and limited public comment, the Commission voted unanimously to recommend approval with 10+ stipulations including a traffic impact study, phased development schedule, and minimum square footage requirements.

Rezoning of Eagle's Nest Golf Course from PUD/PDBB to PDM (Planned Development Mixed Use)Preliminary development plan review for Drees Homes mixed-use communityTraffic impact study requirements from Clermont County Engineer and ODOTPolice Department request for flock safety cameras at all ingress/egress pointsBuffering and screening requirements for adjacent residential propertiesPhased development schedule and commercial priorityHOA structure including master HOA and residential HOACincinnati United Soccer Club facility interconnectionGoshen Township zoning code revision process ongoingService Department requests for centralized mailboxes and no streetscape between curb and sidewalksDevelopment agreement considerations including TIF and infrastructure

Decisions (4)

  • Approved minutes from July 1, 2025 meeting (motion by Lewis, second by Curry)
  • Unanimously recommended approval of zoning case ZC 2025-006 rezoning parcel 112203B047 from PUD/PDBB to PDM with stipulations (motion by Pegram, second by Dinwiddie, 6-0)
  • October Zoning Commission meeting canceled due to no pending applications
  • Meeting adjourned at approximately 7:30 PM

Action Items (11)

  • Developer (Drees Homes) to complete ODOT traffic impact study covering State Route 28, Smith Road, Fay Road, and State Route 48 and implement warranted items
  • Developer to provide a phased development schedule with commercial properties as priority and 50% phase completion requirement before proceeding to next phase
  • Developer to provide elevation drawings for businesses to confirm conformance with Goshen Township architectural standards
  • Developer to provide floor plans showing apartments meet 700/750 sq ft minimum and single-family homes meet 1,400 sq ft minimum
  • Developer to provide drawings showing pedestrian connectivity between residential and commercial areas
  • Developer to consider a development agreement with Goshen Township addressing flock cameras, existing TIF, infrastructure financing, and road improvements
  • Developer to continue conversations with Cincinnati United Soccer Club about interconnection and traffic relief on State Route 28
  • No streetscape to be placed between curb and sidewalks per Service Department requirement
  • Temporary and permanent signage to include Goshen Township where applicable
  • Street signs must match Goshen Township color and style standards
  • Zoning Commission, BZA, and Trustees to attend special joint workshop meeting on Monday September 8, 2025 at 6 PM regarding zoning code revisions

Financials

  • 163.38 acres total site area
  • 93 single-family units at 60-foot width
  • 84 single-family units at 52-foot width
  • 252 apartments (21 acres at approximately 12 units per acre)
  • 54 townhome units (18 three-unit buildings) plus 126 townhomes total mentioned
  • 177 detached single-family homes
  • 28 acres of commercial space
  • 46 acres of open space
  • 555 total residential units referenced from earlier plan
  • 10 total retail units
  • 1,126 commercial parking spaces proposed
  • 2,831 residential parking spaces proposed
  • 34.8% total proposed impervious area
  • Apartment rents expected $1,200 to $1,900 per month
  • Apartment sizes expected 750 to 1,400 sq ft
  • 1,400 sq ft minimum for single-family dwellings
  • 700-750 sq ft minimum for apartment units
  • 50-foot buffer required adjacent to residential properties
  • 50 fewer detached lots and 50 fewer apartments than originally proposed

BOT 8-26-25

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting covering a wide range of topics including a cybersecurity policy adoption, a detailed presentation on the Sunnymead TIF district with a resolution to seek its elimination, a comprehensive strategic plan review, and several fire department personnel actions. The board also approved pursuing an OPWC grant for 2026 road paving in collaboration with the Clermont County Engineer, approved street lighting assessment adjustments, and scheduled a zoning workshop for September 8, 2025.

Critical incident at Country Lake Apartments involving officers serving felony warrantStation 18 fire station construction progress - two weeks ahead of scheduleCybersecurity policy adoption per state law SB 929Zoning workshop scheduled for September 8, 2025Tax Incentive Review Council (TIRC) update - Bryan Equipment CRA in good standingPurdue Pharma/Sackler family opioid settlement fund opt-inSurplus and sale of 2018 Ford Police InterceptorStreet lighting district assessment adjustments (Resolution R-021-2025)OPWC grant pursuit for 2026 road paving - Smith, Snyder, and Fay RoadsCounty paving collaborative with Clermont County EngineerDetailed presentation on Sunnymead TIF district history and request for elimination (Resolution R-022-2025)Comprehensive five-year strategic plan review and updateDiscussion of road paving challenges without a roads levyFuture land use plan development with county planning officeODOT traffic signal improvements at Goshen/SR 28 intersectionODOT speed reduction request on SR 28 near Linton Road following serious accidentGibson and Belfast Road paving nearly completeFire department personnel promotions and hiring (Mueller, Pilcher, Addison, Denowden)Discussion of TIFs, JEDs, RIDs, and NCAs as economic development toolsDries Homes proposed Eagle's Nest/Villages of Belmont expansion project

Decisions (16)

  • Adopted the meeting agenda
  • Approved payment of bills for July 22, 2025 through August 26, 2025
  • Adopted cybersecurity policy as required by state law (SB 929)
  • Scheduled special zoning workshop meeting for September 8, 2025 at 6 p.m.
  • Approved settlement agreement for Purdue Pharma LP and Sackler family opioid settlement funds (CL-1736170)
  • Approved out-of-town travel for Officer Bucksap to attend Canine Cop Police Working Dog Conference in Nashville, TN ($295 registration)
  • Declared surplus and authorized sale on GovDeals of 2018 Ford Police Interceptor (VIN ending 0359)
  • Approved Resolution R-021-2025 adjusting street lighting district assessments
  • Approved township staff to pursue OPWC grant for road paving and participate in county paving collaborative
  • Approved Resolution R-022-2025 requesting elimination of Sunnymead TIF district by Clermont County Board of Commissioners
  • Entered executive session at 7:03 p.m. under ORC 121.22 G1 and G4
  • Promoted part-time firefighter EMT Cameron Mueller to full-time effective September 7, 2025
  • Promoted part-time firefighter EMT Tyler Pilcher to full-time effective September 7, 2025
  • Hired firefighter paramedic Anthony Addison full-time effective September 7, 2025
  • Rehired part-time firefighter paramedic Nathan Denowden at $22.50/hour effective September 7, 2025
  • Adjourned meeting at 8:10 p.m.

  • Administrator Rose to sign documents for Purdue Pharma/Sackler family opioid settlement opt-in

  • Director Meadows to confirm participation in county paving collaborative by September 30 deadline

BZA 8-18-25

The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals (BZA) held a public hearing on case BZA 2025-010, a conditional use request to allow the Goshen School Board to use a former Kindercare building at 6400 Patricia Boulevard as government offices in an R3 zoning district. The staff recommended approval, noting less traffic than the previous daycare use and no adverse impacts. No public comments were received for or against. The BZA approved the conditional use and also approved a variance suspending the requirement that the use not have primary access from a residential street, since the driveway was pre-existing.

Conditional use approval for Goshen School Board government offices at former Kindercare buildingTraffic impact comparison between previous daycare use and proposed office useVariance for residential street access for conditional useAbsence of school board representative at the hearingPrevious denied Domino's rezoning request for nearby vacant parcelDisposition of playground equipment on property

Decisions (4)

  • Approved minutes from July 21, 2025 BZA meeting
  • Approved conditional use BZA 2025-010 for government offices at 6400 Patricia Boulevard in R3 district
  • Approved suspension of the requirement that the proposed use not have primary access on a local residential street due to pre-existing structure and driveway
  • Motion to adjourn at 6:56 PM carried

Action Items (2)

  • Zoning staff to notify board members if a September BZA meeting is needed after August 31st application deadline
  • John to handle next BZA meeting in place of current zoning staff member

Financials

  • None recorded

2025-08-07 Special Meeting contentious
1h 6m ▶ Vimeo

BOT Special 8-7-25

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting to deliberate on Zoning Case ZC-2025-001, a proposed MAP amendment from M1 Light Manufacturing to PD Development District for a $50 million apartment project on Smith Road. The trustees voted 2-1 to deny the rezoning request, with Trustees Allen and Hauserman wanting to preserve the M1 industrial zoning for future business development. The board also approved two zoning resolution text amendments regarding residential site development standards and prohibited activities, and held a workshop with the Zoning Commission, BZA, and attorney Kevin Mayloff to discuss potential future zoning changes including PDR lot sizes, setbacks, and PDM business requirements.

Denial of rezoning from M1 Light Manufacturing to PD Development District for apartment project on Smith RoadPreservation of M1 industrial zoning for future business and economic developmentState Route 28 corridor plan and business development strategyResidential site development standards modification (Table 5-2) changing lot sizes from 5 to 3 acresProhibited activities zoning resolution modification related to property maintenance and House Bill 315PDR minimum lot size discussion - elimination vs. increase as negotiation toolPDM business/industrial requirement addition to prevent PDM from being used as higher-density PDR alternativeSide yard setback requirements - proposal to increase to 20 feet with 7-foot minimum per sideMoratorium on high-density single-family home developmentsOverlay district protections for downtown area and potential Eagle's Nest development

Decisions (5)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed 3-0
  • Resolution denying MAP amendment from M1 to PD Development District for property on north side of Smith Road (ZC-2025-001) passed 2-1 (Allen yes, Hauserman yes, Hodges nay)
  • Resolution R-019-2025 modifying Article 5 Table 5-2 Site Development Standards for Residential Zoning Districts passed 3-0
  • Resolution R-020-2025 modifying Article 5.03A3 Prohibited Activities passed 3-0
  • Motion to adjourn at 7:05 p.m. passed 3-0

Action Items (5)

  • Director Langenheim to research and prepare information on pros/cons of eliminating vs. increasing minimum lot sizes for PDR developments
  • Director Langenheim to research neighboring townships' minimum lot sizes (Miami Township noted at approximately 8,000 sq ft)
  • Director Langenheim to prepare analysis of how setbacks interact with lot size requirements and negotiation implications
  • Director Langenheim and Julia to research how to handle PDM business/residential phasing requirements (business built before or concurrent with residential)
  • Director Langenheim to prepare information on PDM business requirement scenarios, especially related to potential overlay district projects like Eagle's Nest area

Financials

  • $50 million investment proposed for the denied apartment project
  • $67,000 spent by prior Board of Trustees on the State Route 28 corridor plan
  • 5,000 square foot current minimum lot size for PDR
  • 12,000 square foot new minimum lot size for R5 residential (effective in 30 days)
  • 20 foot side setbacks established for R1 through R3 residential districts
  • 1% income tax paid by apartment residents to the school district (referenced in discussion)
  • 50% minimum open space requirement with 4 units/acre gross density and 8 units/acre net density
  • 35% minimum open space requirement with 3 units/acre gross density and 6 units/acre net density
  • 5-25% floor area proposed business/light industrial requirement for PDM

BOT 7-22-25

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting covering a full agenda including adoption of the Adams-Clermont Solid Waste District plan, multiple zoning cases (a zone change/PDP for a 230-unit apartment development by PLK Communities which was tabled for further deliberation, and two text amendments that were approved), purchase of copiers, HVAC replacement, and other administrative items. The board also scheduled a special meeting for August 7th to continue the zoning workshop and deliberate on the PLK Communities zone change case.

Adams-Clermont Solid Waste District 2025 plan presentation and ratificationPLK Communities zone change request from M1 to PDM for 230-unit apartment/townhome development on Smith RoadConcern over losing M1 (light manufacturing) zoned land for future economic developmentText amendment to Section 5.03 regarding prohibited activities (nuisance, junk vehicles, vegetation)Text amendment to Table 5-2 residential development standards (lot sizes and setbacks for R1-R5 districts)Station 18 fire station construction progress - two weeks ahead of schedule, brick work startingBeam signing ceremony for new fire stationClermont County Fair happening in OwensvilleCopier lease vs. purchase analysisHVAC replacement for admin buildingScheduling of August 7 zoning workshop and deliberation sessionFlock safety camera requirement discussion for new developments

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed unanimously
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (June 10 - July 22, 2025) passed unanimously
  • Resolution R-017-2025 approving the Adams-Clermont Solid Waste District solid waste management plan passed unanimously
  • Zoning Case ZC-2025-001 (PLK Communities zone change from M1 to PDM) motion to approve died for lack of a second; tabled for August 7 special meeting
  • Zoning Case ZC-2025-003 text amendments (prohibited activities, Section 5.03) approved unanimously
  • Zoning Case ZC-2025-004 text amendments (Table 5-2 residential development standards) approved with modifications: R1 single family dwellings changed to 3 acres, all other principal uses kept at 5 acres; passed unanimously
  • Resolution R-014-2025 authorizing Bob Rose to seek unclaimed funds from Ohio Department of Commerce passed unanimously
  • Motion to schedule special meeting/zoning workshop for August 7, 2025 at 6 PM passed unanimously
  • Motion to approve out-of-town travel for Sergeant McMillan for homicide investigation training ($1,475) passed unanimously
  • Motion to purchase two Sharp MX3051 copiers for $2,686 from Millennial Business Systems passed unanimously
  • Motion to accept resignation of James Pruitt effective June 10, 2025 passed unanimously
  • Motion to approve HVAC replacement by Tribble Refrigeration at $19,580 plus permit/inspection costs (discussion began, transcript truncated)

BZA 7-21-2025

The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals held a public hearing on two variance cases. BZA 2025-008 involved a request to keep 11 chickens on a lot less than one acre (where only 4 are permitted), which was approved with the cap at 11 and containment/enclosure conditions. BZA 2025-009 involved a request for a 10-foot height variance to allow a 20-foot pole sign for a Marathon Gas Station at 1415 State Route 28, which was also approved with a condition to dim the sign during nighttime hours.

Variance for keeping 11 chickens on a lot under one acre (BZA 2025-008)Comparison of chicken regulations with Miami Township (6 allowed vs Goshen's 4)Height variance for 20-foot Marathon Gas Station pole sign (BZA 2025-009)Electronic messaging center (EMC) on proposed sign and potential driver distraction concernsImpact of illuminated sign on nearby residential propertiesPotential future State Route 28 road wideningMarathon location to include multiple tenants including Blimpie and restaurantsGoshen Warriors community panel on proposed signUpcoming Bray Concrete application for property behind Kroger's

Decisions (4)

  • BZA 2025-008: Approved variance to allow 11 chickens on a lot less than one acre with conditions that the enclosure and containment must fall within Goshen zoning resolution guidelines and the number is capped at 11
  • BZA 2025-009: Approved 10-foot height variance allowing a 20-foot pole sign for the Marathon Gas Station with condition that the sign be dimmed as best possible during nighttime hours
  • Approval of video minutes from June 16th meeting
  • Motion to adjourn approved

Action Items (3)

  • Zoning staff to research possible amendments to the small livestock regulations for lots under one acre for future consideration
  • Zoning staff to post meeting notices with more advanced notice than 12 hours
  • Zoning staff to follow up on Bray Concrete application and request letter of intent and full plans for property behind Kroger's

  • Couple million dollars investment in the Marathon store by the owner
  • 0.49 acres - size of chicken variance property at 6736 Goshen Road
  • 3.24 acres - size of Marathon Gas Station property at 1415 State Route 28
  • 98 square feet - proposed sign area (within 100 sq ft maximum)

2025-07-10 Special Meeting routine
4m ▶ Vimeo

BOT Special Meeting 7-10-25

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting to address financing of a fire truck. The board rescinded a previous resolution and adopted a new, more detailed resolution with additional bond language as recommended by a bond attorney after concerns were raised by legal counsel Brian Shrive about the original bond language in the Flagstar master lease agreement. The meeting lasted approximately five minutes.

Fire truck acquisition financingMaster lease agreement with FlagstarBond language revisions recommended by bond attorneyRescinding previous resolution and adopting revised resolution with proper legal language

Decisions (3)

  • Adopted the agenda for the special meeting
  • Rescinded the previous resolution regarding the fire truck lease financing
  • Adopted a new resolution authorizing lease obligations not to exceed $1,194,118 under ORC 537D for acquisition and financing of fire equipment via master lease agreement with Flagstar, incorporating additional bond language recommended by bond attorney

Action Items (2)

  • Staff to finalize and process all financing documents this week
  • Staff to track down the fiscal officer (Lisa) to get her signature on the documents

Financials

  • Not to exceed $1,194,118 for fire truck lease obligation financing

2025-07-01 Regular Meeting routine
23m ▶ Vimeo

ZC 7-1-25

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held a regular meeting to consider two text amendment cases to the zoning resolution. Case 2025-003 addressed prohibited activities including vegetation height limits, nuisance abatement, and inoperable vehicle storage. Case 2025-004 proposed increasing minimum lot sizes for residential zoning districts (e.g., R1 to five acres) and adjusting side yard setbacks to reduce density in future developments. Both amendments were unanimously recommended for approval to the Board of Trustees.

Text amendments for prohibited activities including vegetation maintenance, hazardous materials storage, and inoperable vehicle restrictions (Case 2025-003)Residential development standards amendments increasing minimum lot sizes to reduce density (Case 2025-004)Zoning map inaccuracies and discrepancies between actual zoning and Clermont County GIS recordsPre-existing nonconforming status of current lots that don't meet new size requirementsDiscussion of sewer and water availability affecting zoning classificationsImpact of new lot size requirements on future lot splits

Decisions (4)

  • Approved meeting minutes from June 24, 2025 (2 yes, 1 abstain)
  • Zoning Case 2025-003: Motion to recommend approval of text amendments on prohibited activities to Board of Trustees passed 3-0
  • Zoning Case 2025-004: Motion to recommend approval of text amendments to residential development standards (lot sizes and setbacks) to Board of Trustees passed 3-0
  • Motion to adjourn passed 3-0

Action Items (3)

  • Staff to research zoning map inaccuracies, particularly regarding properties that may be incorrectly labeled (e.g., R3 vs R1 in areas with septic systems)
  • Staff to investigate prior rezoning cases that were not properly filed under previous administrations and submit corrections to Clermont County GIS
  • Staff to work with trustees on potential correction amendments to zoning maps where discrepancies are found

Financials

  • R1 minimum lot size changed from 40,000/43,560 sq ft to 5 acres
  • R2 minimum lot size currently 30,000 sq ft
  • R3 minimum lot size currently 12,800 sq ft
  • R4 minimum lot size currently 10,000 sq ft
  • R5 minimum lot size currently 18,000 sq ft (believed to be error, intended as 8,000 sq ft)
  • 12-inch height limit for grass/weeds/vegetation before considered a nuisance
  • 10-foot side yard setbacks currently for R1-R5 (R1 setback being removed)

2025-06-24 Special Meeting routine
31m ▶ Vimeo

Special ZC 6-24-25

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held a public hearing on Zoning Case ZC 2025-001, a request to rezone parcel 112208B127 from M1 (light manufacturing) to PDM (planned development mixed use) for a 246-unit residential multifamily development with a clubhouse, amenities, and parking. After staff presented a recommendation for denial due to non-compliance with density and setback requirements, and the applicant acknowledged confusion from a prior meeting, the Commission voted unanimously to deny the rezoning request. The Commission also noted the cancellation of a special workshop and scheduled their next meeting for July 1st regarding tax amendments.

Rezoning request from M1 to PDM for multifamily residential developmentDensity exceeds maximum gross density requirement (8.55 vs 8 units per acre)Front yard setback non-compliance along State Route 28 corridorGoshen Police Department request for developer-funded Flock safety camera systemTraffic impact study required by Clermont County EngineerLoss of M1 industrial-zoned land and impact on township business growthConfusion between zone change approval and preliminary development plan approval from prior meetingODOT traffic report vs full traffic study distinctionArchitectural standards compliance and missing elevation drawingsMissing sidewalk connections in townhome areas

Decisions (3)

  • Approved video meeting minutes from June 3, 2025 (Dinwiddie abstained, all others yes)
  • Denied Zoning Case ZC 2025-001 rezoning from M1 to PDM due to non-compliance with Section 6.02 maximum density of 8 units per acre and Section 6.03 front yard setback requirements (unanimous yes)
  • Approved motion to adjourn at 7:00 PM (unanimous yes)

Action Items (4)

  • Applicant (PLK Communities / Mick Oaks) to address density and setback compliance issues if they wish to resubmit
  • Applicant awaiting ODOT traffic report expected second week of July
  • Director Langenheim to reschedule the canceled special workshop and email commission members availability
  • Staff to coordinate next meeting on July 1st for tax amendment hearings

Financials

  • 246 apartment/townhome units proposed (later revised to 242 by applicant)
  • 28.77 acres total site area
  • 492 parking spaces (2 per unit)
  • 10 acres proposed impervious area
  • 8.55 units per acre proposed vs 8 units per acre maximum allowed
  • 50 foot front yard setback required vs 20 foot setback proposed along Smith Road
  • Flock safety camera installation cost approximately $1,800
  • Flock safety camera annual maintenance cost approximately $3,500
  • 65% total open space proposed
  • 25% minimum open space required
