2023-12-12 Regular Meeting contentious
1h 28m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 12-12-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a combined zoning case hearing and regular meeting. The primary focus was zoning case ZC2023-007, a major modification to an existing Planned Unit Development (PUD) for a 225-home residential subdivision, which was approved with numerous conditions including traffic study, buffering, mounding, and emergency access requirements. The board also addressed financial matters including Ohio EMA reimbursement funds, approved flooring replacement for the administration building, authorized purchase of a police vehicle, administered the oath of office to Trustee Hausermann, and approved a collective bargaining agreement with the firefighters' union.

Zoning case ZC2023-007 - Major modification to Planned Unit Development for 225-home residential subdivisionTraffic safety concerns on State Route 48 and Short 48 from residentsEmergency access road design and proximity to adjacent property (Carl Pringle)Buffer zones, mounding, and screening between PUD and adjacent M1/M2 industrial properties (Carl Nause)Ohio EMA disaster reimbursement fundsAdministration building lower level flooring replacement with polished concrete by Monster CoatPolice vehicle purchaseTrustee Hausermann oath of office re-electionCollective bargaining agreement with Goshen Professional Firefighters Local 3932 (2024-2026)Fire Station 18 architect RFQ processODOT traffic impact study pending for subdivision entrance on Route 48

Decisions (9)

  • Approved major modification to PUD zoning case ZC2023-007 with conditions including final development plan requirements, tree survey, landscape plan, amenity details, building information, updated traffic impact study, buffer on emergency exit with swale and mound, double gates on emergency drive, mounding along M1/M2 property, and privacy fence in areas where mounding is insufficient
  • Approved increase in revenue of $482,950.52 from Ohio EMA for out-of-pocket expenses and mutual aid partners
  • Approved increase in appropriation of ARPA fund 2272-760-720-1229 in the amount of $13,905 for flooring removal and replacement
  • Approved Monster Coat to remove and replace flooring on lower level of administration building
  • Approved purchase of 2023 Ford Explorer Police Interceptor from Lebanon Ford for $41,680
  • Trustee Hausermann re-elected and administered Oath of Office
  • Approved entering executive session for employee discipline and bargaining session matters per ORC 121.22
  • Approved collective bargaining agreement between Goshen Township and Goshen Professional Firefighters Local 3932, effective January 1, 2024 through December 31, 2026
  • Meeting adjourned at 7:52 PM

Action Items (11)

  • Developer (Robert Kerwin/Coach Development) to provide final development plan meeting Article 6 Section 602 requirements
  • Developer to work with certified urban landscape architect and/or foresters to prepare tree survey for the PUD site
  • Developer to provide landscape plan addressing screening between adjacent properties
  • Developer to update traffic impact study with ODOT (expected early February 2024)
  • Developer to implement mounding system along north property line (77 feet to 44 feet buffer areas) adjacent to M1/M2 properties
  • Developer to install double gates on emergency access drive
  • Developer to install privacy fence with pine trees where mounding is insufficient near M1/M2 property
  • Developer to address swale and buffer on south side (Pringle side) of emergency exit
  • Jen Farmer to continue pursuing additional Ohio EMA reimbursement checks through 2024
  • Fire department RFQ responses for Station 18 due December 19 from architects
  • Zoning staff to send updated notification letters to adjacent property owners for future meetings

Financials

  • $219,000 - Township portion of Ohio EMA reimbursement
  • $13,905 - ARPA appropriation for administration building flooring removal and replacement
  • $41,680 - 2023 Ford Explorer Police Interceptor from Lebanon Ford
  • 1,750 square feet - area of flooring to be replaced in administration building lower level
  • 225 homes - proposed residential development in PUD

2023-12-06 Regular Meeting contentious
1h 48m ▶ Vimeo

ZC 12-6-23

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held a public hearing on two zoning cases: ZC-2023-007 (major modification to the Woodhull Crossing PUD, formerly the Reserves at Barclay Woods) and ZC-2023-008 (Cedar Grove Estates final development plan). The commission heard staff presentation and developer testimony for the Woodhull Crossing plan proposing 225 single-family homes with updated lot dimensions, improved traffic circulation, reduced cul-de-sacs, and added amenities. Neighboring property owners raised significant concerns about buffering from adjacent industrial uses, drainage issues, traffic safety at the State Route 48 entrance, and the change from a full entrance to an emergency-only access on Charles Snyder Road. The commission voted to approve the Woodhull Crossing modification with staff recommendations and a traffic study condition.

Woodhull Crossing PUD major modification (formerly Reserves at Barclay Woods Phase 2)Lot dimension changes to accommodate ranch home constructionBuffering and screening between residential PUD and adjacent industrial propertiesTraffic safety concerns at State Route 48 entranceChange from full entrance to emergency-only access on Charles Snyder RoadStormwater drainage management and impact on adjacent propertiesCommunity amenities: pickleball courts, dog park, playground, picnic area, walking trailsIndustrial noise, lighting, and odor impacts from adjacent tank truck building operationCedar Grove Estates final development plan (second case mentioned but not detailed in transcript)

Decisions (1)

  • Motion to approve ZC-2023-007 (Woodhull Crossing PUD major modification) with staff recommendations and requirement for an updated traffic study - passed

Action Items (8)

  • Developer (Robert Gerwin/Coach Development) to provide updated traffic study for State Route 48 entrance
  • Developer to work with certified arborist/landscape architect to prepare tree retention and landscape plan
  • Developer to provide landscape plan addressing screening/buffering between PUD and adjacent industrial properties including mounding and pine trees
  • Developer to provide site plan details for proposed amenities (pickleball courts, dog park, playground, picnic area, walking trails)
  • Developer to provide building elevations, construction materials, and floor plans as part of final development plan
  • Developer to address 20-foot buffer encroachment on three lots near primary access road
  • Developer to address stormwater drainage concerns raised by adjacent property owners (Cornwell property pipe/drainage issue)
  • Staff to ensure notification letters are sent with adequate lead time and clearer language (per Carl's feedback about acronyms)

Financials

  • 225 single-family detached homes proposed
  • 2.86 units per acre density (maximum allowed is 3 units per acre)
  • 30.7% open space (up from 23% in previous plan, but 4.3% below minimum requirement)
  • Average lot size of 7,625 square feet (minimum required is 5,000 sq ft)
  • Lot dimensions changed from 70x116 feet to 61x125 feet
  • Minimum 1,400 square feet floor area required per home
  • 20-foot buffer required along PUD perimeter
  • 50% maximum lot coverage standard
  • Ranch homes typically 1,600 to 2,000 square feet
  • Cul-de-sacs reduced from 8 to 3

2023-12-05 Regular Meeting routine
20m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 12-5-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting on December 5, 2023 at 6 PM. Major items included approving a switch in healthcare providers from Medical Mutual to Anthem (with a 6% increase vs. the 10% renewal offered by Medical Mutual), approving payment for an indigent burial, declaring surplus firearms for sale, appointing a new police officer, approving a disciplinary agreement with the police association, renewing the Police Chief/Township Administrator employment agreement, and approving 2024 non-union wages.

2024 healthcare benefits renewal and provider switch from Medical Mutual to AnthemIndigent burial responsibility and cemetery fund appropriationsSurplus and sale of five forfeited firearmsTree lighting community event recap benefiting CP AAA and police departmentLocal Government Fund and property tax reevaluation legislation support lettersAppointment of new full-time police officer Maggie SykesDisciplinary agreement with Goshen Township Police AssociationEmployment agreement renewal for Bob Rose as Police Chief and Township Administrator for 20242024 non-union wages approvalTornado flag recovered from Mark Park displayed in hallway

Decisions (11)

  • Adopted the meeting agenda
  • Approved payment of bills for November 28 through December 5, 2023
  • Approved switching medical insurance to Anthem, dental to Anthem, and accident/critical illness to Standard for 2024
  • Approved increase in cemetery fund appropriations of $1,806 from cemetery fund (not ARPA) for payment to Advantage Cremation Care of Greater Cincinnati for indigent burial
  • Approved declaring five forfeited firearms as surplus and selling them on GovDeals to an FFL dealer
  • Adjourned into Executive Session
  • Appointed Maggie Sykes as full-time police officer contingent upon successful pre-employment screening
  • Approved disciplinary agreement with Goshen Township Police Association
  • Approved employment agreement with Bob Rose to continue as Police Chief and Township Administrator in 2024
  • Approved 2024 non-union wages
  • Adjourned the regular meeting at 7:10 PM

Action Items (4)

  • Chief Rose / HR to implement healthcare provider switch to Anthem for medical and dental, and Standard for accident/critical illness for 2024
  • Chief Rose to list five forfeited firearms on GovDeals for sale to FFL dealer
  • Complete pre-employment screening process for Maggie Sykes for full-time police officer appointment
  • Fiscal Officer to process $1,806 payment to Advantage Cremation Care from cemetery fund appropriations

Financials

  • $1,806 for indigent burial payment to Advantage Cremation Care of Greater Cincinnati
  • Medical Mutual offered 10% increase on healthcare renewal
  • Anthem proposed at approximately 6% increase over current healthcare costs (5.2% for base plan)
  • Approximately $15,000 anticipated unencumbered balance in cemetery fund at year-end

BOT 11-28-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting on November 28, 2023. Key actions included approving payment of bills, adopting updates to the policies and procedure manual (including sick leave donation and medical opt-out policies), approving the transfer of $1 million into a savings account for interest earnings, approving a job description for a new position, appointing Steve Pegram as Chief Emeritus volunteer, and passing a resolution to proceed with a 4.5 mil replacement EMS tax levy. The board also entered executive session regarding personnel matters and heard public comment on a property/development concern and questions about the levy.

Public comment on property development and fire lane concerns near Charles Snyder RoadReplacement EMS levy questions and resolution (4.5 mil)Policy and procedure manual updates (motor vehicle compliance, medical opt-out, sick leave donation)Transfer of $1 million to savings account for interest earningsAppointment of Steve Pegram as Chief Emeritus volunteerNew job description approvalExecutive session for personnel mattersLight Up Goshen fundraiser announcement (CPAAA)Moment of remembrance for Fayette County fire chief and captain (two line-of-duty deaths)

Decisions (9)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda – passed
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (Nov 14 and Nov 28, 2023) – passed
  • Motion to adopt updates to policies and procedure manual (5 policy revisions including motor vehicle compliance, medical opt-out compensation, sick leave donation policy) – passed
  • Motion to transfer $1 million into savings account – passed
  • Motion to approve resolution R023-2023 to proceed with 4.5 mil replacement EMS tax levy – passed
  • Motion to approve job description for new position – passed
  • Motion to approve appointment of Steve Pegram as Chief Emeritus volunteer effective 11/29/23 – passed
  • Motion to adjourn into executive session at 6:35 PM per ORC 121.22 – passed
  • Motion to adjourn regular meeting at 7:55 PM – passed

Action Items (5)

  • Mr. Rose to contact Don Cornwell (property owner at 6444 Charles Snyder Rd) to set up a meeting with the zoning inspector and fire chief regarding development/fire lane concerns
  • Fiscal officer to execute transfer of $1 million to savings account
  • Fiscal officer to report back after March bank reconciliation on potential adjustment to savings account amount
  • Prosecutor's office to finalize EMS levy documentation for Board signature
  • Michelle Dinwiddie / CPAAA Light Up Goshen tree fundraiser – last day to sign up is November 29

Financials

  • $1 million to be transferred into township savings account
  • 4.5 mil replacement EMS tax levy (fire and emergency medical services)
  • $100 per tree for Light Up Goshen fundraiser
  • Approximately $150 more per year estimated increase for replacement levy
  • Lowest general fund balances by year: 2019 - $2.4M, 2020 - $2.3M, 2021 - $2.5M, 2022 - $3.2M, 2023 - $3.9M
  • $723,000 highest monthly bill amount referenced
  • $1.4 million minimum balance referenced after savings transfer

2023-11-14 Regular Meeting ceremonial
1h 40m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 11-14-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting featuring ceremonial swearing-in of three new police officers and promotion of a detective to sergeant, as well as life-saving awards for staff involved in two cardiac arrest incidents. The board approved the 2024 budget, a cemetery mowing contract, a used V-box purchase for snow removal, initiated the process for a criteria architect for the demolition and reconstruction of Fire Station 18, passed a resolution of necessity to replace an expiring five-year Fire/EMS operating levy at the same millage, and processed grant-related financial motions for the Brownfield Grant, ARPA Retention Grant, and Tech Cred Drone Training Grant.

Swearing-in of three new police officers (Lunsford, Sickles, Pangallo)Promotion of Chris McMillan to police sergeantLife-saving awards for Chad Meadows (youth football cardiac arrest) and Sergeant Skates/medics (Brian Hammonds cardiac arrest)Fire Station 18 demolition and new construction process initiationFive-year Fire/EMS operating levy replacement (Resolution of Necessity R0222023)2024 budget presentation and approval across all departmentsCemetery mowing contract awarded to Mullins MaintenanceBrownfield Grant payments for Combs Dumps environmental assessmentARPA first responder retention grant distributionTech Cred drone training grantWellness committee formation discussionAlternative road paving methods (cape seal vs mill and fill)ARPA fund spending/encumbrance deadlinesLighting district audit planned for 2024Interest savings account implementation

Decisions (16)

  • Adopted the meeting agenda
  • Approved payment of bills for October 24 through November 13, 2023
  • Approved agreement with Mullins Maintenance for 2024 cemetery mowing
  • Approved purchase of used Buyer's two-ton stainless V-box from Walker Landscape not to exceed $6,000
  • Approved refund of $216 to Christy Hodge for duplicate cemetery foundation payment
  • Approved motion to solicit statement of qualifications for criteria architect/engineer for demolition of Station 18 and construction of new fire station
  • Approved increase of revenue of $267,056.85 from Brownfield Grant
  • Approved increase of appropriations of $267,056.85 for Brownfield Grant
  • Approved payment of $267,056.85 to Patriot Engineering from Brownfield Grant funds
  • Approved increase of revenue of $176,671.38 from ARPA Retention Grant
  • Approved increase of appropriations of $176,671.38 from ARPA Retention Grant
  • Approved increase of appropriations of $7,800 from Tech Cred Drone Training Grant
  • Approved Resolution of Necessity R0222023 to replace expiring five-year Fire/EMS operating levy at same millage
  • Approved 2024 budget
  • Approved motion for executive session under ORC 121.22 G1 and G4
  • Approved motion to adjourn at 8:48 PM

Action Items (9)

  • Chief Myers to bring motion to proceed on levy replacement at next meeting and submit documents to Board of Elections before December 20
  • Administrator Rhoads to complete revenue true-up report in December and present to board
  • Administrator Rhoads to audit lighting district in 2024 to address expenses exceeding revenue
  • Administrator Rhoads to come back with recommendations on encumbering ARPA funds before end of 2024
  • Director Meadows to attend Southwest Public Works Association meeting and follow up on cape seal/alternative paving options for 2025
  • HR Manager Eichenhorst to finalize healthcare renewal after meeting the following day
  • Jen Foster to establish wellness committee with representatives from each department meeting monthly
  • Laura Englund and Jen to implement interest savings account by January
  • Administrator Rhoads to earmark $75,000 for road paving in 2025 and seek grant match for $300,000 total

2023-10-24 Regular Meeting routine
1h 18m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 10-24-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting covering the 2024 budget discussion across all departments including general fund, police, fire/EMS, and service. Key topics included tornado expense appropriations, cemetery mowing rebidding after the current contractor closed, disposal of obsolete police intoxilizers, personnel changes including a resignation and a sergeant promotion, and detailed budget presentations with discussions on vehicle purchases, road paving strategy, ARPA fund allocations, and healthcare cost increases. Judge Anita Beckham also introduced herself to the board as an incumbent municipal court judge running for election.

2024 budget discussion across all departments (general, police, fire/EMS, service, ARPA, zoning, cemetery, lighting district)Tornado damage expenses and EMA reimbursement processCemetery mowing contract rebid after Josh Walker closing his businessHR position funding across departments (general fund vs. departmental splits)Police vehicle procurement challenges (Ford strike, potential switch to Dodge Durango or Tahoe)Road paving strategy - skip year to accumulate funds for larger grant-matched projectARPA fund allocation for basement floors, cleanup day, parking lot repairs, IT upgradesFire apparatus cost escalation (engines and ambulances)Body camera and taser five-year replacement cycleLighting district electricity cost increase and assessment needPromotion of Chris McMillan to police sergeantSeparation agreement with Travis AllenPersonnel resignations (Justin Porterfield - police, Aaron Port - fire/EMT)Judge Anita Beckham community introductionHealthcare insurance renewal shopping (initial 11% increase)Paint-the-plow school project and upcoming community events (Trick or Treat, Holly Fair, parade)Sergeant Jeremy Skates life-saving award using AEDDisposal of obsolete Intoxilyzer 5000 unitsContract negotiations with fire department unionOpioid settlement fund management

Decisions (13)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda - passed
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (Oct 11-23, 2023) - passed
  • Motion to increase appropriations for tornado expenses by $75,000 (fund 1000-110-590-0706) - passed
  • Motion to increase appropriation for lighting district electricity by $7,000 (fund 2401310351000) - passed
  • Motion to authorize township administrator to sign USDA rural development certificate of support for Goshen Schools - passed
  • Motion to declare surplus and dispose of two obsolete Intoxilyzer 5000 units from police department - passed
  • Motion to accept resignation of Justin Porterfield effective 10-11-23 - passed
  • Motion to accept resignation of part-time firefighter/EMT Aaron Port effective 10-22-23 - passed
  • Motion to approve out-of-town travel for Officer Tyler Smith for traffic crash reconstruction training (Jan 22 - Feb 2, 2024) - passed
  • Motion to adjourn into executive session at 7:16 PM - passed
  • Motion to promote Chris McMillan to police sergeant effective November 5, 2023 - passed
  • Motion to approve separation agreement with Travis Allen and authorize administrator to sign - passed
  • Motion to adjourn at 7:45 PM - passed

Action Items (12)

  • Director Meadows to bring cemetery mowing bid recommendations by November 14th meeting (bids due Nov 14 at noon)
  • Administrator Rose to reach out to Chad (Duke Energy liaison) about lighting district electricity cost increases
  • Staff to work with auditor's office on lighting district assessment for 2024 to ensure collections are accurate
  • Administrator Rose to research police vehicle leasing options vs. purchasing
  • Administrator Rose to clarify best practice with the state regarding carrying over purchase orders vs. closing them
  • Chief Myers to pursue grant writing for next fire engine replacement
  • Director Meadows to research tar and chip road treatment costs as alternative to full paving
  • Melinda (HR) to shop healthcare insurance renewal to market (initial renewal came in at ~11%)
  • Administrator Rose to follow up with Tyler on finance committee update by end of week and email trustees
  • Laura/Jen to finalize the new interest-earning investment approach (Star Ohio or similar)
  • Jim to continue working on EMA reimbursement for tornado expenses (seeking 75% back)
  • Chief Myers to get quotes and plan for ordering at least one new ambulance in 2024

Financials

  • $7,000 increase for lighting district electricity (budget was $55,000)
  • $132,000 expected EMA reimbursement for tornado (75% of costs)
  • $30,000 estimated interest revenue from new investment approach
  • $55,000 transfer from general fund to zoning (less than historical)
  • $239,000 remaining ARPA funds (must spend by end of 2026)

2023-10-16 Other contentious
50m ▶ Vimeo

BZA 10-16-23

The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals held a public hearing on BZA Case 2023-011, a variance request by Michael Shepard to allow a detached accessory garage built zero feet from the public right-of-way at his property on Clairville Drive. Township zoning staff recommended denial because the structure was built without a permit, extends into the public right-of-way, and the applicant had not met the conditions of a prior 2021 variance resolution including lot consolidation. The board unanimously denied the variance and advised the applicant to reapply for the original variance process, consolidate his lots, and move the structure to comply with required setbacks.

Variance request for detached accessory garage built zero feet from public right-of-way (BZA 2023-011)Structure built without zoning permit or building permitFailure to meet conditions of prior 2021 variance resolution (R-20-2021)Lot consolidation requirement not completed from prior resolutionPrior resolution deemed expired/null and void due to unmet conditionsProperty maintenance complaint that initiated the current proceedingsApplicant's claim that paperwork was lost by previous zoning staffStructure potentially extending beyond property line into public right-of-way

Decisions (3)

  • Approved meeting minutes from September 18, 2023
  • Denied variance request BZA 2023-011 for zero-foot setback from public right-of-way for detached accessory garage (unanimous)
  • Motion to adjourn approved

Action Items (4)

  • Michael Shepard to resubmit variance application including the new garage structure and lot consolidation through the zoning process
  • Michael Shepard to move the accessory garage structure to comply with R1 district setback requirements (10 feet from principal structure and behind the front facade setback line)
  • Michael Shepard to complete lot consolidation through township and county processes
  • Zoning staff to assist Michael Shepard through the resubmission and variance process

Financials

  • $1,600 paid by applicant for original permit applications and fees in 2021
  • 50-foot minimum setback from street right-of-way required in R1 district
  • 48-foot front yard setback previously approved for the home along State Route 48
  • 15-foot side yard setback previously approved via 2021 variance
  • 10-foot minimum distance required between accessory structure and principal building
  • 7 feet current distance between garage and principal structure (3 feet less than required)
  • 9 feet from physical road to structure per applicant's site plan

2023-10-10 Regular Meeting routine

BOT 10-10-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting on October 10, 2023. Key discussions included a CPAAA memorial Christmas tree fundraiser proposal that needs legal review, a zoning text amendment for temporary dwelling units during new home construction that was voted down for rewriting to also address emergency/renovation situations, and planning for the 2024 OTA winter conference. The board also went into executive session regarding a personnel matter and adjourned with no decisions made from that session.

  • Motion to approve three days unpaid leave for Inspector Hundley (12/26–12/28/2023) – passed

  • Motion to adjourn into executive session under ORC 121.22(G)(1) at 6:34 PM – passed
  • Motion to adjourn regular meeting at 6:52 PM – passed

  • Liz/Zoning to rewrite zoning text amendment to add a separate provision (item 8) for temporary dwelling units during major renovation or emergency situations, and bring revised version to next meeting

17m ▶ Vimeo

ZC 10-3-2023

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held a public hearing on Zoning Case DZC2023-006, a request for a minor modification to the Lakefield Place Section Two PUD. The modification would change condominiums from a single parcel into individual parcels. Staff recommended approval, and the Zoning Commission unanimously approved the minor modification.

Minor modification to Lakefield Place Section Two PUD to split condominium units from a single parcel into individual parcelsCompliance with Article 6.02 development standardsStaff finding that code provisions were geared toward single-family homes and condominiums were previously approved

  • Approved minutes of a prior meeting (with some abstentions/absences noted)
  • Approved minor modification to Lakefield Place Section Two PUD (Zoning Case 2023-006) — unanimous yes from Currie, Dinwiddie, Frisbee, Lewis, Otke, and Pegram

2023-09-26 Regular Meeting contentious
1h 36m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 9-26-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting covering a wide range of topics including zoning text amendments, police department reaccreditation, property insurance renewal, and the future of tornado-damaged Station 18 firehouse. The board voted unanimously to demolish Station 18 and build a new firehouse, citing the minimal cost difference between renovation and new construction and the importance of firefighter health and safety. The board also approved insurance renewal, records retention updates, personnel changes, and discussed the township gateway sign relocation.

Zoning text amendment discussions: temporary camper living, solar/wind farms, farm animals on small lots, tiny homes, accessory dwelling units, northeast quadrant 2-acre lotsPolice department CALEA reaccreditation (one of only 7 small agencies in Ohio)Station 18 firehouse demolition and new construction decision following tornado damageProperty insurance renewal with Ohio Plan/HighlandSidewalk safety concerns along Goshen Road and coordination with county engineer/ODOTNew digital gateway sign relocation and design modificationsRecords retention and basement records disposal with Historical Society involvementSubdivision development concerns from residents regarding growth and school capacityHomerama coming to Goshen Township on Klosterman Farm (Smith Road)Township trunk-or-treat event planning for October 31stPersonnel actions: resignation, promotion, and new HR manager hireDetectives recognized by Cincinnati Crimestoppers for crime-solving work

Decisions (14)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda – passed unanimously
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (9/13/23 through 9/26/23) – passed unanimously
  • Motion to renew property insurance with Highland and Ohio Plan at cost not to exceed $122,185 – passed unanimously
  • Motion to immediately demolish Station 18 and begin path to build a new firehouse – passed unanimously
  • Motion to accept donation of eight hand vacuums from Bryan Equipment – passed (Allen yes, Hodges abstain, Hausermann yes)
  • Motion to dispose of records in accordance with existing RC2 with Historical Society given opportunity to retain items – passed unanimously
  • Motion to approve revised RC2 records retention schedule – passed unanimously
  • Motion to accept resignation of service worker Dale Garrett effective 9/27/23 – passed unanimously
  • Motion to accept Ohio Ambulance Transportation Program ARPA grant of $20,227.76 – passed unanimously
  • Motion to approve out-of-town travel for Jen Farmer to O2X workshop October 18-19 at no cost – passed unanimously
  • Motion to enter executive session at 7:34 PM – passed unanimously
  • Motion to promote Charlie Thomas from part-time to full-time service worker effective 10/2/23 at $21.15 with one-year probationary period – passed unanimously
  • Motion to hire Melinda Eichenhorst as HR manager and assistant to the administrator effective 9/27/23 at $62,400 annual salary – passed unanimously
  • Motion to adjourn at 8:58 PM – passed unanimously

  • Zoning consultant to prepare solar/wind energy research and schedule workshop with zoning board, BZA, and trustees (target October)
  • Zoning consultant to forward camper/temporary living language to Administrator Rose for zoning amendment process
  • Zoning consultant to group remaining zoning topics (tiny homes, accessory dwelling units, farm animals on small lots, northeast quadrant 2-acre minimum) for future workshops
  • Laura (project specialist) to confirm gateway sign changes with Justin at One Stop: 45-degree angle, hillside placement, 6-foot height, blue background with white township lettering
  • Administrator Rose to coordinate with new ODOT District 9 contact regarding sidewalk and road concerns on Goshen Road
  • Administrator Rose to work with Carrie on insurance adjustments if/when Station 18 decisions are finalized

  • Don and department heads to plan trunk-or-treat event for October 31st in administration parking lot area

2023-09-19 Special Meeting urgent
46m ▶ Vimeo

BOT Special Meeting 9-19-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting to discuss repair versus replacement options for Station 18 Firehouse, which was damaged by storms and has pre-existing structural deficiencies dating back to its 1982 construction. Chief Myers presented renovation costs of approximately $2.3 million and new construction estimates of approximately $6 million, with potential funding from Ohio EMA (75% of the insurance gap), a state bill (HB 244) for an additional $2.5 million, and BRIC grants. The Board expressed a desire to build a new code-compliant firehouse without raising taxes and decided to continue deliberation at the next regular trustee meeting.

Station 18 Firehouse repair vs. replacement optionsStructural deficiencies of 1982 building including walls not connected to foundationBuilding code compliance for essential/category 4 buildings (250 mph wind zone)Ohio EMA 75% gap funding commitmentHouse Bill 244 for additional $2.5 million state fundingBRIC grant application for resilient infrastructureGoal of zero additional tax burden on residentsPublic comment on urgency and fiscal responsibilitySocial media misinformation about insurance coverage

Decisions (2)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed unanimously
  • Motion to adjourn the meeting passed unanimously

Action Items (5)

  • Board to place Station 18 firehouse discussion on the next regular trustee meeting agenda
  • Staff to seek clarification on whether Ohio EMA 75% funding applies to renovation as well as new construction
  • Staff to pursue BRIC (Building Resilient Infrastructure and Communities) grant application
  • Monitor progress of House Bill 244 introduced by Senator Johnson and Representative Jean Schmidt for additional $2.5 million in funding
  • Chief Myers and staff to continue gathering answers to outstanding questions before next meeting

Financials

  • $2,259,000 - estimated cost to repair storm damage at Station 18
  • $2,315,797 - pre-existing renovation work costs
  • $6,000,000 - estimated cost for new building construction
  • ~$2.2-2.3 million - maximum insurance coverage
  • ~$4 million - estimated funding gap between insurance and new build cost
  • ~$3 million - Ohio EMA 75% coverage of the funding gap
  • $2.5 million - amount in House Bill 244 for additional state funding
  • $22 million - federal funding threshold for uninsured claims (not met)
  • $1 million - amount the township could squeeze into budget for a mortgage if needed
  • ~$1.5 million - potential surplus if HB 244 passes, to cover soft costs

BZA 9-18-2023

The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals held a public hearing on September 18, 2023. The board approved a fence variance (BZA case 2023-010) allowing a 10-foot chain link fence with slats at 6722 State Route 132, which is a B2 commercial property requiring the higher fence per Ohio Board of Pharmacy security requirements for a proposed pharmacy use. The meeting also briefly referenced BZA cases 2022-011, 012, and 013. The next scheduled meeting was set for October 16th.

BZA fence variance request for 10-foot security fence at commercial propertyOhio Board of Pharmacy security requirements for proposed pharmacy useReference to BZA cases 2022-011, 012, and 013

Decisions (3)

  • Approved minutes from July 17th hearing
  • Approved BZA case 2023-010: 10-foot fence variance at 6722 State Route 132 (2-foot variance from 8-foot maximum)
  • Motion to adjourn approved

Action Items (2)

  • Applicant to coordinate with Alyssa (zoning staff) regarding the updated/smaller fenced-in area dimensions
  • Staff to follow up on list of boards and figure out remaining details related to the approved variance

Financials

  • 10-foot fence requested (2-foot variance from 8-foot maximum allowed in B2 commercial district)
  • Fenced area reduced by 10 feet closer to the building from original plan

2023-09-12 Regular Meeting routine
57m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 9-12-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting covering financial matters, personnel changes, and community events. Key actions included approving a new interest-bearing savings account with CF Bank to generate investment income, paying a $50,645.18 IRS tax assessment from a 2020 payroll audit, and creating and filling a new part-time grants coordinator position. The board also approved a Sons of the American Revolution ceremony at Meyer Cemetery and accepted two employee resignations.

CF Bank interest-bearing savings account (IntraFi/ICS) to earn investment income on township fundsIRS tax assessment from 2020 payroll audit regarding Medicare withholding and medical opt-out taxationSons of the American Revolution ceremony at Meyer Cemetery for six Revolutionary War soldiers (Oct 21)Creation of part-time Project Specialist/Grants Coordinator position and appointment of Donna RosickResignation of HR Manager Michaela Young and Firefighter EMT Colin WeissmanIncrease in State of Ohio EMS grantRecognition of Sgt. Beckleheimer and Officer Lunsford for assistance in domestic violence arrestPublic comment regarding anti-vaccine/nanotechnology claimsAnnual tax levy resolution (R-019-2023)Station 18 insurance/architect preparations mentioned

BOT 8-22-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting covering several topics including a presentation by County Engineer Jeremy Evans on a proposed roundabout at the Woodville Pike and Deerfield Road intersection, an update on the township's new digital gateway sign, the purchase of a replacement lawn mower, and an extension of the tornado-related zoning moratorium. The board also entered executive session to discuss personnel matters and approved a suspension of an officer.

Woodville Pike and Deerfield Road roundabout project presentation by County Engineer Jeremy EvansGateway digital sign update and visibility concernsPurchase of replacement lawn mower using ARPA fundsExtension of tornado zoning moratorium allowing temporary RV/camper dwelling through June 2024Vacating portion of Mustang Circle in Villages of BelmontRecognition of Service Director Chad Meadows for saving a 13-year-old football player in cardiac arrestCommendation of Goshen PD officers Alcorn, Groves, and Collier for school community engagementFire station 18 architectural update and Medic 19 ambulance replacement (10-month timeline)Go-to Road bridge reopening expected next dayTrick or treat date set for October 31, 2023Healthcare committee updatePersonnel discipline matter in executive sessionPassing of Pete Schmidt (Rep. Jean Schmidt's husband)

  • Motion to purchase 2023 Gravely lawn mower from Koenig Equipment not to exceed $12,710.06 from ARPA funds – passed
  • Motion to declare surplus/obsolete and sell 2002 X-Mark mower on GovDeals – passed
  • Motion to revise indigent burial of Janice Lay to $853 payable to Advantage Cremation – passed

  • Motion to approve out-of-town travel for Officer Matt Richwine to attend less lethal instructor course (October 18-20, 2023, $895 registration) – passed

  • Motion to suspend Officer Reporter Field for two shifts totaling 24 hours – passed

BOT Meeting 8-8-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting covering various operational updates, personnel matters, and financial items. Key highlights included acceptance of an approximately $196,000 ARPA First Responder Retention Incentive Grant providing $4,000 bonuses to eligible police and fire employees, approval of two indigent burials, hiring of two new full-time police officers, and acceptance of two firefighter/EMT resignations. The board also approved converting a township-owned parking lot to grass and discussed progress on the digital sign, generator repair, tornado siren issues, and Station 18 construction permitting.

Generator restored to admin building after one yearDigital sign installation at Goshen Road and SR 28 and upcoming gateway sign at 28 and DeerfieldTornado siren on Peggy Lane still under repairStation 18 construction permit denial and follow-up with county building department and stateARPA First Responder Retention Incentive Grant acceptance (~$196,000)Medic 19 insurance replacement – 550 chassis found, approximately one year from completionTwo firefighter/EMT resignations due to pay competitiveness issuesHiring of two new full-time police officers (Daniel Pangallo and Megan Sickles)Conversion of township-owned parking lot to grass lotTwo indigent burials and state reimbursement processLocal government fund five-year distribution formula successfully negotiated and approved by county commissioners July 27Property tax re-evaluation advocacy at state levelService department dump truck returned after accident repairAshley Patrick (medic injured in accident) expected back in 3-4 weeksCemetery grave buyback from Newsom family

  • Motion to approve payment of bills (July 25–August 7, 2023) – passed (Hodges abstained)
  • Motion to approve unpaid leave of absence for zoning inspector Nolani Tonganon, August 21–25, 2023 – passed
  • Motion to remove township-owned parking lot at 6765 Goshen Road and convert to grass – passed
  • Motion to approve out-of-town travel for Officer Gary Roush to attend Red Dot site instructor training at OPOTA, Nov 1–3 at $400 registration – passed
  • Motion to accept resignation of firefighter/paramedic John Rose effective August 1, 2023 – passed
  • Motion to accept resignation of firefighter/EMT Lucas Croner effective August 1, 2023 – passed
  • Motion to approve indigent burial of Randy D. Dallas at $803 to Advantage Cremations – passed
  • Motion to approve indigent burial of Janice M. Lay at $803 to Advantage Cremations – passed
  • Motion to buy back two graves from James and Cheryl Newsom at $200 each ($400 total) – passed
  • Motion to accept ARPA First Responder Retention Incentive Grant and authorize Township Administrator to sign documents – passed
  • Motion to adjourn into executive session per ORC 121.22 G1 – passed
  • Motion to hire Daniel Pangallo as full-time police officer contingent upon pre-employment process – passed
  • Motion to hire Megan Sickles as full-time police officer contingent upon pre-employment process – passed
  • Motion to adjourn regular meeting at 7:41 PM – passed

Action Items (7)

  • Director Meadows and Chief Rose to resolve concrete sloping issue at generator site
  • Chief Myers to follow up on tornado siren repair on Peggy Lane and update the board
  • Chief Myers' architect to contact state and county building department regarding Station 18 permit denial questions

  • Trustee Hauserman to attend Clermont County Township Association meeting at Union Township regarding property tax re-evaluation support

  • $803 indigent burial cost for Randy D. Dallas

2023-08-08 Special Meeting routine
7m ▶ Vimeo

BOT Special Meeting 8-8-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting and public hearing on zoning case ZC2023-005, which involved text amendments to the township's zoning resolution. The amendments addressed several items including administrative relief authority for the zoning inspector, barn and storage structure uses, flag lot dimensions, accessory structure standards, and new definitions. The amendments had been recommended for approval by the Clermont County Planning Commission and approved by the Zoning Commission on August 1st. The Board of Trustees unanimously approved the text amendments as presented.

Zoning text amendments to the zoning resolution (ZC2023-005)Authorizing zoning inspector to grant minor administrative relief (Section 2.6a.5)Establishing process for administrative relief (Section 3.8)Adding use for barns and storage structures (Table 5.1, Section 5.4a.2)Updated flag lot dimension requirements (Section 5.5a.2g)Allowing accessory structures on adjacent lots under common ownership (Section 7.1b)Standards for accessory structures in front yard in R1 district (Section 7.1c)New definitions for agricultural building, agricultural, and barn storage structure (Section 16.3)

Decisions (3)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed unanimously (3-0)
  • Motion to approve zoning text amendments ZC2023-005 as presented passed unanimously (3-0)
  • Motion to adjourn passed unanimously (3-0)

Action Items (0)

  • None recorded

Financials

  • None recorded

2023-08-01 Regular Meeting contentious
31m ▶ Vimeo

ZC 8-1-23

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held a public hearing on Zoning Case CC-2023-005, which involved a series of text amendments to the township zoning code. The amendments addressed administrative relief authority for the zoning inspector, barns/storage structures as permitted uses in R1 districts, flag/panhandle lot regulations aligned with Clermont County standards, accessory structures on adjacent lots under common ownership, and new definitions for agricultural terms. Significant discussion arose from a resident concerned about non-conforming status of existing properties in platted subdivisions. The commission voted to approve the proposed text amendments, which will next go before the Board of Trustees on August 8.

Administrative relief authority for zoning inspector and zoning supervisorBarns and storage structures as permitted principal use in R1 districtFlag/panhandle lot regulations aligned with Clermont County standardsAccessory structures on adjacent lots under common ownership (Section 7.1B)Detached accessory structures allowed in front yard on large R1 lots (Section 7.1C)Definitions added for agricultural building, agriculture, banner, and barn/storage structureNon-conforming property concerns from resident in platted subdivisionClermont County Planning Commission unanimously recommended approval

Decisions (2)

  • Approved meeting minutes from the May 2, 2023 meeting
  • Approved proposed zoning text amendments (CC-2023-005) with one abstention (Mr. Curry)

Action Items (2)

  • Text amendments to be presented to the Board of Trustees on August 8, 2023
  • Next Zoning Commission meeting scheduled for Tuesday, September 3, 2023

Financials

  • 25 feet minimum width for flag lot frontage
  • 500 feet maximum length for flag lot access strip
  • 2 acres minimum lot size for barns/storage structures as principal use in R1
  • 75 feet minimum setback from road for barns/storage structures
  • 10% administrative relief threshold regardless of zoning district

27m ▶ Vimeo

BZA Meeting 7-17-2023

The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals held a public hearing on case BZA 2023-009, a variance request for a shed placed approximately five feet from the right-of-way instead of the previously approved 18-foot setback. The discrepancy was attributed to miscommunication between the homeowner and an Amish contractor. After discussion about precedent-setting concerns, the board approved the variance. Staff also announced a full BZA board with alternates filled and the hiring of a second zoning inspector.

Variance request BZA 2023-009 for shed placement closer than previously approvedMiscommunication between homeowner and Amish contractor regarding setback measurementConcerns about setting precedent for future variance violationsEnforcement of BZA decisions when structures are already builtFormal complaint filed by concerned citizen regarding the shed placementAnnouncement of full BZA board with alternates filledHiring of second zoning inspector

Decisions (3)

  • Approved video minutes from previous meeting (Holmes yes, Segal yes, Theodore yes, Pruitt abstained)
  • Approved variance BZA 2023-009 allowing shed to remain approximately five feet from the right-of-way (Holmes yes, Segal abstained, Theodore yes, Pruitt yes)
  • Motion to adjourn approved unanimously

Action Items (2)

  • New zoning inspector to begin employment on July 31st pending completion of pre-employment screening
  • New zoning inspector to begin training and attend future BZA meetings

Financials

  • Original required setback: 50 feet
  • Previously approved variance setback: 18 feet
  • Current actual setback: approximately 5 feet from right-of-way

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting covering bill payments, out-of-town travel approvals for staff training, acceptance of a resignation, hiring of five part-time firefighter/EMTs, and appointment of a part-time service worker. BOT Meeting 6-27-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting on June 27, 2023. Key actions included accepting a firefighter's resignation, approving zoning text amendments (Resolution 15), approving the collective bargaining agreement with the police association for 2024-2026, and appointing Dennis Pruitt as a BZA alternate. A resident spoke about media outreach regarding the governor's unfulfilled promise related to township funding, and the board also addressed sign permit refunds and planned future zoning discussions.

Governor's unfulfilled promise to Goshen Township - resident media outreach on radio (Brian Thomas show/WLW)Zoning text amendments (Resolution 15) - barns/storage structures, 10% administrative reliefFuture zoning priorities: zone change process, additional ag zoning, small solar/wind farms, small livestockCollective bargaining agreement with police association 2024-2026BZA alternate appointment of Dennis PruittLions Club health fair with blood donor and mammogram vansGoshen Community Church sign permit refund disputeResignation of firefighter EMT Elijah Mann to Anderson Township

  • Approved collective bargaining agreement with Goshen Township Police Association for 2024-2026 (CERB case 2023 MED 030215)

  • Adjourned meeting at 9:01 PM

2023-06-27 Special Meeting routine
37m ▶ Vimeo

BOT Special Meeting 6-27-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting for the 2024 tax budget public hearing. Administrator Bob Rose presented a preliminary 30,000-foot overview of the 2024 tax budget across all departments including general fund, zoning, police, service, and fire. Key discussions included transferring HR costs to restricted funds, using ARPA funds to supplement the general fund, proposed police union raises of 18% over three years with elimination of one position, skipping paving in 2024 to save for an OPWC grant match in 2025, and the upcoming need for the fire department to replace its five-year levy.

2024 tax budget public hearing and preliminary budget presentationHR cost allocation across fire, police, and service departmentsARPA fund usage strategy for IT, cleanup day, and building improvementsZoning department revenue increase from new subdivisions and fee schedule adjustmentsZoning consultant model replacing director position to save costsPolice union contract with 18% raises over three years and elimination of one positionOfficer retention challenges due to pay gap with neighboring jurisdictionsBody camera contract renewalPaving strategy - skipping 2024 to save for 2025 OPWC grant matchSalt cost increases from $82 to $109 per tonFire and EMS levy replacement needed as current five-year levy nears endOpioid settlement fund spending optionsCemetery fee adjustments and mausoleum successGeneral fund sustainability concerns - revenue vs expenses

Decisions (4)

  • Adopted the agenda for the special meeting
  • Opened the public hearing on the 2024 tax budget
  • Closed the public hearing on the 2024 tax budget
  • Adjourned the special meeting at 6:37 PM

Action Items (8)

  • Administrator Rose and Fiscal Officer Laura to research whether HR cost transfers from restricted funds (fire, police, service) to general fund can be done directly or must be handled through payroll allocation
  • Administrator Rose to work with Trustee Allen on the proper procedure for transferring restricted fund monies
  • Department heads to prepare detailed budget recommendations for October/November presentation
  • Chad (Service Director) to explore lease options and used vehicles for dump truck acquisition
  • Administrator Rose to apply for body camera grant from the state if available
  • Fire Chief Myers to approach trustees about replacing the five-year fire and EMS levy
  • Staff to conduct cleanup day debriefing and prepare recommended plan for next year
  • Chad and Bob to continue planning for OPWC grant application for 2025 paving

Financials

  • $89,000 expected local government fund revenue for 2024
  • $30,000 HR cost transfer split among fire ($19,800), police ($8,000), and service ($2,000)
  • $45,000 budgeted for general fund contracted services (down from $78,000 in 2023)
  • $66,000 transfer from general fund to zoning (down from $84,000 in 2023)
  • $150,000 conservatively estimated zoning revenue for 2023
  • $20,000 budgeted for basement renovations from ARPA funds
  • $9,500 estimated for cleanup day from ARPA funds
  • $219,000 estimated remaining ARPA funds after proposed spending
  • $14,000-$15,000 in opioid settlement funds
  • $790,000 anticipated safety services levy revenue split 50/50 between police and fire
  • $451,000 police share of safety services levy
  • $85,000 estimated for body camera contract renewal (5-year)
  • 18% raises over three years for police officers per proposed union contract
  • $800,000 spent on paving in current year (historic high for the township)
  • $75,000 set aside for 2025 paving
  • $109/ton salt price (up from $82/ton)
  • $115,000 school resource officer contract
  • $250,000 anticipated fire department ending unencumbered balance
  • $3.1 million estimated overall township ending balance for 2024
  • $8.7 million total estimated revenue, $8.8 million total estimated expenses
  • $35,000 fire and EMS building repair line item
  • $922,000 starting unencumbered for administration/zoning, declining to $879,000
Trustee AllenTrustee HodgesTrustee Hausermann (Halderman)Administrator Bob RoseChad (Service Director)Laura (Fiscal Officer)Michaela Young (HR/Assistant)Dave (public commenter)Fire Chief Myers (absent)
Loading...
2023-06-22 Special Meeting routine
54m ▶ Vimeo

BOT Special Meeting 6-22-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting to discuss potential text amendments to the Goshen Township Zoning Code. Key discussions focused on allowing barns and storage structures as primary structures on R1 lots without a residence, limiting the number of accessory structures and lot coverage percentages on unimproved lots, clarifying rules for accessory structures on adjoining lots under common ownership, and updating the agricultural definition to match state language. The board reached consensus on several zoning text amendment provisions and plans to forward the finalized language for formal resolution and zoning commission review.

Allowing barns and storage structures as primary/principal use on R1 lots without a residenceNumber of accessory structures permitted on lots without a principal residenceLot coverage percentage limits for unimproved lots vs. lots with residencesAccessory structures on adjoining lots under common ownership (Section 701B3)Zoning Inspector authority for administrative relief (Section 206)Agricultural definition update to match Ohio state definitionSetback considerations for future house construction when barn is primary structureProcess for text amendments through Zoning Commission and Planning CommissionPrioritizing next set of zoning code amendments including fences and corner lots

Decisions (8)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed unanimously
  • Consensus reached to allow barns/storage structures as primary use in R1 district on lots 2+ acres with maximum 2 buildings and 10% lot coverage when no residence is present
  • Consensus reached to retain existing lot coverage percentages (no number limit on structures) when a principal residence exists on the property
  • Consensus to remove the second sentence in 701B3 (starting with 'Additionally existing properties may not be subdivided...') and add 'adjoining' language for clarity
  • Consensus to change 'subordinate' to 'incidental' in B2 for consistency with accessory structure definition
  • Consensus on agricultural definition using state language
  • Consensus on Zoning Inspector authority for administrative relief (Section 206)
  • Motion to adjourn at 7:54 PM passed unanimously

Action Items (6)

  • Consultant Liz Fields to finalize zoning text amendment language based on board direction from this meeting
  • Prosecutor Carney to prepare a resolution for adoption and initiation of the text amendments, similar to the previous week's resolution
  • Finalized amendments to be sent to Zoning Commission and Planning Commission for review
  • Staff to present the remaining list of zoning issues to address at the next trustee meeting
  • Board to identify the next three zoning items for future amendment work and forward to consultants
  • Melissa (Zoning Inspector) to identify common variance requests and recurring code issues to help prioritize future amendments

Financials

  • 50% maximum lot coverage currently allowed for accessory structures on lots 2+ acres
  • 10% lot coverage agreed upon for unimproved R1 lots (no residence) with barn/storage as primary structure
  • 15% lot coverage initially discussed but reduced to 10% (~8,000+ sq ft on 2 acres)
  • 75-foot setback required for barns as primary structures from the road
  • 7% maximum size of total lot area for principal barn/storage structure
  • 2 acres minimum lot size required for barn as primary structure in R1
  • 88,000 approximate square feet in 2 acres
  • 10-foot minimum distance between structures mentioned
Trustee AllenTrustee HodgesTrustee HausermannLiz Fields (Consultant)Prosecutor CarneyJulia (referenced, previous meeting explanation)Melissa (Zoning Inspector, referenced)Bob (referenced, previous collaboration)Dan (Trustee Allen, referenced by first name)

2023-06-20 Other routine
19m ▶ Vimeo

BZA 6-20-2023

The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals held a public hearing on case BZA 2023-008, a variance request for a proposed Domino's restaurant at the intersection of State Route 28 and 132. The applicant sought relief from front yard setback requirements (30 feet instead of 50 feet along the north property line) and parking standards (20 spaces instead of the required 23). Staff recommended approval citing the constraints imposed by a 100-year floodplain and creek on the property. The board approved the variances.

Variance request for proposed Domino's restaurant (BZA 2023-008)Front yard setback variance due to 100-year floodplain and creek constraintsParking space reduction variance for carry-out focused restaurant with limited seatingTraffic and driveway access concerns near State Route 132 intersectionDemolition of existing vacant bank buildingRetaining wall proposed on south portion of property for floodplain mitigationWelcome of new board member JohnOpen alternate position on the BZA board

Decisions (3)

  • Approved meeting minutes from previous hearing
  • Granted variance reducing required parking spaces from 23 to 20 for proposed Domino's restaurant (BZA 2023-008)
  • Motion to adjourn approved

Action Items (2)

  • Applicant (Caliber Engineering / Domino's) to address signage package separately under sign ordinance requirements
  • Staff to confirm the second variance (front yard setback) resolution was handled or was not necessary as a separate vote

Financials

  • 50-foot front yard setback required; 30-foot setback proposed (20-foot reduction requested along north property line)
  • 23 parking spaces required; 20 spaces proposed (3-space reduction)
  • 1 parking space per 100 square feet required for restaurants in B2 district
  • 20-foot minimum landscaping buffer required along south and west property lines adjacent to residential districts

2023-06-13 Regular Meeting contentious
1h 9m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 6-13-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting addressing community concerns about the tornado-damaged fire station, zoning enforcement issues, proposed zoning text amendments, solar/wind farm regulations, and multiple fire department personnel actions. A resident urged transparency on fire station rebuilding progress, and the board revealed a state budget line item secured through Senator Terry Johnson and Representative Jean Schmidt to fund the station. The board also discussed but deferred decisions on zoning text amendments and small solar/wind farm regulations, and hired three new firefighter EMTs, accepted a resignation, and promoted a volunteer to part-time.

Fire Station 18 tornado damage rebuilding progress and state budget line item fundingZoning enforcement concerns raised by resident Bob Haas regarding BZA variance violationsProposed zoning text amendments regarding accessory structures, barns, and storage unitsSmall solar farm and wind farm regulation discussion under Section 519.213Fire department personnel hiring and resignations due to full-time opportunities elsewhereEMS Agency of the Year Award for the State of Ohio received by Goshen TownshipMemorial Day celebration and community cleanup day recapPolice department CLIA certification achievementZoning inspector hiring difficultiesZoning commission reappointments

Decisions (13)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda – passed
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (May 23, 2023 through June 12, 2023) – passed
  • Motion to hire Firefighter EMT Brody Green at $15.50/hour with one-year probationary period – passed
  • Motion to hire Firefighter EMT Cody Wilms at $15.50/hour with one-year probationary period – passed
  • Motion to hire Firefighter Paramedic Matthew Leeper at $17.00/hour with one-year probationary period – passed
  • Motion to accept $500 donation from Tom and Carol Queen – passed
  • Motion to accept resignation of part-time Firefighter EMT Nathan Denowden – passed
  • Motion to promote volunteer firefighter Jerry Burns to part-time Firefighter EMT at $15.50/hour with one-year probationary period – passed
  • Motion to accept resignation of Dylan West effective June 26, 2023 – passed
  • Motion to adjourn to executive session at 7:08 PM – passed
  • Motion to reappoint David Dinwiddie to zoning commission with term expiring December 31, 2027 – passed
  • Motion to reappoint David Frisbee Jr. as alternate on zoning commission with term expiring June 13, 2025 – passed
  • Motion to adjourn regular meeting at 8:08 PM – passed

Action Items (6)

  • Administrator Rose to arrange a meeting with trustees, Liz, and the prosecutor before the next meeting to clarify proposed zoning text amendments
  • Administrator Rose to conduct further research on small solar/wind farm regulations and bring findings back to the board at a future meeting
  • Chief Myers to hand-deliver stamped architectural documents to the building department for Station 18 code interpretation
  • Chief Myers to provide regular updates on Station 18 fire station rebuilding progress at meetings
  • Administrator Rose to continue interviewing zoning inspector candidates and bring two candidates to the next trustee meeting for consideration
  • Department heads to hold a debrief on cleanup day and plan for next year including adding a crusher truck

Financials

  • $2.5 million in unencumbered township funds
  • $2.4 million fire station insurance value
  • $727 million Ohio rainy day fund (referenced by resident)
  • $15.50/hour rate for new Firefighter EMTs (Brody Green, Cody Wilms, Jerry Burns)
  • $17.00/hour rate for new Firefighter Paramedic Matthew Leeper
  • $500 donation from Tom and Carol Queen to the fire department
  • $3,000/acre typical solar farm lease rate mentioned
  • State budget line item for Goshen Township Fire Department building (specific amount not disclosed, pending governor's signature by June 30)

BOT 5-23-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting covering zoning enforcement discussions, a land use plan proposal from their planning consultant, solar panel regulation resolutions, local government fund apportionment, and personnel matters including three police officer resignations and one new hire. The board also made appointments to the Zoning Commission and BZA, accepted donations of drones and funds, and approved a fire department radio grant.

Zoning enforcement discussion regarding unpermitted structure at 6353 Belfast Road (Tombs property)Land use plan update discussion with planning consultant Liz FieldsLarge solar facility and wind farm prohibition resolution for Goshen TownshipMid-size solar panel (5-49.5 MW) zoning regulation discussionLocal Government Fund apportionment formula for 2024-2029Three police officer resignations and hiring challenges in law enforcementNew police officer hire (Brandon Lunsford from Batavia PD)Fire department EMS award recognition in ColumbusMemorial Day Parade and Ceremony planningTIRC (Tax Incentive Review Council) appointmentsZoning Commission and BZA membership reorganizationFire department radio grant and Station 18 construction progressDrone donation for multi-department useService department staffing challenges with two employees on medical leaveSRO Memorandum of Understanding renewal with Goshen Local School District

Decisions (23)

  • Adopted the meeting agenda
  • Approved payment of bills from April 11 to May 22, 2023
  • Adopted Resolution 2023-012 requesting Clermont County Commissioners prohibit large solar facilities and large wind farms in Goshen Township
  • Adopted resolution authorizing alternative method of apportionment of the undivided Local Government Fund (LGF) for calendar years 2024-2029
  • Approved support and resources for Memorial Day Parade and Ceremony on 5/29/23
  • Appointed Trustee Allen and Administrator Rose to the TIRC for 2023
  • Accepted $50 donation to police department from Shannon and Esther Wilder
  • Approved 2023-2024 SRO MOU with Goshen Local School District
  • Accepted resignation of Officer Tyler Kramer effective 5/21/23
  • Accepted resignation of Officer Matt Walker effective 5/22/23
  • Accepted resignation of Officer Mike Barron effective 5/26/23
  • Accepted donation of two drones (approx. $7,000 value) from CPAAA
  • Approved buyback of seven cemetery plots from Phyllis Hamblin for $875
  • Approved increase in revenue line item for Marshall Radio Grant of $21,580.98
  • Approved increase in appropriations for Marshall Radio Grant of $21,580.98
  • Appointed Brandon Ottey as member of Zoning Commission (term expiring 12/31/2026)
  • Appointed Derek Holmes as member of Zoning Commission (term expiring 12/31/2026)
  • Appointed Derek Holmes as member of BZA (term expiring 12/31/2027)
  • Appointed Gary Theodore as member of BZA (term expiring 12/31/2024)
  • Appointed John Langenheim as alternate on BZA (term expiring 5/23/2025)
  • Hired Brandon Lunsford as full-time police officer effective 5/30/2023
  • Approved purchase of body armor from Batavia PD for $6,000
  • Declined to pursue litigation against 6353 Belfast Road property owners at this time pending zoning changes review

  • Chad to complete final document for Deerfield and Route 28 sign permit with the state
  • Chief Myers to coordinate drone training for firefighters and police officers
  • Chief Myers to continue following up with architect and construction manager on Station 18 code summary process
  • Fire department representatives to attend EMS award ceremony in Columbus on 5/24/23
  • Submit LGF resolution to auditor's office by August 1st deadline

Financials

  • $875 total for buyback of 7 cemetery plots at $125 each from Phyllis Hamblin
  • $50 donation to police department from Shannon and Esther Wilder
  • $7,000 approximate value of two drones donated by CPAAA
  • $21,580.98 Marshall Radio Grant for fire department radios

2023-05-23 Special Meeting contentious
1h 8m ▶ Vimeo

BOT Special 5-23-23

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting featuring a public hearing on Zoning Case CC-2023-004 and 005. The primary matter was a preliminary development plan for a major modification to the Heritage Woods Planned Unit Development (PUD), proposing to subdivide 38 undeveloped wooded acres into up to 6 single-family lots (with 3 homes currently planned). Residents raised concerns about water drainage, water pressure, traffic, use of HOA resources, and future building standards. The board also initiated text amendments to the zoning resolution covering administrative relief, barns/storage structures as primary uses, flag lot standards, and accessory structure regulations.

Heritage Woods PUD major modification - preliminary development plan for 38-acre subdivision into up to 6 lotsWater drainage and runoff concerns from existing residentsWater pressure concerns in Heritage Woods areaTraffic and road access concerns - single entry/exit through Heritage Woods DriveHOA resource usage by non-HOA members (streetlights, signage, landscaping)Future building standards and permitted uses (animals, structures) on new lotsText amendments to zoning resolution - administrative relief processBarns/storage structures as primary use added to use tableFlag lot dimensional requirements update to match county standardsAccessory structures in front yard in R1 district

Decisions (4)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed unanimously
  • Motion to approve the preliminary development plan and major modification of the Heritage Woods PUD passed unanimously (Hodges-Yes, Allen-Yes, Housen-Yes)
  • Motion to initiate text amendments to the zoning resolution and forward to county passed unanimously (Hodges-Yes, Allen-Yes, Housen-Yes)
  • Motion to adjourn the special meeting at 7:08 PM passed

Action Items (6)

  • Applicant must submit final development plan addressing drainage, water runoff, fire hydrant locations, access road design, shared access easement, and development standards
  • Fire department requested plans for access roads and proposed hydrant locations at final development plan stage
  • Bob Rose (Administrator) to collect contact information from concerned residents and add them to email notification list for future Heritage Woods developments
  • Zoning staff to post Heritage Woods updates on Facebook and township website to notify residents of future meetings
  • Text amendments to be forwarded to Clermont County Planning Commission and then back to Zoning Commission for review
  • Township staff working to pull water flow/line maps for the Heritage Woods area

Financials

  • $11,250 per year - Heritage Woods HOA annual budget paid by 75 homeowners
  • 38 acres - undeveloped land proposed for subdivision
  • Up to 6 single-family lots proposed (minimum 5 acres each)
  • 75 existing homes in Heritage Woods subdivision
  • 3,000-4,000 square feet - anticipated home size from prospective buyer Doug Wilson
  • Minimum 2 acres lot size required for barns/storage structures as primary use
  • 75 feet minimum setback from street for barns/storage structures
  • 25 feet minimum width for flag lot pole portion
  • 500 feet maximum length for flag lot pole portion

BZA 5-15-2023

The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals held a public hearing on Case No. BZA-2023-006, a variance request to expand a nonconforming commercial structure in a residential zoning district by adding 1,792 square feet for cold storage of cars and auto parts restoration. The Board discussed front and rear yard setback reductions, dumpster placement, landscaping requirements, and propane tank relocation. Neighboring property owners spoke in support. The Board unanimously approved the variance with staff-recommended conditions.

Variance request BZA-2023-006 for expansion of nonconforming commercial building in R3 residential districtFront and rear yard setback reductions for building additionDumpster placement and enclosure requirementsPropane tank removal or underground relocationRemoval of mobile storage containersLandscaping and screening requirements for residential neighborsRescheduling June BZA meeting from June 19 to June 20BZA alternate member recruitment

Decisions (5)

  • Motion to amend agenda to add Chairperson's statement and swearing in of witnesses passed unanimously
  • Motion to approve video minutes from February 27 meeting passed unanimously
  • Motion to approve variance BZA-2023-006 (expansion of nonconforming structure, front yard setback reduction from 50 ft to 8.76 ft, rear yard setback reduction from 50 ft to 21.24 ft) with staff conditions passed unanimously
  • Motion to move June BZA meeting from June 19 to June 20, 2023 at 6:30 PM passed unanimously
  • Motion to adjourn passed unanimously

Action Items (6)

  • Applicant (Luke Perry) to submit a landscape plan to township planning staff for review and approval, including screening/landscaping at rear of building addition
  • Applicant to submit dumpster enclosure plan for staff-level review and approval, likely positioned at rear corner of new addition
  • Applicant to remove or relocate propane tanks underground
  • Applicant to remove the two mobile storage containers from the property
  • Applicant to confirm dumpster enclosure materials are consistent with building materials and meet code requirements
  • Staff (Liz) to interview an alternate BZA member at the next trustee meeting on May 23

Financials

  • 1,792 square feet proposed building addition
  • Front yard setback reduction from 50 feet to 8.76 feet (lowest lane)
  • Rear yard setback reduction from 50 feet to 21.24 feet
  • 5 foot accessory structure setback from property line for structures under 200 sq ft
  • 20 foot side yard setback requirement in R3 district

2023-05-02 Regular Meeting contentious
1h 39m ▶ Vimeo

ZC 5-2-23

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held a public hearing on two cases: ZC 2023-004, a request for preliminary development plan approval for a major modification to the Heritage Woods PUD to allow up to six single-family residential lots on an undeveloped 38-acre parcel, and ZC 2023-005, proposed text amendments to the township zoning resolution. Multiple Heritage Woods residents expressed concerns about water pressure, traffic, drainage, construction impacts, lack of HOA control over new lots, and potential agricultural/animal uses. Both cases were approved with staff recommendations forwarded to the Board of Trustees.

Heritage Woods PUD major modification - preliminary development plan for 6 lots on 38 undeveloped acresResident concerns about water pressure in Heritage Woods dead-end street systemTraffic and construction concerns from Heritage Woods residentsHOA governance and control over new lots not part of existing HOAPotential agricultural uses and animal keeping on new lotsGreen belt/buffer zone disputes between existing residents and new property boundariesText amendments: administrative relief authority for zoning inspectorText amendments: barns/storage structures as principal use in R1 without a houseText amendments: removal of 2,000 sq ft maximum for accessory structuresText amendments: accessory structures in front yard for R1 lots over 2 acresText amendments: updated flag lot dimensional requirementsDiscussion of future comprehensive land use plan update and potential new zoning districtsWestern Water vs. public water utility distinction and fire protection implicationsStalled Domino's zoning case - applicant possibly looking at another location

Decisions (3)

  • ZC 2023-004: Motion to approve staff recommendation for preliminary development plan for major modification to Heritage Woods PUD (up to 6 single-family lots on 38 acres) - Passed unanimously
  • ZC 2023-005: Motion to approve proposed text amendments to township zoning resolution as recommended by staff - Passed unanimously
  • Approval of April 4th meeting minutes - Passed (with two abstentions from Settle and Pegram)

Action Items (7)

  • Staff to research prior zoning code amendments from approximately 2020-2021 regarding accessory structure height restrictions to determine if changes were already made and need to be removed from current proposal
  • Staff to verify corner lot provisions and accessory structure percentage changes previously approved
  • Text amendments to be forwarded to Board of Trustees for May 23rd meeting
  • Heritage Woods HOA president David Williams invited to submit written comments/recommendations regarding desired PUD regulations for new lots to planning and zoning department
  • Applicant Rick Stumpf to prepare final development plan for Heritage Woods PUD modification including shared access drive details, setback requirements, and development standards
  • Staff to coordinate with public works department regarding water pressure concerns from Heritage Woods residents
  • Staff to look into future land use map update and comprehensive review of R1/R2/R3 lot size standards

  • Up to 6 single-family lots proposed (likely 3 lots of 10+ acres each)

  • 75 existing homes in Heritage Woods subdivision
  • 104 acres originally purchased by developer Mike West
  • 42 lots originally planned for phase three by Mike West
