2022-09-13 Regular Meeting routine
1h 14m ▶ Vimeo

BOT Mtg 9.13.22.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting focused heavily on tornado recovery efforts from the July 6th storm, accepting multiple grants and donations, and approving various departmental purchases. Key discussions included updates on the damaged Station 18 fire building (estimated 2-3 year rebuild timeline), ARP fund allocations totaling approximately $1.4 million, approval of LED monument signs, a new police K-9, and the creation of a new zoning inspector position. Duke Energy presented a $10,000 check to help replace items lost in the tornado.

Ongoing tornado recovery from July 6th stormStation 18 fire station damage assessment and rebuild/repair timeline (estimated 2-3 years)Duke Energy donation presentation and community partnership recognitionARP fund update and allocation summaryNew police K-9 Kirker accepted and K-9-6 retiredLED monument signs for Station 19 and township campus - design and placement discussionNew zoning inspector/plan reviewer position creationPolice cruiser early purchase due to 6-18 month vehicle wait timesDenial of Cove of Barclay Woods PUD adjustment for undersized dwellingLong Term Recovery Committee supporting tornado-affected residentsTemporary fire station setup at public works garageCell tower antenna installation and generator for emergency communicationsCPAAA fundraiser at Texas Roadhouse and Euchre tournament announcementsRecycling bins restored at townshipCode enforcement on Maple Drive easement propertiesJennifer Farmer resignation as part-time administrative assistantEnterprise zone agreement amendment for Brian EquipmentRoad paving fund reallocation from road and bridge to gas taxExecutive session on employee compensation (no decisions made)Insurance process challenges for Station 18 rebuildVan Lanningham property purchase completed; second property offer declined

2022-08-15 Regular Meeting routine
1h 0m ▶ Vimeo

BZA 8.15.22.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals held a regular session addressing old and new business. Old business included rescinding a prior resolution prepared in error, granting continuances for Jeffrey Kelly cases, and approving resolutions for Don Cornwell's patio and deck variances contingent on lot consolidation. New business included approving a setback variance for an unpermitted pavilion at 6334 Liberty Lane, a use variance for temporary RV living at 2439 Woodville Pike during home construction, and a lot coverage variance for a shed at 6005 Mark Circle.

Rescinding erroneously prepared resolution R-032-22Continuances for Jeffrey Kelly cases to October 17Don Cornwell lot consolidation and variance approvals for patio and deck at 6607 State Route 48Variance for unpermitted pavilion at 6334 Liberty Lane (8-inch setback deficiency)Privacy breach - applicant's bank account information accidentally posted on township websiteUse variance for temporary RV living during home construction at 2439 Woodville PikeLot coverage variance for shed at 6005 Mark CircleText amendment in progress to allow temporary RV use with building permitsNew 310-home subdivision development discussion with connectivity to schools and parksAlternate board member vacancy still open

  • Approved Resolution for Don Cornwell variance (deck) at 6607 State Route 48 with lot consolidation condition
  • Approved BZA 2022-014 area variance for accessory structure at 6334 Liberty Lane

2022-08-02 Special Meeting urgent
2h 11m ▶ Vimeo

Spc. BOT Mtg 8.2.22.mp4

Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting focused on tornado recovery updates and a public hearing for zoning case EC-2022-005 (Pebble Groves development). Staff provided extensive updates on disaster relief efforts, unmet needs coordination, insurance claims, and mutual aid from 31 municipalities for debris cleanup. The zoning hearing addressed Grand Communities' proposal for a 113-acre, 310-home planned development residential (PDR) community on Linton Road, with public testimony and board deliberation on conditions including lot sizes, setbacks, traffic studies, and architectural standards.

Tornado recovery and disaster relief coordination including Long-Term Recovery Committee transitionState and federal disaster funding thresholds and application processesManufactured/mobile home inspection challenges post-tornadoMutual aid from 31 municipalities for debris cleanupInsurance claims for township building and vehicle damageZoning case EC-2022-005: Pebble Groves PDR development on Linton Road (113 acres, 310 homes)Traffic impact study for proposed developmentConnectivity between proposed development, school district, and county parkFire Station 18 structural assessment with forensic engineerMotorola tower and communications infrastructure damageNational Night Out event August 3School resource officer community engagementRecycling dumpster relocation after tornado damageLocal government fund passage by Clermont County Commissioners

Decisions (2)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed 3-0
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (June 29 through August 2, 2022) passed 3-0

Action Items (9)

  • Steve Pegram to continue pursuing the 25 uninsured destroyed properties threshold to unlock state funding programs
  • Harry Holbert to find a third-party engineer/inspector for manufactured home inspections, funded through EMA
  • Harry Holbert to continue working with state and county prosecutor on Combs and Clock property cleanup
  • Chad Meadows to coordinate with EMA on debris disposal after August 5 mutual aid conclusion
  • Cheryl Hall to continue pursuing insurance claims for township property damage
  • Sam Houserman, Harry, and Steve to bring proposals to the board on allowing RVs during new house construction and accessory structure rule changes
  • Traffic engineer to potentially study Linton Road to Manila Road corridor per Trustee Allen's concern
  • Grand Communities to provide streetlight plan, dwelling unit calculations, HOA documents, and sidewalk layout at final development plan (FTP) review
  • Aaron Ellerman (chaplain) to chair the Long-Term Recovery Committee for tornado relief

Financials

  • $12,500 donation from Mercy Health Clermont County through Connect Clermont
  • $10,000 Duke Energy grant awarded for fire department
  • $855,000 uninsured loss threshold required for state public assistance eligibility
  • Several million dollars in damage to township-owned property
  • $200,000 to $500,000 price range for proposed Pebble Groves homes
  • ~$220,000 price point for two-family units
  • 4,200 loads of debris hauled from tornado cleanup

  • 23 of 25 required uninsured destroyed properties identified so far

2022-06-28 Regular Meeting contentious
1h 45m ▶ Vimeo

BOT Reg Mtg 6.28.22.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting covering community forum items including zoning complaints, a request to live on property during construction, a lot split delay, and a denied building permit that was later overturned by court. The board extensively discussed the use of American Rescue Plan (ARP) funds for a major paving project, approved a base bid of $936,640 for road paving across six roads, approved a replacement truck body and V-box spreader for the service department, and entered executive session regarding complaints against a public employee.

Community forum: Library promotion and services (Lisa from Goshen Public Library)Community forum: Zoning violations complaint at Maple Drive and Abandoned Hill RoadCommunity forum: Request to live on property in campers during home construction (2439 Woodville Pike)Community forum: Lot split application delayed in zoning department for two monthsCommunity forum: Building permit denial overturned by Clermont County court (rear-to-rear setback misinterpretation)ARP (American Rescue Plan) fund project status update and spending prioritiesMajor 2022-2023 road paving project using OPWC grant and ARP fundsVillages of Belmont Phase 2 - Condition #8 road repair responsibility dispute with Drees HomesService department plow truck bed replacement with flatbed body and V-box spreaderIRS mileage reimbursement rate mid-year increase to 62.5 centsNuisance property declarationDebate over ARP fund allocation priorities (paving vs. electronic sign vs. service garage)Clermont County Park District improvements to township park (paved entry and parking lot)National Night Out scheduled for first Wednesday in August at Mark Cook School campusSergeant leadership training program approvalDocTrack software renewalExecutive session for investigation of complaints against public employeeSteve Pegram completed three-week Harvard program

  • Motion to adopt amended agenda - passed

  • Motion to increase appropriations $50,000 in road and bridge fund for road paving - passed

  • Motion to increase appropriations in gas tax fund $36,517 for truck body replacement - passed

  • ~$70,000 - Estimated cost per electronic sign (from prior pricing)

  • ~$300,000 - Projected fire department parking lot/repair bid
  • $413,000 - General fund unencumbered balance (~$363K usable)

2022-07-19 Special Meeting urgent
25m ▶ Vimeo

Spc. BOT Mtg 7.19.22.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting to address zoning relief measures for residents affected by the July 6, 2022 tornado. The board adopted an emergency resolution directing staff on zoning provisions, creating a new application form for tornado-damaged properties, waiving fees, allowing temporary RV/camper placement during rebuilding, protecting nonconforming uses, and implementing a temporary moratorium on enforcement of certain zoning provisions. The meeting lasted approximately 25 minutes.

Zoning relief for July 6, 2022 tornado recoveryWaiver of zoning permit fees for tornado-damaged propertiesTemporary allowance for RVs/campers on properties during rebuildingProtection of nonconforming uses to allow rebuildingNew tornado-specific zoning application form and numbering system (GT-2022-XXX-T)Expedited application processing for tornado-affected residentsSite plan requirements and setback complianceCounty coordination on building permit fees

Decisions (3)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed unanimously
  • Resolution R-041-2022 adopted unanimously: directing staff on zoning relief for tornado-affected properties, authorizing new application forms, implementing temporary moratorium on enforcement of certain zoning provisions, waiving fees, and declaring an emergency
  • Motion to adjourn at 7:25 PM passed unanimously

Action Items (4)

  • Staff to help tornado-affected property owners with site plans using GIS capabilities
  • Staff to contact known tornado-damaged property owners to inform them of relief measures
  • Staff to post resolution information on Facebook and other public notification sites
  • Zoning/building department to implement new permit numbering system with 'T' suffix for tornado-related applications

Financials

  • Zoning fees waived for construction and reconstruction of structures destroyed by act of God (tornado)
Trustee HodgesTrustee AllenTrustee HausermanBen YoderDan (Director/staff)Gary (Director Holberg)Kerry (staff member)Victor Eckler (mentioned, did legwork on zoning resolution)Commissioner Painter (mentioned, assisted with county permit fee clarification)

2022-06-28 Special Meeting routine
8m ▶ Vimeo

Spc. Mtg 6.28.22 July Tax Budget.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting and public hearing for the proposed 2023 tax budget. The administrator presented a preliminary budget overview noting that revenue is higher than expenses across departments, with savings in the service department due to ARPA and grant money covering paving costs. The police department projects approximately $3 million in revenue against $2.9 million in expenses, and the fire/EMS department has ongoing capital payments for a fire truck and two ambulances. The board approved the 2023 tax budget for submission to the county auditor by July 15.

2023 preliminary tax budget presentation and public hearingService department savings due to ARPA and grant money covering paving costsPolice department budget and vehicle purchases planned for next yearFire/EMS capital payments (fire truck Station 19 and two ambulances)Fire and EMS levy renewal discussion anticipated for 2024Zoning revenue exceeding full-year projections alreadyUpcoming concerns about property/casualty insurance, workers comp, and contract renewals

Decisions (5)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed unanimously
  • Motion to open the public hearing for 2023 tax budget passed unanimously
  • Motion to close the public hearing for 2023 tax budget passed unanimously
  • Motion to approve the proposed 2023 tax budget passed unanimously
  • Motion to adjourn at 6:14 PM passed

Action Items (3)

  • Administrator to work with Fiscal Officer England to enter the 2023 tax budget into UAN and submit it to the county auditor by July 15
  • Administration to conduct a deeper budget review closer to December with real numbers
  • Public hearing on the July tax budget to be advertised on the township website and in the newspaper per ORC requirements

Financials

  • $927,000 projected general fund spending
  • $662,000 ending unencumbered balance (general fund), compared to $656,000 last year
  • $413,000 unencumbered balance in service department
  • $980,000 projected service department revenue next year
  • Over $700,000 projected service department expenses
  • $850,000+ unencumbered balance in police department
  • Over $3 million projected police department revenue
  • $2.9 million projected police department expenses
  • $893,000 projected police department ending unencumbered balance
  • $550,000+ projected fire/EMS unencumbered balance
  • Fire engine paid off in 2027; two ambulances paid off in 2024

2022-07-12 Special Meeting urgent
1h 11m ▶ Vimeo

Special BOT MTG 7.12.22

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting primarily focused on the aftermath of a tornado that struck the community on July 6, 2022. Department heads provided updates on damage to emergency facilities, police cruisers, fire apparatus, and community infrastructure. The board took several actions including appropriating $100,000 for tornado recovery, approving demolition contracts for the damaged fire station, suspending zoning permit fees, and ratifying the emergency declaration made electronically the week prior. Community members expressed gratitude for the emergency response and mutual aid from surrounding municipalities.

Aftermath and recovery from July 6, 2022 tornado that damaged township emergency facilitiesFire Station 18 damage, demolition, and temporary relocation to township building basementPolice department damage and mutual aid security patrols to prevent lootingMassive multi-municipality debris removal effort (1,971 loads in one week)FEMA aid unlikely due to small scale relative to state; state aid potentially available based on uninsured property damageZoning and permit process modifications for tornado-affected propertiesNon-conforming structures and rebuilding regulations discussionLocal government fund redistribution formula (Resolution R-035-2022)Community volunteer response including Lions Club, CPAA, churches, and food pantriesUnmet Needs Committee formation with county EMA, schools, and community organizationsResignation of firefighter paramedic and promotion of part-time to full-timeRatification of emergency electronic board meeting and state of emergency declarationClermont County suspended building permit fees through August; siren test scheduled for next dayDuke Energy response and grant support from Duke Foundation

Decisions (10)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda - passed
  • Motion to accept resignation of full-time firefighter paramedic James Mc effective July 18, 2022 - passed
  • Motion to promote a part-time firefighter EMT paramedic to full-time status effective July 17, 2022 - passed
  • Motion to rescind Resolution R-034-2022 (regarding local government fund) - passed
  • Motion to approve Resolution R-035-2022 adopting alternate method of apportionment of undivided local government fund for calendar year 2023 - passed
  • Motion to increase appropriation in general fund line item 1000-110-590-0706 (Other expenses - 7622 Tornado) by $100,000 - passed
  • Motion to suspend zoning permit fees (related to tornado damage) - passed
  • Motion to approve contract with Conger Construction not to exceed $100,000 for demolition and securing of tornado-damaged Station 18 - passed
  • Motion to approve Resolution R-037-2022 ratifying Resolution R-036-2022 and all actions taken pursuant to the emergency declaration - passed
  • Motion to adjourn - passed

Action Items (8)

  • Austin (Brinker and Eckler counsel) and Harry (zoning inspector) to develop proposals for streamlined zoning/permit processes for tornado-affected properties and present at next Tuesday's special meeting
  • Cheryl to post notice for special meeting on Tuesday July 19, 2022
  • Administrator Pegram to enter into contract with Conger Construction for Station 18 demolition and securing
  • Chief Myers to apply for additional Duke Foundation grant for fire department needs
  • Harry and building department to work with Duke Energy and EMA to develop instructions for residents to get power restored
  • Staff to continue updating tornado relief page on township website and Facebook
  • David Barkle to follow up with township regarding motorcycle fundraiser run on August 27th route planning and coordination
  • Administrator Pegram to determine whether $100,000 tornado appropriation comes from general fund, fire fund, or ARP fund

Financials

  • $100,000 appropriated for tornado recovery fund (new line item 1000-110-590-0706)
  • $100,000 not-to-exceed contract with Conger Construction for Station 18 demolition and securing ($80,000 estimated actual cost)
  • Less than $10,000 additional funding from new local government fund formula for Goshen
  • 1,971 total loads of debris removed through July 12
  • 62 total dump trucks from 25 different municipalities assisted with cleanup
  • 6 of 9 marked police cruisers damaged
  • 8 fire department vehicles damaged
  • 26 different mutual aid fire departments responded
  • ~50 different municipalities provided aid
  • Second tranche of ARP (American Rescue Plan) money received from federal government
  • Property tax payments received same day

BZA 62322 2 of 2

The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals (BZA) resumed its meeting to address zoning cases. BZA-2022-014 was continued to the August 15th meeting. BZA-2022-015, an appeal for 6765 Goshen Road involving a behavioral health and recovery center (Three Waters Recovery), was discussed extensively, including whether the facility constitutes a hospital or a medical/dental clinic under zoning definitions. The appeal was granted unanimously.

Continuance of BZA-2022-014 variance requestAppeal for 6765 Goshen Road (BZA-2022-015) regarding Three Waters Recovery behavioral health and recovery centerZoning definition debate: hospital vs. medical/dental office and clinic classificationParking space sufficiency for the facilityNeighbor concern about behavioral health center proximity to residential property with childrenOhio Revised Code Section 3701.07 hospital definition discussion

  • BZA-2022-014 variance request continued to August 15th meeting
  • BZA-2022-015 appeal for 6765 Goshen Road granted (passed 3-0: Curie yes, Siegler yes, Theodore yes)

Action Items (2)

  • Next BZA meeting scheduled for July 18th
  • Consider barrier/screening for neighboring residential property per John Rose's request

BZA 6.23.22 1 of 2.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals held a public hearing addressing old business cases including dismissal of two dumping complaints and resolutions for previously decided variance and conditional use cases. New business included granting a variance for an above-ground pool in a front yard due to topography challenges, and extensive testimony and public comment on a use variance request by Jeffrey Kelly to operate a truck repair business in a residential (R3) zoned area at 6823 Goshen Road, where the building was originally permitted as an agricultural-exempt structure.

  • Resolution approving variance for David and Christina Tudor at 6866 Clubside Drive (deck in rear yard setback) - passed
  • Resolution approving conditional use for Alicia Reinerhart at 6790 Gaynor Road (56 ground-mounted solar panels) - passed
  • Resolution denying variance for Ryan Holmes at Codes of Barclay Woods on State Route 48 (1,366 sq ft home below 1,400 sq ft minimum) - passed
  • BZA 2022-010 variance approved for Anthony Moni at 6559 Smith Road for above-ground pool in front yard due to topography, with 10-foot side setback requirement maintained

  • Resolution for Reinerhart conditional use to be signed by chair and secretary
  • Jeff Kelly use variance case (BZA 2022-013) deliberation to continue (transcript truncated before decision)

2022-06-27 Special Meeting routine
3h 10m ▶ Vimeo

Spc Mtg BOT 6.27.22 Zoning Workshop.mp4

Goshen Township held a special meeting work session on zoning, introducing Ben Yoder of Bricker and Eckler as new special counsel for zoning matters. Yoder presented an extensive training on the roles of the Zoning Commission, Board of Zoning Appeals (BZA), and Board of Trustees, covering hearing procedures, best practices for running meetings, maintaining good records, swearing in witnesses, the distinction between legislative and administrative actions, non-conforming uses, variances, conditional uses, and ethics considerations. The session brought together members of all three zoning-related boards for collaborative discussion.

Introduction of Ben Yoder as special counsel for zoningRoles and powers of Zoning Commission, BZA, and Board of TrusteesDifference between legislative and administrative actions in zoningHearing procedures and best practices for zoning meetingsUse variances vs. area/dimensional variancesNon-conforming uses and how to handle themSwearing in witnesses and maintaining good records for legal defensibilityRole of Clermont County Regional Planning CommissionSite plan review processConditional uses and their standardsApplication deadlines and staff report preparationRaising minimum house square footage requirementZoning inspector decision appeals to BZA

Decisions (0)

  • None recorded

Action Items (5)

  • Harry to send BZA/Zoning Commission chairperson hearing statements to Ben Yoder for review and critique
  • Ben Yoder to provide sign-in sheet format template to the boards
  • Harry to discuss specific use variance case (tractor-trailer repair shop in residential district) with Ben Yoder after the session
  • Trustees and boards to consider what Goshen Township should look like in the next 10 years
  • Trustee Hodges to pursue discussion on raising minimum house square footage above 1,400 sq ft

Financials

  • 1,400 square feet - current minimum house size requirement in Goshen Township
  • 1,500 square feet - referenced as example updated minimum in non-conforming use discussion
  • 150 attorneys at Bricker and Eckler firm
  • Over 1,000 political subdivisions represented by Bricker and Eckler
  • 10,000 people - population threshold for limited home rule township exemption from regional planning commission

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees reconvened and discussed multiple items including a dump truck bed replacement, hiring a new firefighter paramedic, local government fund apportionment, scheduling a zoning workshop, reallocating ARP funds for hiring a zoning attorney, and a drone purchase presentation for use by fire, police, service, and zoning departments. The meeting concluded with an executive session regarding public employee matters.

Dump truck bed replacement options and used truck alternativesHiring of probationary firefighter paramedic John GroomsLocal government fund apportionment resolution for 2023-2027Special joint zoning workshop meeting scheduled for June 27, 2022Hiring of Bricker and Eckler as special zoning counselDrone acquisition presentation for multi-department use (fire, police, service, zoning)Legal concerns about drone use for zoning enforcement and admissibility of evidenceExecutive session for employee compensation/appointment matters

  • Motion to approve Resolution R0292022 authorizing alternative apportionment of the undivided local government fund for 2023-2027 – passed
  • Motion to set special joint meeting of Board of Trustees, Zoning Commission, and Board of Zoning Appeals for June 27, 2022 at 6:30 PM for zoning workshop – passed
  • Motion to reallocate $50,000 from ARP funds for hiring of zoning attorney – passed
  • Motion to approve and authorize Township Administrator to sign engagement letter retaining Ben Yoder and Bricker and Eckler as special counsel for zoning – passed
  • Motion to reallocate $15,000 from ARP funds for drone purchase – passed
  • Dump truck bed replacement tabled to June 24 agenda
  • Motion to adjourn into executive session at 8:09 PM per ORC 121.22G1 – passed

  • Township Administrator to sign engagement letter with Ben Yoder / Bricker and Eckler for zoning legal counsel
  • Chief Myers to proceed with drone acquisition under $9,000 budget
  • John Grooms to begin as probationary full-time firefighter paramedic on June 19, 2022

2022-06-01 Special Meeting contentious
1h 46m ▶ Vimeo

Spc Mtg BOT 6.1.22.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting as a public hearing to consider two zoning cases. In ZC-2022-003, the board unanimously denied a request by Ryan Homes/Goshen Development LLC to build a 1,366 sq ft home model in Barclay Woods, as it fell below the township's 1,400 sq ft minimum floor area requirement. In ZC-2022-004, the board considered a 117-lot PUD development by Grand Communities LLC on Curbit Road, hearing extensive public testimony about traffic safety, drainage, road improvements, and property values before moving toward approval with conditions.

Denial of Ryan Homes 1,366 sq ft ranch model below 1,400 sq ft minimum in Barclay Woods PUDGrand Communities 117-lot PUD development on Curbit Road (ZC-2022-004)Traffic safety concerns at Curbit Road and SR 132 intersectionEmergency access road vs. public road debate - resolved as gated 20-foot paved emergency accessCurbit Road widening and infrastructure improvementsStormwater drainage and retention pond concerns from adjacent property ownersProperty value impacts on existing Curbit Road residentsWater line extension along Curbit RoadSchool capacity concerns with new residential developmentComparison to prior Fisher Homes proposal denied 17 years agoClermont County Planning Commission approved 6-0 with conditionsGoshen Township Zoning Commission approved 4-0 with conditionsRelief requested for rear-loading garage and 5-foot garage setback requirements

Decisions (3)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed unanimously (3-0)
  • ZC-2022-003: Motion to deny the 1,366 sq ft Spruce home model for Barclay Woods passed unanimously (3-0)
  • ZC-2022-004: Motion to approve Grand Communities 117-lot PUD on Curbit Road with conditions (approval indicated in discussion, transcript truncated before final vote)

Action Items (5)

  • Grand Communities to provide project manager contact information to township staff for construction complaints
  • Grand Communities to implement weekly street sweeping and monitoring program on Curbit Road during construction
  • Fire Chief Ed Myers to approve specifications for gated 20-foot emergency access road per Ohio Fire Code
  • Grand Communities to work with Clermont County on Curbit Road widening improvements including culvert replacement and utility relocation
  • Harry Holbrook to coordinate with applicant on Curbit Road driveway apron requirements and restoration standards

Financials

  • 1,366 sq ft proposed Spruce model vs. 1,400 sq ft minimum requirement (ZC-2022-003)
  • $280,000-$300,000 price range for Spruce ranch home model
  • $284,350 starting price range for Grand Communities homes
  • 117 proposed homes on 39.48 acres at 2.96 dwelling units per acre
  • 35.7% open space provided (35% required)
  • $50,000 estimated property value loss cited by resident due to proposed road
  • $7,000 approximate water tap-in fee mentioned by resident
  • $1,000+ spent by resident on culvert pipe replacement
  • 6,240 sq ft minimum lot size proposed (5,000 sq ft minimum required)
  • Previous consent decree approved 255 homes on same property; now proposing 117

BOT MTG 5.24.2022.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting on May 24, 2022. The board accepted several Police Week donations including gift cards from Texas Roadhouse, LaRosa's via CP AAA, and from Jimmy and Donna Ropsick. Personnel matters were addressed including resignations of part-time firefighters and a volunteer termination, and after an executive session the board voted to terminate a probationary full-time firefighter paramedic. Administrator Pegram discussed a recent successful Township Association meeting with 30 community leaders and announced plans for regular meetings among administrators of the five largest Clermont County townships.

Police Week donation acceptancesFire department personnel changes – resignations and termination of volunteerExecutive session regarding public employee discipline/terminationTermination of probationary full-time firefighter paramedicTownship Association meeting recap with 30 community leadersNew initiative for five largest Clermont County township administrators to meet regularlyTrash cleanup on a Township roadResolution from Township Association needing board voteTraining fire scheduled on 132 North

  • Motion to adopt the agenda – passed
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (May 11–May 24, 2022) – passed

  • Motion to accept four $40 LaRosa's gift cards from CP AAA – passed
  • Motion to accept a $100 LaRosa's gift card from Jimmy and Donna Ropsick – passed
  • Motion to accept resignation of part-time firefighter Slate – passed
  • Motion to accept resignation of part-time firefighter EMT Matt Traver – passed
  • Motion to terminate volunteer Tyler Fritz effective 5-20-2022 due to inability to obtain firefighter certification – passed
  • Motion to adjourn into executive session at 7:13 PM – passed
  • Motion to terminate a probationary full-time firefighter paramedic effective May 24, 2022 at 0700 – passed
  • Motion to adjourn at 8:57 PM – passed

Action Items (3)

  • Donna to forward the resolution to all three trustees for vote at the May 28th meeting

  • Fire department to conduct training burn on 132 North on May 25, 2022

Gosen Zoning Mtg 4-5-22

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held a public hearing on April 5, 2022, addressing three zoning cases. They approved a final development plan for TPT Holding at 6600 Patricia Boulevard, approved a zone change to PUD for Green Girl Garden landscaping business at 1591 Petz Avenue with conditions including a driveway agreement with neighboring property owners, and approved a PDO plan for Stole Properties LLC at 1636 State Route 28 with multiple conditions including paving, screening, and landscaping requirements.

Final development plan review for TPT Holding at 6600 Patricia Boulevard (PBDD)Zone change from B2 to PUD for Green Girl Garden landscaping business at 1591 Petz AvenuePrivate driveway access and maintenance concerns from neighboring property owners on Petz LanePDO State Route 28 overlay district zone change for Stole Properties LLC at 1636 State Route 28Revocation of erroneously issued zoning permit for 1636 State Route 28Building face material relief from 80% brick/stone/split face block/EFIS requirementStormwater retention and watershed concerns

Decisions (4)

  • Approved minutes from previous meeting
  • ZC2021-007: Approved final development plan for TPT Holding at 6600 Patricia Boulevard with Board of Trustees conditions
  • ZC2022-001: Approved zone change to PUD for Green Girl Garden at 1591 Petz Avenue with conditions including mutual driveway agreement with neighboring property owners
  • ZC2022-002: Approved PDO plan for Stole Properties LLC at 1636 State Route 28 with staff conditions including paving, privacy fencing, screening, landscaping, and other requirements

Action Items (6)

  • Mears Tucker (Green Girl Garden) to work out a mutual driveway maintenance agreement with neighboring property owners on the private drive
  • Mears Tucker to provide documentation regarding legal ingress/egress to the subject property at 1591 Petz Avenue
  • Stole Properties LLC to comply with all staff conditions including paving, privacy fencing, screening, landscaping, exterior lighting shielding, and cross access easement
  • Board of Trustees to revoke the erroneously issued zoning permit for 1636 State Route 28
  • Gary to send email to commission members if there are cases for next meeting
  • ZC2022-001 and ZC2022-002 to go before Board of Trustees on April 26, 2022

Financials

  • 6,720 square feet per building at 1636 State Route 28 (13,440 total for both buildings)
  • 10 proposed parking spaces for Stole Properties at 1636 State Route 28
  • ISR change from 9.4% to 16.7% at 1636 State Route 28
  • 15-foot ingress/egress utility easement at 1591 Petz Avenue
  • 6-foot minimum to 8-foot maximum privacy fencing required (multiple cases)
  • Clermont County Planning Commission recommended approval 7-0 for ZC2022-002
  • 10% gross area to be preserved as green space for ZC2022-002

Goshen Twp. BOT Mtg 5.10.22

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting covering American Recovery Plan (ARP) fund reallocations, road paving updates, employee evaluation process changes, and various administrative items. The board recognized Linda Griffin with a community honor, discussed upcoming zoning review meetings, approved a school resource officer contract with Goshen Local Schools, and entered executive session with no resulting actions.

American Recovery Plan (ARP) fund allocations and project updatesRoad paving plan combining 2022 and 2023 projects with OPWC grantAdministration building facade and roof renovation projectsRecognition of Linda Griffin as community honoreePolice department commended for solving Dollar General robberySchool resource officers contract renewal with Goshen Local SchoolsUpcoming zoning and land use plan review discussionEmployee evaluation process redesignLand bank redevelopment inquiryExecutive session held

Decisions (10)

  • Adopted amended agenda
  • Approved payment of bills for April 27 through May 10, 2022
  • Approved reallocation of $73,999 in ARP funds for vehicle purchases (Hodges abstained)
  • Approved reallocation of $18,828 in ARP funds for road signs
  • Reappointed Bill Tudor and Jessica Holmes to the Goshen Park Board for three-year terms (May 2022–May 2025)
  • Accepted $50 donation from Shannon and Esther Wilde
  • Approved 2022-2023 school resource officers contract with Goshen Local Schools
  • Declared surplus and approved sale on GovDeal of six laptop mounts and one vehicle gun mount
  • Entered executive session at 7:47 PM; no motions resulted
  • Adjourned regular meeting at 9:27 PM

Action Items (5)

  • Administrator Pegram to coordinate with Harry and Mike to schedule a special zoning/land use review meeting, targeting June 14 or June 12 as backup
  • Paving bids to go out in June for 2022 and 2023 road projects including Thompson and Gibbs (OPWC grant roads)
  • Administration building facade project started; roof project planned for late summer
  • Staff to get pictures of Ranger for surplus listing on GovDeal
  • Administrator Pegram to keep board updated on land bank redevelopment progress

Financials

  • $73,999 ARP funds reallocated for vehicle purchases
  • $18,828 ARP funds reallocated for road signs
  • $50 donation from Shannon and Esther Wilde
  • OPWC grant awarded for Thompson and Gibbs roads (50/50 match with state)

2022-05-03 Regular Meeting contentious
2h 31m ▶ Vimeo

Goshen Zoning Mtg 5.3.2022

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held a public hearing on May 3, 2022, addressing three zoning cases. ZC 2022-003 involved Ryan Homes requesting a major adjustment to the Coves of Barclay Woods PUD to allow a 1,366 sq ft ranch home (below the 1,400 sq ft minimum); the commission denied the request. ZC 2022-004 involved Grand Communities proposing a 117-unit subdivision on Curbert Road, which was approved with staff recommendations despite significant public opposition regarding traffic, drainage, and property impacts. ZC 2022-005 involved Grand Communities proposing a 310-lot multi-generational community on Linton Road with single-family, two-family, and duplex products, which generated substantial public concern about density, traffic near schools, and loss of rural character.

Ryan Homes request to build 1,366 sq ft ranch homes in Coves of Barclay Woods PUD (denied)Grand Communities 117-unit Curbert Road subdivision (approved with conditions)Grand Communities 310-lot Linton Road multi-generational community with duplexesTraffic concerns on Curbert Road due to dead-end road and ball field trafficTraffic concerns on Linton Road near Goshen schoolsStormwater drainage concerns from residents on both Curbert and Linton RoadsProperty value impact concerns from adjacent homeownersPrivacy and buffer concerns from neighboring residentsRural character preservation vs. development density debatePrecedent-setting concerns for future zoning variance requestsRoad widening and infrastructure improvements on Curbert Road and Linton RoadSewer extension needs for Clermont County parkPedestrian connectivity to schools and parksRear-loading garage relief requests for both Grand Communities projectsRecruitment appeal for volunteer zoning commission members

Decisions (5)

  • ZC 2021-01 and ZC 2022-02 previously approved by Board of Trustees (old business confirmation)
  • Minutes from previous meeting approved (with one abstention from Mr. Denwiddie)
  • ZC 2022-003 (Coves of Barclay Woods / Ryan Homes 1,366 sq ft ranch): Motion to approve with cap of 6 units failed for lack of second; motion to continue to next meeting failed for lack of second; motion to deny the proposed major adjustment passed (Curry aye, Denwiddie yes, Lewis yes, Settle yes)
  • ZC 2022-004 (Curbert Road / Grand Communities 117-unit subdivision): Approved with staff recommendations (Curry aye, Denwiddie yes, Lewis yes, Settle yes)
  • ZC 2022-005 (Linton Road / Grand Communities 310-lot subdivision): Discussion held, transcript truncated before final vote

Action Items (9)

  • Harry to update PA system for meeting room within next month or two
  • Grand Communities to provide streetlight plan for Linton Road project in PDP submittal at June 1, 2022 Board of Trustee meeting
  • Grand Communities to provide dwelling unit density calculations for Linton Road project for June 1, 2022 Board of Trustee meeting
  • Grand Communities to provide traffic studies for Linton Road at specified times: 8-8:30 AM, 2:30-4 PM, and 4-6 PM
  • Grand Communities to work with adjacent property owners on Curbert Road regarding privacy fence for properties next to new road
  • Grand Communities to provide sidewalks on development side of Linton Road per county and township standards
  • Grand Communities to coordinate with Clermont County Park District regarding sewer extension and pedestrian connection to park
  • Grand Communities to coordinate with school district regarding crosswalk or pedestrian connection on Linton Road
  • Staff to seek ODOT verification on traffic study recommendations for Linton Road

Financials

  • 1,366 sq ft proposed ranch home by Ryan Homes (34 sq ft below 1,400 sq ft minimum)
  • 1,400 sq ft minimum floor area required by Goshen Township zoning resolution
  • 138 total lots in Coves of Barclay Woods subdivision
  • 117 total units proposed for Curbert Road subdivision (39.48 acres)
  • 310 lots proposed for Linton Road subdivision (113.43 acres)
  • 2.96 dwelling units per acre for Curbert Road (max 3 allowed)
  • 2.73 dwelling units per acre for Linton Road (max 3 allowed)
  • 35.7% open space for Curbert Road (35% required)
  • 36.97% open space for Linton Road (35% required)
  • 6,240 sq ft minimum lot size for Curbert Road (5,000 sq ft required)
  • 4,000 sq ft minimum lot size proposed for Linton Road (5,000 sq ft required - not met)
  • 34 ft minimum lot width proposed for Linton Road duplexes (50 ft required - not met)
  • $275,000 estimated current property value for resident on Curbert Road
  • $210,000-$230,000 estimated property value after road construction (per resident testimony)
  • $50 per meeting pay for zoning commission members
  • $1,000+ spent by Curbert Road resident on culvert replacement
  • $15,000 spent by neighbor on entryway improvements that would be removed
  • 1,000 sq ft minimum for multi-family/duplex units on Linton Road (700 sq ft required)

2022-04-26 Regular Meeting routine
56m ▶ Vimeo

Goshen Twp. BOT 4/26/2022

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting on April 26, 2022. The board heard an announcement that the township park has been officially transferred to Clermont County Park District. Two zoning cases were heard and approved: a B-2 to PDM rezoning for a landscape business at 1591 State Route 28, and a B-2 to PDM rezoning for warehouse/product assembly buildings at 1636 State Route 28. A noise complaint from residents near State Route 28 regarding a landscaping company operating late at night was also raised during community forum.

Transfer of Goshen Township park to Clermont County Park DistrictZoning case ZC-2022-001: B-2 to PDM rezoning for landscape business at 1591 State Route 28Zoning case ZC-2022-002: B-2 to PDM rezoning for warehouse/product assembly at 1636 State Route 28Noise complaint from residents near State Route 28 about late-night business operationsLocal Government Fund distribution negotiations with villages, cities, and townshipsClermont County Planning Commission recommendations on proposed Curb and Linton subdivision developmentsOverburning issues in the township being addressed via social media and websiteRecent CPR save by fire department

Decisions (5)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed unanimously
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (April 13-26, 2022) passed unanimously
  • Zoning case ZC-2022-001 (1591 State Route 28) approved with staff recommendations and PDM zoning designation instead of M-1
  • Zoning case ZC-2022-002 (1636 State Route 28) approved with conditions as submitted under PDM zoning
  • Motion to adjourn at 7:55 PM passed

Action Items (7)

  • Director Holbert to work with Dale and Victoria Sweet regarding noise complaint from business near 1785 State Route 28
  • Harry Holbert to send out notices and information on proposed Curb and Linton subdivision developments to zoning commission and then board of trustees
  • Jessica Holmes/Park Board to finalize fiscal year-end matters related to park transfer to Clermont County
  • Clermont Capital to issue public relations announcement regarding park transfer
  • Mears Tucker (1591 SR 28) to obtain formal maintenance agreement with shared driveway property owners after property closing
  • Daniel Mac (1636 SR 28) to submit updated architect plans with 5-foot stone veneer, landscaping plan, green space notation, and apply for building permits
  • Board to review Local Government Fund distribution formula paperwork ahead of May Township Association meeting

Financials

  • 1591 State Route 28 property: 0.46-0.49 acres
  • 1591 SR 28 existing building: approximately 3,258 square feet (600 sq ft office, 2,200 sq ft garage/storage)
  • 1636 SR 28: two buildings each 6,720 square feet for combined 13,340 square feet (note: transcript figures slightly inconsistent)
  • 1636 SR 28 impervious surface ratio: existing 9.4%, proposed 16.7% (code allows up to 75%)
  • 1636 SR 28: 10 total parking spaces proposed (5 per building)
  • 10% minimum green space preservation required for 1636 SR 28

Goshen Twp. BZA Mtg 4/18/22

The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals held a public hearing addressing multiple cases including variance requests, an appeal denial, a conditional use request for ground-mounted solar panels, and a withdrawal of an application. The board appointed a new secretary, approved and denied several resolutions, and scheduled a special meeting for June 23, 2022.

Appointment of new BZA secretaryVariance for sidewalk requirements in TC district (CDDG Goshen LLC)Denial of privacy fence variance in front yardNon-conforming principle structure classification at Greenstone TraceAppeal withdrawal by Sierra LewisDenial of appeal of planning and zoning director's decision (Humphreys/Campbell)Variance for new principle structure behind non-conforming agricultural accessory structuresRear setback variance for covered patio at Clubside Drive near golf course pondConditional use approval for ground-mounted solar panels exceeding 200 sq ft code limitDiscussion of need to update zoning code for solar panel installationsScheduling of special June meeting due to Juneteenth federal holiday

Decisions (11)

  • Appointed Mr. Curry as new BZA secretary
  • Approved previous meeting minutes
  • Approved variance request for CDDG Goshen LLC regarding sidewalk requirements at 1910 State Route 28 (BZA 2022-001)
  • Approved resolution denying variance request for accessory structure/privacy fence at 1910 State Route 28 (BZA 2022-002)
  • Approved variance for Victor Capitola/Capital Custom Homes for non-conforming principle structure at 6981 Greenstone Trace (BZA 2022-003)
  • Accepted withdrawal of appeal application by Sierra Lewis (BZA 2022-004)
  • Approved resolution denying appeal of planning and zoning director's decision for Scott Humphreys and Amy Campbell at 2439 Woodville Pike (BZA 2022-005)
  • Approved variance to construct principle structure behind existing accessory structures for Scott Humphreys and Amy Campbell (BZA 2022-006)
  • Approved variance reducing rear setback from 35 feet to 16 feet for Dave and Christina Tutor at 6866 Clubside Drive for covered patio (BZA 2022-007)
  • Approved conditional use for 1200 sq ft ground-mounted solar panels for Rodney and Victoria Siebel at 6790 Gaines Road (BZA 2022-008)
  • Scheduled special BZA meeting for June 23, 2022 at 6:30 PM

Action Items (3)

  • Mr. Curry to serve as new BZA secretary
  • Staff to notify applicants of board decisions in accordance with legal authority
  • Schedule and notify members of special BZA meeting on June 23, 2022

Financials

  • 35-foot rear setback reduced to 16 feet for Tutor covered patio variance (BZA 2022-007)
  • 200 square feet maximum lot area for freestanding solar panels per current code vs. 1078 square feet (two arrays: 11x56 and 11x42) requested for solar panel conditional use (BZA 2022-008)
  • 20-foot wide private drainage easement on Tutor property
  • 15-foot utility easement and 15x15 fire hydrant easement noted on Clubside Drive property

BOT 4.12.22.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting covering ARP (American Rescue Plan) fund allocations for multiple capital projects, recognized firefighters for Bauer Awards, approved a basement renovation/gym project, adopted new fee schedules for subdivision addressing and lot splits, approved vehicle purchases, and appointed a new full-time police officer following executive session.

Recognition of Lt. Travis Allen and Lt. Todd Estes for Bauer Awards from Ohio Fire Service Hall of FameARP (American Rescue Plan) funding overview and project prioritizationBasement renovation and employee fitness facility projectJuneteenth holiday MOUs with three bargaining unitsNew fee schedules for subdivision addressing and lot splitsMultiple upcoming zoning cases including Grand Communities proposing 117 and 310 unit subdivisionsHomes cleanup project and Brownfield grant applicationSpeed study request for Shiloh Road south of WoodvillePurchase of service department mower and pickup truckPurchase of police vehicle (Chevrolet Blazer) for new positionAppointment of Tyler Kramer as full-time police officerMOUs correcting vacation step increase language for GTPA and FOPElectronic sign discussion for township buildingsTim Rudd introduction as county auditor candidateDetective Greg McAllister to be recognized at annual law enforcement banquetRescinding and replacing Choice One Engineering pavement analysis resolution

Decisions (21)

  • Motion to amend agenda moving item F to item B - passed
  • Motion to adopt agenda as amended - passed
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (March 23 - April 12, 2022) - passed
  • Motion to approve reallocation of ARP funds for basement renovation project - passed
  • Motion to approve basement renovation project - passed
  • Motion to approve MOUs with IAFF Local 3932, GTPA, and FOP adding June 19th (Juneteenth) as a holiday - passed
  • Motion to adopt fee schedule for addressing for subdivisions - passed
  • Motion to adopt fee schedule change for lot splits for subdivisions - passed
  • Motion to rescind resolution R-020-2022 (Choice One engineering contract authorization) - passed
  • Motion to approve resolution R-023-2022 authorizing township administrator to contract with Choice One Engineering for pavement analysis - passed
  • Motion to accept six $10 Skyline gift cards from Wage for Apparel - passed
  • Motion to accept five $10 gift cards from Go to Community Church - passed
  • Motion to accept $25 gift card from anonymous donor - passed
  • Motion to approve purchase of Gravely Pro Stance mower for $8,999 from ARP funds - passed (Trustee Hodges abstained due to employment at Caning Equipment)
  • Motion to approve purchase of Ford F-250 pickup truck for $25,000 from ARP funds - passed
  • Motion to approve resolution R-022-2022 requesting speed study on Shiloh Road south of Woodville - passed
  • Motion to approve purchase of 2022 Chevrolet Blazer not to exceed $36,500 for police department - passed
  • Motion to adjourn into executive session at 8:09 PM - passed
  • Motion to appoint Tyler Kramer as full-time police officer effective April 18, 2022 - passed
  • Motion to approve MOUs with GTPA and FOP regarding vacation step increases - passed
  • Motion to adjourn at 9:24 PM - passed

Action Items (13)

  • Steve Pegram to meet with union bosses and chiefs to sign and execute Juneteenth MOUs
  • Steve Pegram to place Park Board reappointments (Phil Tudor and Jessica Holmes) on May agenda
  • Harry to bring REZ and JEDS applications information to Administrator Pegram for board presentation
  • Harry to present zoning cases 2022-001 and 2022-002 at April 26 meeting
  • Harry to present zoning cases 2022-003, 2022-004, and 2022-005 at June 1 meeting
  • Steve Pegram to research and get bids for electronic signs for fire stations and township building
  • Chad to get paving quotes for upcoming road paving projects
  • Steve Pegram to work on logistics for April 26 meeting conflicting with Salute to Leaders event
  • Sergeant West/Chief Rose to begin basement renovation project right away
  • Steve Pegram to sign Choice One Engineering contract for Belmont subdivision pavement analysis
  • Chief Rose to recommend candidate for remaining open police officer position
  • Township to put up signs for cleanup day at old school property
  • Chad to proceed with Ford F-250 purchase and Gravely mower purchase

Financials

  • $2,453,224 estimated cost for Homes cleanup project from engineer
  • $1,000,000 Brownfield grant applied for (Homes cleanup)
  • $250,000 Goshen funding match for Ohio Public Works paving grant
  • $250,000 state funding for paving grant (total $500,000 project)
  • Over $1.6 million in total ARP-funded projects planned
  • Approximately $52,000 remaining in ARP fund after all planned projects
  • $8,999 for Gravely Pro Stance mower from ARP funds
  • $25,000 for Ford F-250 pickup truck from ARP funds
  • $36,500 maximum for 2022 Chevrolet Blazer police vehicle
  • $60,000 approximate cost per electronic monument sign (last priced 5 years ago)
  • $150 per address fee (current); proposed sliding scale down to $15 for large subdivisions
  • $60 per lot split fee (current); proposed sliding scale down to $20 for large subdivisions

  • $18,000 would be lot split fees for 300-home subdivision at current rate

2022-03-22 Regular Meeting ceremonial
45m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 3.22.22.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting featuring a significant police employee recognition ceremony, including the swearing-in of the department's first-ever police chaplain, Pastor Aaron Ellermann. The board also addressed a resident's concerns about speeding on Shiloh Road, approved an OCJS body worn camera grant, awarded a $301,692 bid for roofing and building facade repairs funded by American Recovery Plan stimulus money, and approved a pre/post construction pavement analysis contract for the Villages of Belmont subdivision.

Resident complaint about dangerous speeding on Shiloh Road between Woodville and 727Appointment and swearing-in of first police chaplain in 51-year department history - Pastor Aaron EllermannPolice employee recognition: top drug interdiction officers for 2021Detective Greg McAllister named Officer of the Year 2021Recognition of Seven Hills Vet Clinic for free canine unit veterinary careNew body camera upgrades deployed to all officersOCJS body worn camera grant for part-time position to manage body camera recordsMajor building repairs funded by American Recovery Plan stimulus moneyVillages of Belmont subdivision - Drees Homes completing 90 patio homes with pre/post road inspectionCitizens Police Academy Alumni Association recognized for support

Decisions (9)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (March 9-22, 2022) passed
  • Motion to approve OCJS body worn camera grant in the amount of $10,713.76 passed
  • Motion to declare surplus/obsolete and sell refrigerator unit and soda machine on GovDeals passed
  • Motion to approve retroactive out-of-town training for Sergeant West for hostage negotiations training in Myrtle Beach, SC at $575 passed
  • Motion to reallocate $186,692 from ARP line item to correct ARP fund line item passed
  • Motion to award roofing and building repair bid to Garland DBS Inc. for total of $301,692 from ARP funds passed
  • Resolution R-020-2022 authorizing contract with Choice One Engineering for pavement analysis at Villages of Belmont subdivision for $2,400 passed
  • Motion to adjourn passed

Action Items (2)

  • Chief Rose and Chad to look into potential solutions for speeding on Shiloh Road and report back to resident Dave Lockhart
  • Administrator to share Captain Hampton's awards ceremony livestream link on township Facebook page at 2pm the following day

Financials

  • $10,713.76 - OCJS body worn camera grant approved
  • $575 - Sergeant West hostage negotiations training cost
  • $186,692 - ARP fund reallocation for roofing project
  • $7,685 - Roofing repairs at Station 18
  • $186,642 - Roof replacement at township administration/police station
  • $107,365 - Cleaning, repairing, and sealing of administration building brick and windows
  • $301,692 - Total bid awarded to Garland DBS Inc. from ARP funds
  • $2,400 - Choice One Engineering pavement analysis contract for Villages of Belmont

BZA Mtg 2.28.22.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals held a public hearing on February 28, 2022. The board approved a resolution for the Cincinnati United Soccer Club conditional use request previously granted, continued a sidewalk variance case for CDDG Goshen LLC pending more information from ODOT, and denied a front-yard privacy fence variance for the same applicant at 1910 State Route 28.

Resolution R-012-2022 for Cincinnati United Soccer Club conditional use approvalSidewalk variance request for CDDG Goshen LLC in TC district along State Route 28ODOT sidewalk maintenance liability issue - township trustees declined responsibilityFront-yard privacy fence variance for CDDG Goshen LLC at 1910 State Route 28TC district connectivity and sidewalk requirements for new developmentSetting precedent for future sidewalk variance cases

Decisions (5)

  • Approved minutes from the last meeting
  • Approved Resolution R-012-2022 granting conditional use for Cincinnati United Soccer Club for an active park with indoor training facility and five athletic fields
  • Continued Case 2022-001 (sidewalk variance for CDDG Goshen LLC) to the next meeting pending ODOT clarification on liability
  • Denied the front-yard privacy fence variance for CDDG Goshen LLC at 1910 State Route 28
  • Motion to adjourn passed

Action Items (3)

  • Staff to contact ODOT to get a defined answer regarding sidewalk liability and whether the property owner can sign the maintenance agreement directly
  • Staff to present ODOT findings to the board at the next meeting (March 22, 2022)
  • Staff to print evaluation criteria handouts for board members at the next meeting

Financials

  • 39,600 square foot indoor training facility for Cincinnati United Soccer Club
  • 30 foot maximum height netting approved for soccer facility
  • 250 feet minimum activity area distance from residential property

BZA 3.21.22 2 of 2.mp4

This is part 2 of a Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals (BZA) meeting. The board returned from executive session and voted to deny the appeal in case BZA 2022-005. They then considered variance request BZA 2022-006 for the same property, zoned R1, seeking relief from code requirements to construct a home behind two existing structures, and voted to approve that variance. The board also discussed upcoming BZA applications, vacancies, and the next meeting date.

  • Motion to return from executive session – approved unanimously
  • Motion to deny appeal BZA 2022-005 – approved unanimously
  • Motion to approve variance BZA 2022-006 as submitted – approved unanimously
  • Motion to adjourn – approved unanimously

  • Zoning staff to work backwards on notification timelines for the next BZA case to ensure 10-day notice and newspaper publication requirements are met

BZA 3.21.22 1 of 2.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals held a public hearing covering multiple cases including property maintenance continuances, a Dollar General sidewalk variance, a construction setback variance for a duplex in Greenstone Trace, a dumpster placement appeal for Sprinkles ice cream shop, and an appeal/variance request for a new single family home on agricultural property on Woodville Pike. The board handled procedural matters including appointing a temporary secretary and approving prior meeting minutes.

Property maintenance enforcement along Goshen Road with court involvementDollar General sidewalk variance due to ODOT refusing to let applicant maintain sidewalks in state right-of-wayFoundation contractor error causing setback violation at Greenstone Trace developmentSprinkles ice cream shop dumpster placement dispute - front yard prohibition and appeal vs variance procedural confusionAgricultural exemption interpretation under Section 3.02 for new home construction on Woodville PikeDebate over whether agricultural-exempt structures can be used to deny placement of new principal structureSmall business owner frustration with zoning application complexityAppointment of temporary BZA secretary

Decisions (8)

  • Approved minutes from February 28th meeting
  • Granted continuance of BZA 2021-03 (property maintenance) until June
  • Granted continuance of BZA 2021-007 (property maintenance) until June
  • Granted variance for BZA 2022-001 (Dollar General sidewalk requirement under 12.08A)
  • Approved Resolution RO21-2022 denying variance to construct 6-foot privacy fence in front yard at 1910 State Route 28
  • Granted variance for BZA 2022-003 (Greenstone Trace setback variance of 3.8 feet due to foundation contractor error)
  • Granted continuance for BZA 2022-004 (Sprinkles dumpster appeal) to allow resubmission as variance request
  • Board entered executive session regarding BZA 2022-005 (Woodville Pike home appeal)

Action Items (5)

  • Sprinkles owner to resubmit application as a variance request instead of an appeal for dumpster placement (BZA 2022-004)
  • Harry Hobart to coordinate notification timeline for Sprinkles special hearing with newspaper publication deadlines
  • Derek to serve as pro tem secretary for meeting notes
  • Appointment of permanent BZA secretary to be continued to next meeting
  • Board of Trustees to address sidewalk/ODOT responsibility issue at future trustee meetings

Financials

  • 3.8 feet variance requested for Greenstone Trace duplex setback (46.6 feet actual vs 50 foot required)
  • Half million dollar home proposed on Woodville Pike agricultural property
  • 15 acres - size of Humphrey property on Woodville Pike
  • 5 acres - minimum lot size for agricultural exemption under Section 3.02
  • 50 foot front yard setback required for Greenstone Trace lots

2022-03-08 Regular Meeting routine
1h 4m ▶ Vimeo

Reg BOT 3.8.22.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting covering ARP fund project updates, zoning resolutions, and infrastructure planning. Major discussions included design services for Fire Station 18 sewer/water/parking lot repairs, road paving priorities, service garage renovation planning, and multiple nuisance/junk vehicle abatement resolutions. The board also discussed community signage, electronic signs, parking lot lighting, and the Brownfield remediation grant application the township will pursue independently after county commissioners declined to complete their part.

Fire Station 18 sewer, water line replacement and parking lot repaving projectARP (American Rescue Plan) fund project planning and prioritizationRoad paving options and priorities for 2022Service department garage renovation vs. new construction long-term planningTownship boundary/welcome signage with new logoElectronic message signs for Fire Station 18 and Station 19Brownfield Remediation Program grant - township pursuing independently after county commissioner issuesNuisance abatement and junk vehicle resolutions for Combs propertiesPreliminary development plan approval for 6600 Patricia BoulevardZone change denial for 1867 Kerber RoadPark district property transfer to Clermont CountyLocal government fund dispute with City of Lublin and City of MilfordParking lot lighting and security concernsCaptain Allen and Lieutenant Estep receiving State Fire Marshal valor awardCemetery mowing contract awardDisappointment with County Engineer's road budget excluding Goshen Township

Decisions (16)

  • Adopted the meeting agenda
  • Approved payment of bills for February 23 through March 8, 2022
  • Approved contract with Choice One Engineering not to exceed $30,000 for Fire Station 18 sewer, water, and parking lot repair design using ARP funds
  • Accepted resignation of part-time firefighter/EMT Jacob Spears effective February 2022
  • Accepted State Fire Marshal's training reimbursement grant of $565
  • Accepted Ohio Department of Public Safety pediatric EMS grant up to $1,500
  • Approved Resolution R-013-2022 denying zone change request for 1867 Kerber Road by Fisher Holmes
  • Approved Resolution R-014-2022 authorizing application to Ohio Department of Development for Brownfield Remediation Program grant
  • Approved Resolution R-015-2022 for abatement/removal of nuisance at 1779 Parker Road
  • Approved Resolution R-016-2022 approving preliminary development plan for 6600 Patricia Boulevard
  • Approved Resolution R-017-2022 determining junk motor vehicle at 1503 State Route 28
  • Approved Resolution R-018-2022 determining junk motor vehicle at 1503 State Route 28
  • Approved Resolution R-019-2022 determining junk motor vehicle at 1503 State Route 28
  • Tabled Item O (Choice One contract review) until next trustee meeting
  • Approved indigent burial of Tamara Hartley at a cost of $703
  • Awarded mowing bid for Goshen and Myers cemeteries to Walker Landscape and Lawn Care at $1,225 per cut for one year

Action Items (11)

  • Administrator Peoples and Chad to continue getting cost estimates for road paving options and bring back to board
  • Administrator Peoples to bring roof and facade project details and quotes for admin building to next meeting
  • Director Holbrook to independently pursue Brownfield Remediation Program grant application for Goshen Township
  • Director Holbrook to have prosecutor review Choice One contract and bring back at next meeting
  • Chad to get pricing on new township boundary/welcome signs and all hardware
  • Staff to investigate electronic sign options for Fire Station 18 and Station 19 on Route 48
  • Staff to get updated pricing on parking lot lighting improvements including LED conversion
  • Chief Myers to share link for Captain Allen and Lieutenant Estep's State Fire Marshal valor award ceremony
  • Administrator Peoples to coordinate walk-over of fire station property for electronic sign placement before parking lot paving
  • Director Holbrook to work with engineering group on Brownfield grant application for Combs properties
  • Future discussion needed on service garage renovation vs. new construction and long-term financing options

  • $565 - State Fire Marshal's training reimbursement grant
  • Up to $1,500 - Ohio Department of Public Safety pediatric EMS grant

  • $60,000 - previous quote for monument sign with electronic sign board
  • $250,000 - service department 2022 budget for road paving
  • $15,000 - previous estimate for parking lot lighting upgrade

2022-02-26 Special Meeting routine
2h 28m ▶ Vimeo

Special BOT 2.26.22.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting on Saturday, February 26, 2022, to approve a corrected pipeline easement resolution for Duke Energy and to conduct a work session on economic development. Mike Hennecamp of Ohio Plan presented an educational overview of economic development tools available to Ohio townships, including enterprise zones, CRAs, TIFs, RIDs, JEDs, and new community authorities. The presentation emphasized Goshen's predominantly residential zoning as a long-term financial sustainability concern and encouraged the board to consider diversifying revenue sources as the township grows. No decisions on economic development strategies were made; the session was informational only.

Approval of corrected Duke Energy pipeline easement resolution (R-011-2022)Review of Goshen Township strategic plan and SWOT analysis from 2020-2021Concern that predominantly residential zoning (~80%) threatens long-term financial sustainabilityEnterprise zones as economic development tool (Bryant Equipment cited as local example)Community Reinvestment Areas (CRAs) for residential and retail incentivesTax Increment Financing (TIFs) as revenue diversion tool for infrastructureResidential Improvement Districts (RIDs) as residential equivalent of TIFsJoint Economic Development Districts (JEDs) as new income tax revenue source for townshipsNew Community Authorities (NCAs) as development financing toolSpringfield Township comparisons and lessons learned from bedroom community challengesImportance of updating the township land use planAnnexation protection through JED agreements

Decisions (2)

  • Adopted the meeting agenda
  • Approved Resolution R-011-2022 authorizing the administrator to execute a pipeline easement to Duke Energy of Ohio Inc., replacing Resolution R-010-2022 which had typographical errors

Action Items (5)

  • Board to consider expediting the update of the township's land use plan as recommended by Mike Hennecamp
  • Administrator Pecrum to facilitate future conversations about which economic development tools may be appropriate for Goshen
  • Consider forming a CIC (Community Improvement Corporation) as identified in the strategic plan
  • Consider consulting with Andy Brossard (economic development finance expert) if the board decides to pursue any specific tools
  • Prepare economic development strategies as outlined in the township's strategic plan

Financials

  • One mill of taxation generates approximately $300,000 in Goshen Township
  • Springfield Township's JED/JEDZ generates $3.5-$4 million per year
  • Springfield Township's TIF district generates approximately $1 million per year
  • Enterprise zones can abate up to 60% of assessed property value for up to 15 years (can exceed with school board approval)
  • TIF exemption can be up to 75% of assessed value, up to 30 years with school board approval
  • Mount Healthy receives approximately $600,000/year from its JEDZ partnership with Springfield Township

BOT Mtg 2.22.22.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting, starting late at 7:50 PM due to a prior meeting running over. Key actions included granting a utility easement to Duke Energy for emergency generator power at the fire station, adding Juneteenth as a paid holiday for township employees, approving use of American Recovery Plan funds under the standard deduction method, adopting an alternate local government fund apportionment method, approving an indigent burial, denying a mowing bid with plans to rebid, appointing a police chaplain, and approving a zone change resolution for properties on Main Street.

Duke Energy utility easement for emergency generator power at fire stationAddition of Juneteenth (June 19th) as paid township employee holidayAmerican Recovery Plan (ARP) fund usage under U.S. Treasury Final Rule standard deductionLocal government fund alternate apportionment method (Resolution R-009-2022)Indigent burial approvalBZA member Mr. Haas leave of absenceCemetery mowing contract rebidAppointment of Aaron Ellerman as police chaplainZone change approval for Main Street properties (R-008-2022)Non-sworn police position appointment

Decisions (13)

  • Approved amendment to agenda adding item C1 (Duke Energy utility easement resolution R-010-2022)
  • Adopted agenda as amended
  • Approved payment of bills for February 9-22, 2022
  • Approved amendment to Township Employee Policy 6.0 adding June 19th (Juneteenth) as a paid holiday per ORC 511.10
  • Approved Resolution R-010-2022 granting permanent utility easement and temporary construction easement to Duke Energy of Ohio
  • Approved use of American Recovery Plan (ARP) funds using standard deduction for continuance of government services per U.S. Treasury Final Rule
  • Approved Resolution R-009-2022 authorizing alternate method of apportionment of undivided local government funds
  • Approved indigent burial of Vence Neal Coogan at a cost of $703
  • Denied mowing bid and approved rebidding
  • Approved non-sworn position appointment (details unclear from transcript)
  • Appointed Aaron Ellerman as police chaplain
  • Approved Resolution R-008-2022 approving zone change for parcels 112214.096 and 112214.097 on Main Street
  • Adjourned at 8:23 PM

Action Items (5)

  • Rebid mowing contract after denying current bid
  • Make additional phone calls to find mowing service providers
  • Mr. Haas granted leave of absence from BZA until October 2022 - revisit at that time

Financials

  • $800,000+ additional ARP funds expected in 2022
  • $703 for indigent burial of Vence Neal Coogan
  • $50,000 received by City of Milford from local government fund (comparison example)

2022-02-22 Special Meeting contentious
1h 36m ▶ Vimeo

Spc. BOT 2.22.22 ZC.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting public hearing on zoning case ZC-2021-008, a request by Grand Communities (developer for Fischer Homes) to rezone 39.48 acres on Kerbert Road from R2 to PDR for a 117-lot single-family residential subdivision. After extensive staff presentation, applicant testimony, and public comment raising concerns about traffic, road width, drainage, school capacity, housing density, and the secondary emergency access road, the board unanimously denied the application because the proposed 1,200 square foot minimum home size did not meet the township's 1,400 square foot zoning requirement.

Zoning case ZC-2021-008: R2 to PDR zone change request for 117-lot subdivision on Kerbert RoadMinimum home square footage requirement (1,200 proposed vs 1,400 required)Kerbert Road traffic, width, and safety concernsIntersection safety at Kerbert Road and State Route 132 (line of sight issues)Secondary emergency access road design (structural grass pavers vs public roadway)Drainage and stormwater retention concerns for neighboring propertiesImpact on Heisler Park/Goshen Junior Sports operations and trafficCounty water line extension and tap fees for existing residentsSchool district capacity concerns with additional developmentCommunity desire for larger, higher-end homes rather than starter/entry-level homesCumulative traffic impact with other proposed developments (Short 48 area)Fire code requirements for two means of ingress/egress for developments over 30 homes

Decisions (3)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda - passed
  • Motion to deny zoning case ZC-2021-008 (zone change from R2 to PDR for 117-lot subdivision on Kerbert Road) due to failure to meet the 1,400 square foot minimum living space zoning requirement - passed unanimously (Pazos yes, Allen yes, Hodges yes)
  • Motion to adjourn the special meeting at 7:36 PM - passed

Action Items (1)

  • Director Holbrook to notify applicant Grand Communities of the board's denial decision in accordance with applicable legal authority

Financials

  • 117 proposed single-family residential lots
  • 39.48 acres total project area
  • 1,200 square feet proposed minimum home size (did not meet 1,400 sq ft requirement)
  • 1,400 square feet minimum required by zoning resolution
  • 1,483 square feet average existing home size on Kerbert Road
  • 35.7% open space provided (35% required)
  • 2.96 dwelling units per acre (3 DUA maximum)
  • 6,240 to 11,657 square foot lot sizes
  • 52 foot minimum lot width proposed
  • Price range of high $270,000s to $320,000s for proposed homes
  • $1,200 per year farming lease income Goshen Junior Sports would lose on 16 acres
  • Estimated 800 cars per day from proposed development
  • 50-foot right-of-way on Kerbert Road
  • 18 feet current road width on Kerbert Road

2022-02-08 Regular Meeting routine
57m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 2.8.22.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting on February 8, 2022, covering storm response costs, ARP-funded capital projects, a police employee wellness gym, and economic development planning. The board discussed the significant ice storm that cost the township over $15,000, reviewed preliminary pricing for roof replacement, parking lot repaving, and sewer/water line repairs funded by ARP stimulus money, and approved converting a downstairs room into an employee fitness facility. A work session on economic development tools was planned for around March 22nd.

Major ice storm response recap and costsARP (American Rescue Plan) fund project planning: roof replacement, sewer/water lines at Station 18, parking lot repaving, service garage, road pavingEconomic development work session planning with Mike Hennekamp to discuss TIFs, tax abatements, and other toolsPolice department employee wellness gym conversion and on-duty workout policy debatePUD major adjustment resolution for The Belmont Phase 2 at 1592 State Route 28Sergeant Dylan West approved as Clermont County SRT negotiatorExecutive session regarding creation of partially grant-funded police positionEmployee anniversary recognitionsPolice officer health statistics and wellness initiatives

  • Motion to adopt the agenda – passed
  • Motion to approve payment dates January 26, 2022 through February 8, 2022 – passed
  • Motion to approve out-of-town travel for Harry Holbert to attend OTA conference in Columbus retroactive to January 27, 2022 at cost not to exceed $300 – passed
  • Resolution approving major adjustment to PUD ZC-2021-005 at 1592 State Route 28 (The Belmont Phase 2) – passed
  • Motion to approve Chief Meyers' travel to FDIC in Indianapolis April 26-30, 2022 at cost not to exceed $1,500 – passed
  • Motion to approve converting a downstairs room into a gym for employee wellness with discussed contingencies – passed
  • Motion to declare surplus/obsolete items and sell on GovDeals – passed
  • Motion to approve Sergeant Dylan West becoming a member of Clermont County Special Response Team – passed
  • Motion to adjourn into executive session at 7:56 PM to discuss appointment, employment, and compensation of a public employee – passed
  • No motion resulted from executive session; authorization given to begin advertising for a new partially grant-funded police position
  • Motion to adjourn regular meeting at 8:30 PM – passed

Action Items (8)

  • Administrator Payton to coordinate with Mike Hennekamp on scheduling economic development work session, possibly March 22 or a Saturday
  • Administrator Payton to bring preliminary pricing on ARP-funded projects (roof, sewer/water lines, parking lot, service garage) to next board meeting
  • Administrator Payton to re-engage architectural firm regarding service garage project
  • Chief Rose and Sergeant West to draft restrictive on-duty workout policy and share via email with trustees for review
  • Chief Rose to obtain pricing for soundproofing, LED lighting, and ceiling improvements for entire downstairs community room area
  • Chief Rose to begin advertising for new partially grant-funded police department position
  • Chad (service department) to coordinate delivery of 300 tons of salt to replenish supply
  • Administrator Payton to explore electronic signage for firehouse and gateway/branding signage using ARP funds

Financials

  • $15,284.94 total storm cost over 2.5 days (not including benefits and retirement)
  • $9,585 for 145 tons of salt used during storm
  • $2,293 overtime hours (62.5 hours)
  • $2,198 straight time hours (115.5 hours)
  • $1,207 fuel cost for 233 gallons
  • 1,113 miles driven by 5 trucks during storm
  • $175,000 to $320,000 preliminary roof replacement estimate
  • $300 not to exceed for Harry Holbert OTA conference travel
  • $1,500 not to exceed for Chief Meyers FDIC conference travel
  • $22,000 maximum total project cost for employee gym
  • $12,000 to $15,000 estimated exercise equipment cost
  • $4,500 budgeted for gym room improvements
  • $450,000 for road paving grant match from ARP funds
  • 50% match required on road paving grant
