2022-01-25 Regular Meeting contentious
2h 4m ▶ Vimeo

BOT Mtg 1.25.202.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting covering a proclamation for Congenital Heart Defect Awareness Week, approved the 2022 budget true-up, scheduled the annual cleanup day for June 4, hired eight part-time firefighter/EMT personnel, approved police training and credit card authorization changes, and held a public hearing on zoning case ZC-2021-007 for a preliminary development plan (PDP) at 6600 Patricia Boulevard for a platform tennis court manufacturing business. Residents voiced significant concerns about truck traffic, child safety, notification procedures, and the appropriateness of commercial use in a residential area, while the zoning director defended the township's notification and procedural compliance.

Congenital Heart Defect Awareness Week proclamation (Feb 7-14, 2022)2022 budget true-up approvalAnnual cleanup day scheduled for June 4, 2022Eight part-time firefighter/EMT new hiresPolice officer training approvals and credit card authorization updatesAmerican Recovery Plan (ARC) stimulus fund spending clarification from prosecutor's officeNew plow truck delivered and displayed at Ohio Township ConferenceAmbulance loaned to Madera Indian Department Fire DistrictZoning case ZC-2021-007 public hearing for PDP/FDP at 6600 Patricia Boulevard (Total Platform Tennis)Resident concerns about truck traffic safety near Kinder Care daycare and residential streetsZoning notification procedures and public records request concernsVGA case 2021-10 conditional use for athletic turf fields and field house approved

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting and public hearing on Zoning Case ZC 2021-009, a request to rezone a property from B1 (Business) to R3 (Residential). Applicant Timothy Hansford of 1918 Main Street, Goshen, presented the case for a zone change on a vacant lot surrounded by mostly residential properties, which had already undergone a lot consolidation. The Board unanimously approved the zone change with no public opposition.

2022-01-11 Regular Meeting routine
53m ▶ Vimeo

Goshen BOT Regular Meeting 1.11.22

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their first regular meeting of 2022, conducting annual organizational business including electing Lisa Allen as chairperson and Bob Hauserman as vice chairperson. Clermont County Commissioner David Painter provided a state-of-the-county update covering Nestle Purina construction, road paving investments, and COVID-19 impacts. The board addressed multiple personnel actions in the fire department, approved the 2022 meeting schedule, set compensation rates for trustees, fiscal officer, and zoning board members, and discussed ongoing issues including the Tommy Combs property cleanup and upcoming zoning cases.

Annual organizational meeting - election of chair and vice chairClermont County Commissioner David Painter state-of-the-county updateNestle Purina manufacturing plant construction progressCOVID-19 impacts on county employees and township staffNew fire chief Ed Myers sworn in (replacing Steve Pegram who became part-time administrator)Tommy Combs property cleanup and bankruptcy proceedingsUpcoming zoning cases including Kerbett subdivision (117 units) and soccer facility on SR 28New IT provider transition and technology upgrades neededTraffic light approved at Smith Road and SR 28New service department truck to be displayed at OTA conventionSolar panel/energy project zoning considerations2022 compensation and meeting schedulesRoad mileage certification including new Smith Road extensionFire/EMS staffing changes and personnel actionsPolice sergeant training approvalsDeath of Greg Carson, Williamsburg Township fiscal officerSubdivisions and development growth concerns impacting emergency services

Decisions (22)

  • Adopted the meeting agenda
  • Approved payment of bills from December 15, 2021 through January 11, 2022
  • Nominated Lisa Allen as Board of Trustees Chairperson for 2022

  • Adopted the 2022 Board of Trustees meeting schedule
  • Scheduled 2022 July tax budget hearing for Tuesday, June 28, 2022 at 6 PM
  • Approved trustees' compensation at $2,025.33 per month from the general fund
  • Approved Fiscal Officer Laura Englert's compensation at $2,774.75 per month from the general fund
  • Set 2022 standard mileage rate at $0.585 per business mile
  • Set 2022 Zoning Commission member compensation at $50 per month, not to exceed $599 annually
  • Set 2022 Board of Zoning Appeals member compensation at $50 per month, not to exceed $599 annually
  • Approved sale of surplus equipment via govdeals.com (Resolution R-001-2022)
  • Approved 2022 road mileage for Goshen Township at 47.062 miles

  • Accepted resignation of part-time firefighter paramedic Taylor Niemann effective 12-23-2021
  • Terminated probationary firefighter EMT Jared effective 12-28-2021
  • Tabled item Q regarding resignation of Sean McCarthy (staying on part-time)

  • Accepted $50 in Kroger gift cards from anonymous donor for police department
  • Approved training for Sergeant Pfeffelheimer to attend OACP Supervisor Training (STEP) at $2,150
  • Approved Sergeants Skates, Collier, Pfeffelheimer, and West to attend Supervising Patrol Critical Incidents Training at $294 per sergeant

2022-01-04 Regular Meeting contentious
1h 29m ▶ Vimeo

Zoning Commission Mtg 1.4.2022

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held its first meeting of 2022, appointing officers, approving the 2022 meeting calendar, and hearing three zoning cases. Case ZC2021-007 approved a preliminary and final development plan for a welding/assembly business at 6600 Patricia Boulevard despite neighborhood opposition. Case ZC2021-008 recommended approval of a 117-lot residential subdivision called Cobblestone on Kerbet Road by Crane/Grand Communities (Fisher Homes), with multiple conditions including a second emergency access point. Case ZC2021-009 approved a zone change from B1 to R3 for parcels at 1918 Main Street to allow consolidation for a single-family dwelling.

Officer appointments for 20222022 meeting calendar approvalPDP/FDP approval for welding and assembly business at 6600 Patricia Boulevard (TPT Holdings - platform tennis court manufacturer)Cobblestone subdivision - 117-lot residential development on Kerbet Road by Grand Communities/Fisher HomesTraffic and safety concerns on Kerbet Road including ball field traffic at John Heisler BallparkEmergency access and second ingress/egress requirements for new subdivisionWater and sewer infrastructure extension from State Route 132Zone change from B1 to R3 at 1918 Main Street for residential consolidationNeighborhood opposition to commercial use on Patricia BoulevardRoad improvement needs on Kerbet Road

Decisions (6)

  • Appointed Terry as chairperson
  • Appointed Lee as vice chairperson
  • Approved 2022 Zoning Commission meeting calendar
  • Approved ZC2021-007 PDP/FDP for 6600 Patricia Boulevard with staff-recommended conditions
  • Recommended approval of ZC2021-008 zone change for 117-lot Cobblestone subdivision at 1867 Kerbet Road with conditions
  • Recommended approval of ZC2021-009 zone change from B1 to R3 for 1918 Main Street

Action Items (7)

  • Staff (Harry Holder) to work with Grand Communities/Fisher Homes on reworking subdivision plan to provide second emergency ingress/egress access
  • Grand Communities to complete traffic impact study review with county engineer and ODOT
  • Grand Communities to provide landscape plan with buffering requirements per Article VI
  • Grand Communities to make road improvements to Kerbet Road as part of phase one of development
  • Applicant for 6600 Patricia Boulevard to comply with staff conditions including paving easement, privacy fence, landscaping, and lighting
  • Applicant for 1918 Main Street to proceed with lot consolidation through Board of Trustees
  • Staff to recruit zoning commission alternates

Financials

  • 117 proposed lots in Cobblestone subdivision
  • 37% open space in Cobblestone subdivision
  • Lot sizes ranging from 6,447 to 17,252 square feet in Cobblestone
  • Home prices ranging from $284,000 to $350,000 in Cobblestone
  • Home square footages ranging from 1,200 to 2,400 sq ft in Cobblestone
  • Minimum 1,400 square foot homes required per staff recommendation
  • 17+ acres at 6600 Patricia Boulevard
  • 0.562 acres consolidated lot at 1918 Main Street
  • Maximum 4 semi-truck deliveries per month at 6600 Patricia Boulevard
  • 400-600 cars on weekends during ball season on Kerbet Road

2021-12-14 Special Meeting routine
32m ▶ Vimeo

Spc. BOT 12.14.21 ZC2021.004.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting to conduct a public hearing on zoning case ZC2021-004, involving a rezoning to a Planned Development (PD) for a storage building development near State Route 28. The board discussed site plan details including building setbacks, privacy fencing requirements, the 150-foot residential district buffer, and the 50-foot property line setback. After hearing from staff, the applicant (Eric White), and discussing potential adjustments to building placement, the board voted unanimously to approve the zoning change with conditions recommended by the Zoning Commission and Zoning Director.

Zoning case ZC2021-004 public hearing and rezoning to Planned Development (PD)Storage building development site plan review near State Route 28Building setback requirements (50-foot property line and 150-foot residential district buffer)Privacy fence requirements and front yard restrictionsSplit jurisdiction between Goshen Township (Clermont County) and Warren CountyRetention pond controls and drainage to adjoining propertiesTraffic concerns at the intersectionClermont County Planning Commission approved 5-0 with conditions

Decisions (3)

  • Adopted the agenda for the special meeting (passed unanimously)
  • Approved zoning case change ZC-2021-004 with conditions as presented by the Zoning Commission and Zoning Director (passed 3-0)
  • Motion to adjourn special meeting (passed 3-0)

Action Items (2)

  • Eric White (applicant) to comply with all conditions of the zoning approval including inside storage only, paved entrance/drives, 20-foot minimum between buildings, 24-hour fire department access via lockbox, retention pond controls, privacy fencing, temporary sign permits, and yearly retention pond maintenance
  • Applicant to work with staff on adjusting building placement to meet 50-foot rear setback requirement

Financials

  • Proposed buildings ranging from 3,000 to 7,800 square feet
  • Property approximately 0.020 acres (combined parcel)
  • 35 feet current setback from property line to building (50 feet required by zoning resolution)
  • 150-foot buffer recommended from residential district for storage buildings
  • Minimum 8-foot tall privacy fence required
  • Minimum 20 feet between each building
  • 24 feet minimum drive width for dual traffic around buildings
  • 180 feet wide at front of lot near Edenton Pleasant Plain and State Route 28 intersection
Bob Hauserman (Trustee/Chairperson)Dan Hodges (Trustee/Vice Chairperson)Lisa Allen (Trustee)Terry (Zoning Director/Staff)Eric White (Applicant)Chairperson Ortega (Zoning Commission reference)

2021-12-14 Regular Meeting routine
40m ▶ Vimeo

BOT Mtg 12.14.2021.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting covering budget approval, zoning enforcement, police and fire donations, radio tower construction, and health insurance updates. The board approved the 2022 budget of approximately $12.4 million in revenue and $10.3 million in expenses, authorized up to $90,000 in ARP funds for a radio/broadband tower project at Station 18, and approved an amended health care plan with a reduced 2.5% premium increase. The board also addressed junk vehicle removal, a fund balance adjustment between police and fire departments, and introduced elected officials and staff for the live-stream audience.

2022 township budget approval ($12.4M revenue, $10.3M expenses)Radio/broadband communications tower construction behind firehouse (Station 18) to address dead air/dead space issuesARP (American Rescue Plan) fund usage for tower and fire station emergency powerFire station emergency generator installation funded by Clermont CountyJunk motor vehicle removal at 6952 Goshen Road (two abandoned box trucks)2022 health insurance plan - significant rate reduction from 8.5% to 2.5% increaseSection 125 cafeteria plan establishment for HSA pre-tax contributionsShop with a Cop event scheduled for the next dayFund balance adjustment correction between police and fire departmentsCoalition of Large Urban Townships (CLOUT) membership renewalAmended 2022 wage resolution to include firefighter engineer pay rateSchool property sale expected to close December 15Bus turn-around sign needed on Hill Station RoadFuture scheduling of zoning hearings at 6:00 PM before trustee meetingsIntroduction of elected officials and staff for live-stream viewers

Decisions (16)

  • Adopted the meeting agenda
  • Approved payment of bills for December 1-14, 2021
  • Accepted $50 donation to the police department
  • Accepted $25 gift card donation to the fire and EMS department
  • Approved Resolution R-054-2021 for junk motor vehicle removal at 6952 Goshen Road (first box truck)
  • Approved Resolution R-055-2021 for junk motor vehicle removal at 6952 Goshen Road (second yellow box truck)
  • Approved the 2022 budget for Goshen Township as presented
  • Approved use of no more than $90,000 in ARP funds for radio/broadband tower project at Station 18
  • Approved contract with Pyramid Network Services Inc. not to exceed $90,000 for electrical work on the tower project
  • Approved amended 2022 wages for elected officials and non-union employees effective January 2, 2022 (adding firefighter engineer pay rate)
  • Approved Resolution R-057-2021 authorizing membership in the Coalition of Large Urban Townships (CLOUT)
  • Approved Resolution R-056-2021 establishing Section 125 premium only plan document and summary plan description for HSA contributions effective January 1, 2022
  • Approved amended Medical Mutual health care plan premium rates for 2022 with 6% rate reduction
  • Approved $38,095.80 fund balance adjustment from police district to fire department
  • Approved request for advance of taxes collected by Clermont County Auditor for 2022
  • Meeting adjourned at 7:47 PM

Action Items (4)

  • Steve Pegram to schedule future zoning commission hearings at 6:00 PM on the same nights as trustee meetings to avoid running over
  • Administration to perform budget true-up in early February 2022 after 2021 year-end reconciliation
  • Darrell to contact the county about getting a bus turn-around sign on Hill Station Road near Mike Day's area
  • Laura to process the $38,095.80 fund balance adjustment between fund 2111 (fire) and fund 2081 (police)

Financials

  • $12.4 million - approximate 2022 budget revenue
  • $10.3 million - approximate 2022 budget expenses
  • $90,000 - maximum ARP funds for radio/broadband tower electrical project at Station 18
  • $90,000 - contract with Pyramid Network Services for electrical work
  • $800,000+ - ARP stimulus funds already received
  • $800,000+ - additional ARP stimulus funds expected next year
  • $38,095.80 - fund balance adjustment from police district to fire department
  • $50 - donation to police department
  • $25 - gift card donation to fire and EMS department
  • $400 - anonymous donation plus gift cards to Kroger for community Random Acts of Kindness program
  • 6% total rate reduction on Medical Mutual health care premiums (from 8.5% increase down to 2.5% increase)
  • Initial insurance renewal quotes started at 46% increase; Anthem lowest was 23%; Medical Mutual came in at 8.5%, then reduced to 2.5%

2021-11-30 Special Meeting ceremonial
1h 22m ▶ Vimeo

BOT Mtg 11.30.21.mp4

Goshen Township held a special meeting highlighted by the ceremonial swearing-in of three new police officers: Chris McMillan, Mike Varon, and Laura Idem. The board also approved the 2022 health care, dental, and HSA plans for employees, conducted the 2021 revenue true-up showing an overall increase of $183,260.12, reviewed the 2022 budget, approved 2022 wages for elected officials and non-union employees, renewed the township administrator's employment agreement, and passed nuisance/junk vehicle abatement resolutions.

Swearing-in ceremony for three new police officers (Chris McMillan, Mike Varon, Laura Idem)2022 health care, dental, and HSA plan approvals2021 revenue true-up across all township funds2022 budget review and presentation2022 wages for elected officials and non-union employeesTownship administrator employment agreement renewalNuisance abatement and junk vehicle removal resolutionsClean audit for 2019-2020 with no findingsOhio State Valor Award for Travis and Todd (fire rescue)Community forum on traffic safety at SR 28/SR 132 Kroger intersectionCharles Snyder road widening project completion and additional paving grantAmerican Rescue Plan (ARP) fund usage and pending federal legislation

Decisions (11)

  • Adopted the meeting agenda
  • Approved payment of bills for October 27 through November 30, 2021
  • Approved 2022 health care plan with Medical Mutual of Ohio effective 1-1-2022
  • Approved 2022 dental plan with Superior Dental Care effective 1-1-2022
  • Approved change to HSA for township employees with contributions of $500 single, $1,000 married/single with children, $1,500 family
  • Approved 2021 township revenue true-up with overall increase of $183,260.12
  • Approved township administrator employment agreement for 2022
  • Approved 2022 wages for elected officials and non-union employees
  • Approved Resolution R-008-2021 for nuisance abatement at 6972 OH-132
  • Approved resolution for junk motor vehicle removal at 2305 State Route 28
  • Approved 15-minute break for swearing-in refreshments

Action Items (6)

  • Administrator Figueroa to present 2022 budget for formal approval at December 14 meeting
  • True-up of 2022 budget to occur in February once 2021 is fully closed
  • Harry Holbert (zoning director) to obtain contractor bid for nuisance abatement on three properties before the 15th
  • Chief Myers to work on strategic plan for fire/EMS department and make recommendation on deputy chief position
  • Administrator to monitor ARP/ARPA legislation for expanded spending eligibility and present projects if law changes
  • Police department to hire one additional officer

Financials

  • $200,000 Community Development Block Grant for Charles Snyder turn lane/road widening project (100% grant funded)
  • Over $400,000 additional grant for road paving received
  • Anthem initial quote was 40%+ increase; settled on Medical Mutual at 8.7% increase for 2022 health care
  • $183,260.12 overall increase in 2021 township revenue true-up
  • 2022 budget: $3.2 million starting unencumbered, $9.2 million revenue, $8.1 million operating, $2.2 million capital
  • $854,611.52 ARP funds already in bank; additional $854,611 expected in 2022/2023
  • HSA contributions: $500 single, $1,000 married/single with children, $1,500 family
  • 3% raise recommended for full-time non-union employees; $0.50 to $1.00/hour raise for part-time employees

2021-11-09 Regular Meeting ceremonial
40m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 11.9.21.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting on November 9, 2021. The most significant action was accepting Fire Chief and Township Administrator Steve Peter's resignation effective January 1, 2022, appointing him as part-time Township Administrator effective January 2, 2022, and appointing Ed Myers as the new Chief of Fire and EMS effective January 2, 2022. The board also reviewed the preliminary 2022 budget, accepted donations to the police department, and received updates on road construction projects and service department activities.

Resignation of Steve Peter as Fire Chief and Township Administrator, transition to part-time Township AdministratorAppointment of Ed Myers as new Chief of Fire and EMS2022 preliminary budget reviewCDBG grant-funded road construction at Charles Snyder and State Route 28 intersectionService department restoration of 2006 dump truck and upcoming delivery of new truckSale of old backhoe on gov deals for $21,500Cemetery fund financial concernsHealth care renewal process and potential cost increasesAmerican Recovery Plan (ARP) funds and pending federal rule changesNew subdivision development and ODOT-required turn lane at Route 28 and Route 48Zoning revenue exceeding projections due to growth and constructionHolly Fair and Light of Goshen parade on November 20thExecutive session regarding employment of a public employee

Decisions (8)

  • Amended agenda to remove items F, G, and H due to Director Holbrook being out sick
  • Amended agenda to add personnel items B1-B5
  • Accepted resignation of Fire Chief and Township Administrator Steve Peter effective January 1, 2022
  • Appointed Steve Peter as part-time Township Administrator effective January 2, 2022
  • Approved employment agreement between Goshen Township Board of Trustees and Steve Peter for part-time Township Administrator
  • Appointed Ed Myers as Chief of Fire and EMS effective January 2, 2022
  • Accepted donation of a $50 Skyline gift card from James Nolder to the police department
  • Accepted donation of four $25 Skyline gift cards from Ms. Pims Child Care to the police department

Action Items (6)

  • Steve Peter to contact fire and EMS personnel personally about leadership transition before information spreads publicly
  • Steve Peter and Cheryl to finalize health care renewal quotes and meet with vendors next week
  • Chad to collect bids for bonus items before final budget numbers come in first Monday of December
  • Service department to have Christmas lights up prior to November 20th Holly Fair and Light of Goshen parade
  • Board to conduct detailed fund-by-fund budget review at November 30th meeting
  • Plan a formal recognition event for new Fire Chief Ed Myers

Financials

  • $200,000+ CDBG grant for Charles Snyder and State Route 28 intersection improvements at zero cost to Goshen
  • $21,500 received from gov deals sale of old backhoe (previously offered $8,500 trade-in value in 2012)
  • 2022 projected revenue of approximately $9 million
  • 2022 projected expenses of approximately $7.9 million
  • 2022 projected capital projects of approximately $2.2 million
  • 2022 projected starting balance of $2.8 million and ending balance of $1.8 million
  • $50 Skyline gift card donation from James Nolder
  • Four $25 Skyline gift cards donation from Ms. Pims Child Care
  • American Recovery Plan (ARP) stimulus funds partially received with second half expected in 2022

2021-11-03 Special Meeting contentious
1h 44m ▶ Vimeo

BOT Special Meeting 11.3.21 ZC-2001-005

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special public hearing for Zoning Case ZC-2021-005, a major adjustment to the Village of Belmont PUD development. Drees Homes, the applicant, proposes building approximately 92 townhome/duplex-style units in the undeveloped Phase 2 section at a density of 3.5 units per acre. After extensive discussion about construction traffic routing, road damage liability, the Charles Snyder/State Route 28 access point, and HOA requirements, the Board approved the zoning case with modified conditions.

Major adjustment to Village of Belmont PUD for Phase 2 development by Drees HomesConstruction traffic routing and road damage liability dispute between Drees Homes and the townshipCharles Snyder Road access to State Route 28 - required by fire chief for emergency ingress/egressHOA structure - whether Drees must join existing HOA or can create separate HOA with shared maintenance agreementODOT involvement in State Route 28 intersection decisionsFire and EMS access concerns - two means of ingress/egress required for developments over 30 homesFay Road safety concerns from residents regarding construction trafficTraffic impact study submitted to ODOT recommending full intersection at Charles Snyder and SR 28

Decisions (6)

  • Adoption of the agenda passed unanimously
  • Zoning Case ZC-2021-005 (Village of Belmont Phase 2 major adjustment for Drees Homes) approved with 12 conditions, some modified from staff recommendations
  • Condition 7 (no construction traffic between 9 PM and 7 AM) was struck
  • Condition 8 (construction traffic to enter via Fay Road only) was struck
  • Condition 12 was modified to read 'if required by existing HOA documents, Section 2 is to be combined with the same HOA as Section 1A Phase 1'
  • Motion to adjourn at 7:15 PM passed unanimously

Action Items (6)

  • Drees Homes to meet with Chad (Service Director) and township staff to walk the site, photograph existing road conditions, and agree on road damage responsibility terms before construction begins
  • Drees Homes to pursue Charles Snyder Road access to State Route 28 with ODOT approval as a priority
  • Drees Homes to submit preliminary development plan to staff for review with all conditions met
  • Drees Homes to review existing HOA documents and approach existing HOA board regarding membership/shared maintenance agreement
  • Director Holbrook to coordinate county and state agency reviews of submitted development plans
  • County engineer requires T-turnaround at stub street for snow plows and fire apparatus

Financials

  • Units priced between $255,000 and $300,000 depending on upgrades
  • 1,418 square feet for one model and 1,750 square feet for the other model
  • 92 proposed new units in Phase 2
  • 72-74 existing units in Phase 1
  • 3.5 units per acre proposed density (reduced from 4.05 in Phase 1)
  • 25-35 off-street parking spaces to be added
  • Building setbacks: front 25 feet, sides 10 feet each, rear 15 feet

BOT Mtg 10.26.21.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting covering IT infrastructure upgrades, surplus vehicle disposals, and a road closure for the Light Up Goshen Parade. County Commissioner David Painter provided updates on federal funding legislation, the Nestle Purina construction project, and regional infrastructure developments. The board also went into executive session to discuss employment and compensation of public employees.

New IT services contract with InTrustIT replacing current providerIT infrastructure repairs and reappropriation of fundsSurplus vehicle disposal (2007 Trailblazer and 2004 Tahoe)Light Up Goshen Parade road closure November 20, 2021Resignation of part-time firefighter/EMT John LahnemanCounty Commissioner Painter update on Senate Bill S3011 (ARPA flexibility)Nestle Purina construction project update in Clermont CountyCitizens Patrol Academy graduationClermont County Trustees Association meeting and jail/training facility tourColumbarium use and donation of additional chairsBody camera upgrades for police department at no additional costDecember meeting consolidation from two meetings to oneExecutive session on employment and compensationFire department house burn training and Holly Fair November 20Village dissolution discussions (Newtonsville completed, Owensville on ballot)

Decisions (10)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed
  • Motion to consolidate December meetings from two to one passed
  • Motion to accept resignation of part-time firefighter/EMT John Lahneman effective October 19, 2021 passed
  • Motion to declare surplus and sell 2007 Chevrolet Trailblazer (VIN last four: 3740) on govdeals.com passed
  • Motion to declare surplus and sell 2004 Chevrolet Tahoe (VIN last four: 5859) on govdeals.com passed
  • Motion to approve road closure on November 20, 2021 for Light Up Goshen Parade on Linton, State Route 28, and Goshen Road passed
  • Motion to approve IT services contract with InTrustIT at average monthly cost of $5,564 starting December 1, 2021 passed
  • Motion to reappropriate $25,280.25 from general fund CDBG line item to contract and services for IT repairs and maintenance passed
  • Motion to enter executive session under R.C. 121.22 G1 to discuss employment and compensation of public employees passed
  • Motion to adjourn at 8:03 p.m. passed

Action Items (6)

  • Pass information to Chad about donating a dozen chairs for the columbarium from the Hawes family
  • Steve to prepare the budget for next week's meeting
  • Contact House representatives to support Senate Bill S3011 for ARPA funding flexibility
  • Coordinate with InTrustIT for December 1 onboarding with one-month overlap with current IT contractor
  • Consider adding 'Goshen Township' identification on back of new ambulances
  • Fire department to coordinate November 20 house burn training with East Fork, Loveland, and Milford departments

Financials

  • $5,564 average monthly cost for new IT services with InTrustIT
  • $11,000 annual increase over current IT services cost
  • $25,280.25 reappropriation from CDBG line item to contract and services for IT repairs
  • $550 million Nestle Purina construction cost ($754 million total project price)
  • 1,200,000 square feet Nestle Purina facility under construction
  • 197 acres purchased by Nestle plus 200 additional adjacent acres

2021-10-05 Regular Meeting contentious
2h 22m ▶ Vimeo

ZC 10.5.21.mp4

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held a public hearing on October 5, 2021, addressing multiple zoning cases including a PBDD final development plan update, a zone change request for self-storage facilities on State Route 28, a major adjustment to the Village of Belmont PUD for new townhome construction by Drees Homes, a minor PUD adjustment for a patio enclosure, and a final development plan review for Barclay Woods. Residents voiced significant concerns about traffic, road access (particularly the opening of State Route 28 and potential closure of Fay Road), HOA integration, and neighborhood safety in the Village of Belmont development.

PBDD final development plan status for property at end of Patricia (ZC 2021-03)Zone change from B1/A1 to PUD for self-storage facility on State Route 28 (ZC 2021-04)Village of Belmont PUD major adjustment for Phase 2 townhome development by Drees Homes (ZC 2021-05)Resident concerns about opening State Route 28 access and desire to close Fay Road entranceTraffic safety concerns including speeding, stop sign compliance, and emergency response timesHOA integration between existing and proposed Village of Belmont sectionsFire chief requirement for two access points per Ohio Fire Code for subdivisions with 30+ dwellingsMinor PUD adjustment for rear patio enclosure variance at 3046 Abbey Way (ZC 2021-06)Final development plan approval for Cove of Barclay Woods (ZC 249)Mustang Road modification to cul-de-sac and Charles Snyder T-intersection recommendation

Decisions (5)

  • ZC 2021-03: No action taken; applicant must restart PBDD and FDP approval process from scratch per prosecutor's guidance
  • ZC 2021-04: Motion passed to continue case; applicant to pursue PUD zoning instead of M1 for self-storage facility at 7098 State Route 28, with conditions to return next month with modified plans
  • ZC 2021-05: Motion passed to approve the major adjustment to the Village of Belmont PUD with T intersection at end of Charles Snyder as recommended by county engineer
  • ZC 2021-06 (Richard Woodward, 3046 Abbey Way): Motion passed to approve minor PUD adjustment for 12x14 patio enclosure with rear yard variance
  • ZC 249: Final development plan for Cove of Barclay Woods reviewed with conditions that applicant meet with staff to finalize remaining plan details

Action Items (6)

  • Staff (Holbrook) to meet with ZC 2021-03 applicant to go over the PBDD and FDP resubmission process
  • Eric White (ZC 2021-04) to prepare modified PUD plans with landscaping, parking, retention, and other requirements and resubmit for next month's meeting
  • ZC 2021-05 recommendation to be forwarded to Board of Trustees with public notification in paper 10 days prior to hearing; adjoining property owners to be notified
  • Drees Homes (Alex Martin) to coordinate with Choice One engineers on road extension to State Route 28 and CDBG road widening project alignment
  • Barclay Woods applicant (Choice One) to meet with staff to work through final remaining plan details and add required details to plans
  • Staff to coordinate with Warren County on ZC 2021-04 self-storage project after township process concludes

Financials

  • $255,000-$300,000: Proposed price range for Drees Homes townhome units in Village of Belmont
  • $244,000: CDBG grant received by township for road widening projects
  • 1,418 sq ft: End unit size for proposed Drees townhomes
  • 1,750 sq ft: Middle unit size for proposed Drees townhomes
  • 150 feet: Minimum setback required from residential lot lines for self-storage facilities
  • 25-35: Proposed off-street parking spaces for Village of Belmont new section
  • 168 sq ft: Proposed patio enclosure size (12x14) for Woodward variance request

BOT 10.11.21.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting covering trick-or-treat planning, ARP funding updates, zoning nuisance and junk vehicle resolutions for two properties, budget appropriation increases, and community updates. Significant time was spent on declaring nuisances and junk motor vehicles at 6833 Linton Road (three parcels with 12 junk vehicles) and 6952 Goshen Road. Budget increases were approved for cemetery mowing, firefighter turnout gear, and fire/EMS fuel costs. Community updates included the sale of the Green property, new businesses, and upcoming Light Up Goshen Parade planning.

Trick-or-treat and trunk-or-treat planning for October 31ARP funding update - still under temporary rules, limited use for townshipsZoning revenue approaching 2021 projectionsVillage of Belmont final development plan - special hearing scheduled November 3Nuisance property declarations and junk vehicle resolutions at 6833 Linton Road and 6952 Goshen RoadBudget appropriation increases for cemetery mowing, turnout gear, and fuelCongressional Fire Caucus update on potential fire station construction grantsGoshen Chamber of Commerce Light Up Goshen Parade request for November 20Green property sale and Ben Puckett building sale to Critter Gone LLCPolice department training new pre-due officersMultiple grant applications submitted for road projects (sidewalks, paving, turn lanes)Chamber shifting focus from community events to business advocacy

  • Approved payment of bills for September 29 through October 12, 2021
  • Approved payment of $199.98 to Bob Summerhill for civilian tire repair from spike strip deployment

  • Approved $6,500 increase in fire/EMS fund for fuel costs
  • Meeting adjourned at 7:47 PM

  • $7.5 billion proposed federal fire station construction grant program

2021-09-28 Regular Meeting contentious
2h 42m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 9.28.2021.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting that included a presentation from the Autism Society of Greater Cincinnati donating sensory kits to first responders, a public hearing on zoning case ZC-2021-002 requesting a zone change from R3 residential to M2 heavy manufacturing for a truck repair business at 6823 Goshen Road, and police department hiring actions. The zone change was denied by the trustees in accordance with Clermont County and the Goshen Zoning Commission's recommendations, with encouragement for the applicant to explore a variance. Three new police officers were appointed and Beggar's Night was scheduled for October 31.

Autism Society of Greater Cincinnati sensory kit donation and first responder trainingPublic hearing on zoning case ZC-2021-002: zone change from R3 to M2 for truck repair business at 6823 Goshen RoadHistory of permit and address confusion between 6823 and 6825 Goshen RoadAllegations of prior zoning director issuing incorrect permits and providing misleading guidanceScheduling of Beggar's Night 2021Hiring of new police officers to fill vacancies and meet 2020 levy staffing goalsBody armor grant awarded to police department

Decisions (7)

  • Motion to amend agenda to add B1 (Beggar's Night scheduling) - approved
  • Motion to adopt agenda as amended - approved
  • Motion to approve payment of bills for September 14-28, 2021 - approved
  • Motion to schedule Beggar's Night on October 31, 2021 from 6-8 PM - approved
  • Motion to deny zone change ZC-2021-002 from R3 to M2 for 6823 Goshen Road - approved (denied the zone change)
  • Motion to accept $175 Kroger gift card from Becky Reed and Modern Woodsmen - approved
  • Motion to appoint Chris McMillan as full-time police officer effective October 10, 2021 - approved

Action Items (4)

  • Harry Holbert to meet with Jeff Kelly and neighbors to explore potential variance options for the truck repair business
  • Jeff Kelly encouraged to work with zoning director on a possible variance application
  • Autism Society training with fire department scheduled for October 7
  • Officer Rose administers body armor grant from Ohio Attorney General's Office

Financials

  • $6,366 body armor grant awarded from Ohio Attorney General's Office
  • $1,000 approximate cost per officer for body armor
  • $250,000 Jeff Kelly's stated investment in his business/building
  • $3,200 annual tax revenue from Jeff Kelly's parcel
  • $175 Kroger gift card donation from Becky Reed and Modern Woodsmen
  • 100 sensory kits donated by Autism Society (50 for police, 50 for fire departments)

BOT Mtg 9.14.21.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting on September 14, 2021, featuring a drone photography presentation from Cincy Drone Pilot for potential township marketing and social media use. The board handled multiple fire department resignations, approved a CDBG road widening project for Charles Snyder Road at State Route 28, accepted American Rescue Plan funds of $854,611, and approved promotions within the fire department. New blue and white township street signs were also approved.

Cincy Drone Pilot presentation for township marketing and social media contentAmerican Rescue Plan funds received - usage restrictions and guidance still evolvingCharles Snyder Road and SR 28 intersection widening project (CDBG funded)Smith Road and SR 28 traffic light progress updateMultiple fire department resignations (4 total)Fire department promotions (lieutenant and captain)Medical director contract transition from US Acute Care Solutions to EMS Doctors LLCRescinding outdated 2004 loud car stereo enforcement resolutionNew township street sign design approved (blue with white background)Fire conference to be hosted in Goshen Township in NovemberFire/EMS awards and recognition banquet praised by trusteesState biannual audit in progressAbandoned Linton Road property sold to new owners

Decisions (25)

  • Adopted tonight's agenda
  • Approved payment of bills from August 11 through September 14, 2021
  • Declared surplus and authorized sale of 2001 Case loader backhoe on GovDeals.com
  • Approved buyback of cemetery grave from Jill Side at Goshen Cemetery Section 2 Lot 43 Grade 5 for $450
  • Accepted four Skyline gift cards from Jamie Ash for police department
  • Accepted eight Starbucks gift cards valued at $40 from unknown donor for police department
  • Approved training opportunity (details partially unclear from transcript)
  • Approved Detective McAllister to attend Digital DNA training in Columbus, Ohio (no cost)
  • Rescinded 2004 resolution regarding enforcement of loud car stereos in neighborhoods
  • Accepted resignation of part-time firefighter paramedic Travis Gilkinson effective August 11, 2021
  • Accepted resignation of part-time firefighter paramedic Troy Myrick retroactive to April 28, 2021
  • Accepted resignation of volunteer firefighter Henry Overbeck effective September 7, 2021
  • Accepted resignation of volunteer firefighter Matt Ailer effective August 19, 2021
  • Declared surplus and authorized sale of 1983 Onan generator on GovDeals.com
  • Authorized Fire Chief Steve Pegram to terminate contract with US Acute Care Solutions for medical direction effective 60 days from notification
  • Approved contract with EMS Doctors LLC (Dr. Charlton) for medical director services at $4,000 every six months
  • Approved Resolution R-021-2021 accepting tax budget amounts and rates for fiscal year 2022
  • Approved Resolution R-022-2021 establishing Fund 2272 for Local Fiscal Recovery Funds
  • Approved increase of Fund 2272 revenue line in the amount of $854,611
  • Approved out-of-town travel for Fire Chief/Administrator to attend Congressional Fire Service Caucus dinner in Washington DC October 1-3 at no cost
  • Waived zoning permit fees for Goshen Township Historical Society Museum sign replacement
  • Awarded CDBG contract for Charles Snyder Road widening and turn lanes at SR 28 to Rack and Baller Excavating for $166,498.75
  • Approved new blue with white background street sign design (bottom right option)
  • Approved promotion of firefighter paramedic to rank of fire lieutenant effective/retroactive September 12, 2021
  • Approved promotion of fire lieutenant to rank of fire and EMS captain effective/retroactive September 12, 2021

Action Items (7)

  • Steve Allgaier and Cheryl to schedule follow-up meeting with Cincy Drone Pilot to discuss contract and budget for township marketing videos
  • Chad Meadows to begin ordering new blue/white street signs and develop replacement plan over 2-3 years with cost analysis
  • Steve Allgaier to send emails to state/federal counterparts regarding ARP fund usage guidance for townships
  • Board members to attend County Township Association meeting Thursday at fairgrounds at 6 PM to pick trick-or-treat date
  • Deputy Chief Myers and fire department to coordinate with park board on demolition of park house including EPA inspections and potential live fire training
  • Fire Chief Pegram to terminate contract with US Acute Care Solutions and execute new contract with Dr. Charlton/EMS Doctors LLC
  • Harry and Steve to continue working on Smith Road and SR 28 traffic light project with developer and City of Davis

Financials

  • $450 - cemetery grave buyback from Jill Side
  • $40 - Starbucks gift cards from unknown donor
  • $854,611.02 - first payment of American Rescue Plan Local Fiscal Recovery Funds
  • Approximately $1.6 million total expected ARP funds (two payments)
  • $4,000 every six months - medical director contract with Dr. Charlton/EMS Doctors LLC

2021-08-03 Regular Meeting contentious
2h 2m ▶ Vimeo

ZC 8.3.2021

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held a public hearing on two zone change cases. Case ZC-2021-002 involved Jeffrey Kelly's request to rezone 6.436 acres from R3 to M2 for a truck repair garage on Goshen Road, which the commission unanimously denied after significant neighbor opposition and a county planning commission recommendation of denial. Case ZC-2021-003 involved TPT Holdings' request to rezone 17.736 acres at 6600 Patricia Boulevard from R1/PBDD to M1 for a platform tennis court assembly facility, which generated extensive neighbor opposition regarding traffic, noise, safety, and property values. The commission discussed potentially using a Planned Development Manufacturing (PDM) designation instead of straight M1 zoning for the second case.

Zone change request from R3 to M2 for truck repair business at 6823 Goshen Road (denied)Zone change request from R1/PBDD to M1 for platform tennis court assembly at 6600 Patricia BoulevardSpot zoning concerns and precedent-setting implicationsDisputed permits and prior zoning director's actions regarding Kelly propertyTraffic, noise, and safety concerns on Patricia Boulevard from residentsProperty value impacts on residential neighborhoodsEnvironmental concerns regarding oil and fluid runoff to O'Banion CreekEasement and sewer access disputes between Stacey Briggs and TPT HoldingsAlternative access route through Gainer Road and state nature preservePBDD vs PDM vs straight M1 zoning classification discussionCounty Planning Commission recommended denial for ZC-2021-002 and approval for ZC-2021-003

Decisions (2)

  • Case ZC-2021-002 (Kelly, 6823 Goshen Road, R3 to M2): Motion to deny passed unanimously (5-0 - Curie, Dinwiddie, Perkins, Lewis, Sutton all voted deny)
  • Case ZC-2021-003 (TPT Holdings, 6600 Patricia Boulevard, R1/PBDD to M1): Discussion held but no final vote recorded in truncated transcript

Action Items (4)

  • Zoning director to notify Jeffrey Kelly of denial and schedule case before Board of Trustees
  • Zoning director to research original PBDD conditions and approved plans for the 6600 Patricia Boulevard property
  • Zoning director to investigate whether a PDM designation rather than straight M1 would be more appropriate for 6600 Patricia Boulevard
  • Mark Keeby (TPT Holdings) to explore renewed negotiations with Buzzard's Roost State Nature Preserve for alternative access from Gainer Road to bypass Patricia Boulevard neighborhood

Financials

  • $250,000 approximate investment by Jeffrey Kelly in truck repair building and business
  • 6.436 acres - size of Kelly property (ZC-2021-002)
  • 17.736 acres - size of TPT Holdings property (ZC-2021-003)
  • 4,800 square feet - Kelly's commercial/agricultural building
  • 34,500 square feet - existing building at 6600 Patricia Boulevard
  • 5,000 square feet - TPT's current facility space on Route 28
  • 40 tons of gravel placed by TPT Holdings on access road
  • 15 employees at Total Platform Tennis
  • 25 platform tennis courts built per year on average

2021-07-06 Special Meeting routine
45m ▶ Vimeo

Spc Mtg BOT 7621

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting for regular business on July 6, 2021. Major topics included an update on the criminal sentencing of Donnie Combs to four years in prison for eight felony charges related to illegal burning, dumping, and operating an illegal solid waste facility, with civil proceedings still pending. The board approved the 2021 street resurfacing bid, road striping, a waste/recycling contract with Rumpke, a postage meter lease, the 2022 tax budget, and accepted a fire captain's resignation due to disability. The board also entered executive session to discuss an appointment.

Donnie Combs criminal sentencing – 4 years prison for 8 felony charges (illegal burning, dumping, illegal solid waste facility); civil case pending for $1.5M fine and asset seizure2021 street resurfacing and striping projectsOWPC state grant application for road infrastructureOnline property maintenance complaint system for residentsNew Officer Nick Culver and Officer Groves joining police departmentResignation of fire and EMS captain due to disabilityRumpke waste/recycling 36-month contract replacing RepublicPostage meter lease with Quadient Leasing2022 tax budget approvalGoshen Park District insurance and kickball tournamentWebsite updatesExecutive session regarding appointment

Decisions (12)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda – passed
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (June 23 – July 6, 2021) – passed
  • Motion to increase appropriations by $24,500 for 2021 striping project – passed
  • Motion to award 2021 street resurfacing bid to Jurgensen's in the amount of $116,394 – passed
  • Motion to approve road edge striping, lane striping, and stop/arrow markings by Arrow Mark Company in the amount of $7,446 – passed
  • Motion to accept resignation of fire and EMS captain effective retroactive to June 29, 2021 – passed
  • Motion to approve 2021-2022 property casualty insurance for Goshen Park District paid to Island Insurance/The Ohio Plan in the amount of $2,408 – passed
  • Motion to approve the 2022 tax budget – passed
  • Motion to authorize township administrator to enter 36-month contract with Rumpke Waste and Recycling Services for township dumpster locations – passed
  • Motion to authorize township administrator to enter 60-month contract with Quadient Leasing for postage meter at $73.26/month – passed
  • Motion to enter executive session to discuss appointment – passed
  • Motion to adjourn at 8:04 PM – passed

Action Items (8)

  • Township administrator and Chad to work on OWPC state grant application for road infrastructure projects
  • Set up online property maintenance complaint form for residents
  • Melissa to continue posting updates to the township website
  • Plan recognition event for retiring fire and EMS captain
  • Push information about Chamber of Commerce appreciation banquet (August 26) to board and administrator
  • Pursue civil case against Donnie Combs for asset seizure and site cleanup
  • Gene Schmidt presentation to be scheduled for August 10 meeting
  • Service department to continue shed construction project at fire station

Financials

  • $24,500 – increased appropriations for 2021 striping project
  • $116,394 – 2021 street resurfacing bid awarded to Jurgensen's
  • $7,446 – road edge striping and lane markings by Arrow Mark Company
  • $2,408 – property casualty insurance for Goshen Park District
  • $1.5 million – civil fine owed by Donnie Combs to the State of Ohio
  • $3 million – estimated cleanup cost for Combs contaminated sites
  • $1,300 – estimated savings over 3 years with Rumpke waste contract vs. Republic
  • $73.26/month – postage meter lease rate locked in for 60 months

BOT MTG 6.22.21

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting featuring the ceremonial swearing-in of Officer Tyler Smith and the promotion of James Beckleheimer to Sergeant. The board approved a nuisance abatement resolution for properties on Clarewell Drive, approved out-of-town travel for fire department leadership, declared surplus vehicles, and opened street painting bids. Park board members Bill VanName and Dave Curry were reappointed retroactively.

Ceremonial swearing-in of Officer Tyler SmithPromotion of James Beckleheimer to SergeantNuisance abatement resolution for Clarewell Drive propertiesSenate Bill 52 – township zoning authority over wind and solar facilitiesAmerican Rescue Plan (HB 111) amendment potentially including townships for direct federal stimulus funding2021 street painting program bid opening (O'Banion Creek Lane, O'Banion Bluff, East Huntley Road, Gibson Road, Linton Road, Moeller Road)Surplus and transfer of fire/zoning department vehiclesContract negotiations in progress by HR/finance directorReappointment of Park Board membersGoshen Park District kickball event planned for August 7

  • Motion to adopt the evening's agenda – passed
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (June 9–22, 2021) – passed
  • Resolution OR-013-021 approved: nuisance abatement for parcels on Clarewell Drive – passed
  • Motion to approve out-of-town travel for Fire Chief to attend IAFC Conference July 27-30 at no cost – passed
  • Motion to approve out-of-town travel for Deputy Chief to attend FDIC conference in Indianapolis August 30 not to exceed $1,500 – passed
  • Motion to declare surplus and sell 2007 Chevrolet Tahoe (VIN ending 446) on GovDeals.com – passed
  • Motion to transfer 2008 Ford Escape from fire department to zoning department – passed
  • Motion to declare surplus and sell 2007 vehicle (VIN ending 7740, current zoning vehicle) on GovDeals.com – passed
  • Motion to reappoint Bill VanName and Dave Curry to Goshen Park Board retroactive to 5-10-2021 for three-year terms expiring 5-14-2024 – passed
  • Motion to adjourn at 8:25 PM – passed

  • Harry Hobert to notify property owner and lien holders regarding nuisance abatement on Clarewell Drive properties and meet with owner
  • Jeff (service department) to send updated road paving schedule/spreadsheet to trustees before July meeting
  • Staff to review street painting bids and make recommendations for July 6 meeting
  • Cheryl Allgaier to present contract negotiations at the next trustee meeting

BOT MTG 6.8.21

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting on June 8, 2021. Key highlights included recognition of Tyler and Tasha Bailey of Wage War Apparel for their community contributions including designing a new police patch and township logo, the appointment of Nick Colliver as a full-time police officer, approval to sell a 1998 Sutton fire engine to Scott Township for $8,500, approval of a HealthSource easement exchange for a CDBG turn lane project, rescheduling the August meeting to avoid conflicting with National Night Out, and appointment of Gary Theodore as BBA alternate.

Recognition of Tyler and Tasha Bailey (Wage War Apparel) for designing new police patch and township logo50th anniversary of Goshen Township Police Department establishmentNew township logo with message 'Love Where You Live'Appointment of full-time police officer Nick ColliverSale of surplus 1998 fire engine to Scott TownshipHealthSource easement exchange for CDBG turn lane widening on Charles Snyder RoadRescheduling August meeting to avoid National Night Out conflict2022 tax budget discussionLEAD program graduation (Harry) and enrollment (Cheryl)Memorial Day event and community cleanup day recapExecutive session followed by BBA alternate appointmentSpeeding enforcement warning on Goshen Road

Decisions (7)

  • Adopted the meeting agenda
  • Approved appointment of Nick Colliver as full-time police officer at $67,237.28 salary effective June 28, 2021 with one-year probationary period
  • Approved sale of 1998 Sutton fire engine to Scott Township (Adams County) for $8,500
  • Approved in concept the expansion of HealthSource entrance/exit on Charles Snyder Road in exchange for easement for 2021 CDBG turn lane widening project
  • Approved moving August special meeting from August 3rd back to regular meeting date of August 10th at 7pm to avoid conflict with National Night Out
  • Appointed Gary Theodore as BBA alternate for remainder of year with term expiring December 31, 2022
  • Adjourned at 8:22 p.m.

Action Items (5)

  • Cheryl to coordinate a photo day with new police car, fire truck, and service truck in front of building with Doug doing drone photography
  • Chief Rose to follow up on speeding enforcement on Goshen Road
  • Administrator Pegram to continue work on 2022 tax budget preparation for July
  • Choice One Engineering designing the CDBG turn lane widening project on Charles Snyder Road
  • Planning for kickball game with police officers in August

Financials

  • $67,237.28 - starting salary for new police officer Nick Colliver
  • $8,500 - sale price for 1998 Sutton fire engine to Scott Township
  • $17,000 - original purchase price of the fire engine
  • $12,000 - Scott Township's initial offer for fire engine

1h 33m ▶ Vimeo

Goshen Twp. BOT MTG 5.25.21

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting with Trustee Hauserman absent due to a family emergency. Major items included the re-approval of a preliminary development plan (PDP) for a large residential development (Zoning Case 309) with extensive conditions, the recognition of Milford Fire Chief Mark Baer who thanked the township for lending a spare ambulance for three months, adoption of a new township logo, and the acknowledgment of retiring legal counsel Ernie Ramis along with the introduction of new attorney Brian Tribe.

Milford Fire Chief Mark Baer thanking township for three-month loan of spare ambulanceRe-approval of Preliminary Development Plan (PDP) for Zoning Case 309 - large mixed-use residential development with 207+ single-family homes and 48 multifamily unitsCommunity opposition to high-density residential development near Deerfield Road and SR 132Termination of medical director Dr. Hoffman and new contract with Dr. Charlton/Emergency Medicine Physicians LimitedAdoption of new Goshen Township logo after multi-year branding effortRecognition of retiring legal counsel Ernie Ramis and introduction of new attorney Brian TribePolice training approvals for Sergeant CollierPotential sale or donation of surplus 1998 Sutphen fire engine to Scott Township (Adams County)Goshen Lions Club Flags for Heroes fundraiser announcement for Fourth of July

Decisions (14)

  • Motion to adopt the agenda passed 2-0
  • Motion to approve payment of bills (May 12-25, 2021) passed 2-0
  • Motion to accept $200 in gift cards and a NutriBullet from CPAA for National Police Week passed 2-0
  • Motion to approve out-of-town training for Sergeant Collier for Standardized Field Sobriety Testing Instructor Training (9/13-9/17/21) at no cost passed 2-0
  • Motion to approve out-of-town training for Sergeant Collier for Sexual Assault Training (10/20-10/21/21) at $230 passed 2-0
  • Motion to approve out-of-town training for Sergeant Collier for Internal Investigations Training (11/2-11/3/21) at $230 passed 2-0
  • Motion to terminate contract with Dr. Matthew Hoffman as medical director effective June 30, 2021 passed 2-0
  • Motion to authorize fire/EMS chief to approve medical director contract with Dr. Charlton of Emergency Medicine Physicians Limited effective July 1, 2021 at $8,000/year passed 2-0
  • Motion to increase appropriations in fire/EMS fund by $1,915.48 for 2019 911 dispatch fee passed 2-0
  • Motion to table sale/donation of 1998 Sutphen fire engine to Scott Township to next meeting passed 2-0
  • Motion to approve PDP for Zoning Case 309 with extensive conditions passed 2-0
  • Motion to adopt new township logo passed 2-0
  • Motion to table executive session (Item M) to next meeting passed 2-0
  • Motion to adjourn passed 2-0

Action Items (5)

  • Harry Holbrook to continue working with developer on remaining zoning conditions for Case 309 final development plan
  • Developer/applicant to submit traffic impact study to ODOT and Clermont County for Case 309
  • Fire Chief Pegram to finalize medical director contract with Dr. Charlton/Emergency Medicine Physicians Limited
  • Trustees to discuss sale vs. donation of 1998 Sutphen fire engine to Scott Township at next meeting with Trustee Hauserman present
  • Executive session item to be addressed at next meeting

Financials

  • $250 donation from Milford Community Firefighters Club toward Goshen firefighters union autism sensory kits
  • $200 in gift cards from CPAA for National Police Week
  • $230 cost for Sergeant Collier Sexual Assault Training
  • $230 cost for Sergeant Collier Internal Investigations Training
  • $8,000 per year ($4,000 every six months) for new medical director contract with Dr. Charlton
  • $1,915.48 increase in appropriations for 2019 911 dispatch fee
  • $12,000 offered by Scott Township for 1998 Sutphen fire engine

2021-05-11 Regular Meeting routine
26m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 5-11-21.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a regular meeting covering police department personnel matters, adoption of a new police patch, and a resolution to petition for the vacation of Broadway Street. Officer Beckelheimer was promoted to Sergeant, an officer's resignation was accepted, unpaid leave was approved for a new officer, and the board approved Resolution R0112021 to vacate three sections of Broadway Street at the request of a property owner.

Police department personnel: resignation, unpaid leave, and promotion to SergeantAdoption of new police patch design with American flag motif by Tyler Bailey at Wage of War ApparelResolution R0112021 to vacate three sections of Broadway Street per property owner Charles Thomas requestGrand opening of Loveland Express at 132 and Deerfield (12-acre reconstructed site)Federal CARES Act distribution rules and upcoming Zoom meetingNew police department floor installation completedFire department new recruit orientation completion

Decisions (6)

  • Approved payment of bills for April 28, 2021 through May 11, 2021
  • Approved unpaid leave of absence for Officer Tyler Smith June 6-12, 2021
  • Accepted resignation of Gareth L. Carter effective April 29, 2021
  • Approved promotion of Officer James Beckelheimer to Sergeant effective May 23, 2021
  • Adopted new police patch design
  • Approved Resolution R0112021 to petition to vacate Broadway Street (three sections)

Action Items (4)

  • Township Clerk to file copy of Resolution R0112021 with Board of County Commissioners and certify copy to County Engineer
  • Branding and logo discussion scheduled for May 18 meeting
  • Township Association meeting on May 20 to discuss CARES Act distribution rules
  • Next CPAAA class to be held in August

Financials

  • Broadway Street vacation parcels: 0.3776 acres, 0.2955 acres, and 0.0821 acres

2021-05-04 Special Meeting contentious
2h 7m ▶ Vimeo

ZC 5421 132 Hearing

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held a public hearing on Zoning Case 309, a PUD/TDP renewal for Lakeville Place Development LLC, proposing 207 single-family homes and 48 multi-family (land condominium) units on approximately 111 acres near Deerfield Road and State Route 132. The original zone change was approved in 2019 but the preliminary development plan expired due to COVID-related delays. Multiple residents voiced opposition citing traffic congestion, water runoff/flooding, infrastructure capacity, property tax concerns, and changes to community character. The commission voted to approve the preliminary development plan with the condition that all seven staff recommendations be met, forwarding the case to the Board of Trustees for their May 25, 2021 meeting.

PUD preliminary development plan renewal for Lakeville Place Development (Zoning Case 309)Traffic congestion concerns on Deerfield Road, State Route 132, and surrounding roadsStormwater runoff and flooding concerns from neighboring property ownersWater pressure concerns in Heritage Woods subdivisionInfrastructure capacity (sewer, water, roads) for supporting new developmentProperty tax increase concerns from existing residentsCommunity character preservation vs. development growthReference to 2005 referendum where 69% of community voted against similar projectOutside agency reviews from fire chief, soil and water, health district, ODOT, Army Corps of EngineersWetland protection and EPA stormwater requirementsHOA-managed amenities including pool, cabana, ball fields, soccer fields, and walking trailsLift station needed for sanitary sewer connection to Deerfield RoadArchitectural review standards for home construction quality

Decisions (3)

  • Approved minutes from the March 23rd meeting
  • Approved the preliminary development plan for Zoning Case 309 (Lakeville Place Development LLC) with seven staff recommendations as conditions, forwarding to the Board of Trustees
  • Motion to adjourn passed

Action Items (7)

  • Applicant (Scott Huber/Abercrombie & Associates and Joe Perugio) to review and comply with all seven staff recommendations regarding missing plan details
  • Applicant to submit landscape plans, cabana/pool details, athletic field plans, and architectural standards documentation
  • Applicant to complete and submit updated traffic study before the Board of Trustees meeting
  • Applicant to work with staff to address code compliance items in Articles 5 and 6 of the zoning resolution
  • Harry Holbert (staff) to forward zoning commission recommendation and agency comments to Board of Trustees for May 25, 2021 meeting
  • Applicant to obtain comment from Clermont County Permit Central (building and planning) which was not yet received
  • Applicant to address wetland delineation and Army Corps of Engineers requirements

Financials

  • 111.47 acres gross site area
  • 255 total units (207 single-family homes + 48 multi-family/land condominium units)
  • 2.31 units per acre proposed density (maximum allowable is 6 units per acre under Table 6-1)
  • Lot sizes ranging from 8,199 sq ft to 23,340 sq ft with average of 11,395 sq ft
  • Homes expected to start in the high $200,000s to $350,000 range
  • 50-foot wooded buffer surrounding the entire property
  • 60 lots in phase one, 56 lots in phase two
  • 28-foot street width (back to back, standard county width)
  • Original 2005 proposal was 330 units, reduced to 255 units

2021-04-27 Regular Meeting ceremonial
1h 42m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 4.27.21

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting on April 27, 2021, highlighted by the ceremonial swearing-in of new Police Officer Kyle Houck. The board approved numerous police department actions including donations, a detective reassignment, a school resource officer reassignment, and an SRO MOU with Goshen Local Schools. The board also adopted the township strategic plan, approved a lease agreement with Clermont County Commissioners for a radio communications tower behind the fire station, approved zoning text amendments, authorized a Cincinnati Bell phone/data contract saving $10,400 over three years, and discussed sidewalk planning and township branding/logo design.

Ceremonial swearing-in of new Police Officer Kyle HouckPolice department personnel changes: detective reassignment, SRO reassignment, resignation acceptanceDonation of four SUVs from Deerfield Township Fire/EMSPromotion of Todd Estep to Fire LieutenantGoshen Lions Club Flags for Heroes programNew police patch design presentationCitizens Police Academy hallway decoration projectMemorial Day parade and celebration planningColumbarium completion and dedication planningFirst quarter financial review - trending well below budgetAdoption of Goshen Township Strategic PlanTownship branding and logo discussion - special meeting planned for May 18Cincinnati Bell telecommunications contract renewalZoning resolution text amendments (Resolution R010-2021)Transient vendors/solicitors/peddlers registration transfer from police to zoningEast Huntley Road quit claim deed for bus turnaround relocationClermont County radio communications tower lease at fire stationSidewalk planning discussion (phase one from Mark Hook School area)COVID stimulus funding discussion and township funding advocacyNew part-time admin assistant Alyssa Hundley startedExecutive session regarding police department vacanciesTrustee Hauserman elected Vice President of Clermont County Trustee AssociationSRO MOU with Goshen Local School District for 2021-2022

  • Adopted the meeting agenda
  • Approved payment of bills
  • Granted permission to Goshen Lions Club for Flags for Heroes program at fire department with permanent bases
  • Accepted $25 gift card donation from Mike Elam for Police Department

  • Accepted $50 donation from Shannon and Esther Wilder for Police Department
  • Approved reassignment of Officer McAllister to detective upon completion of field training duties
  • Accepted resignation of Ryan Saylor effective June 5, 2021
  • Approved reassignment of Officer Groves from duty officer to school resource officer
  • Approved SRO Alcorn to attend 2021 National School Safety Conference at no cost to township
  • Approved SRO MOU with Goshen Local School District for 2021-2022 school year for two SROs
  • Declared surplus/obsolete items for sale on GovDeals
  • Accepted donation of four SUVs from Deerfield Township Fire and EMS Department
  • Authorized fire chief to promote firefighter paramedic Todd Estep to lieutenant effective/retroactive to April 25, 2021 with six-month probation
  • Approved Memorial Day festivities on May 31 coordinated by Goshen Chamber of Commerce
  • Adopted the Goshen Township Strategic Plan
  • Authorized 36-month contract with Cincinnati Bell for phone, data, and TV services
  • Decreased appropriations in zoning budget by $35 for housekeeping
  • Approved Resolution R008-2021 modifying transient vendors/solicitors/peddlers registration from police to zoning department
  • Approved Resolution R010-2021 modifying zoning resolution text amendments (Articles 7.01 C2A, C2B, C2C, and C3)
  • Authorized township administrator to sign quit claim deed for vacating portion of East Huntley Road
  • Approved Resolution R009-2021 lease agreement with Clermont County for radio communications antenna tower behind fire station at $1 lease term
  • Entered executive session to discuss police department vacancies; no decisions made
  • Adjourned regular meeting

Action Items (9)

  • Steve Pegram and Sherell Allgaier to finalize and post job ad for administrative assistant in paper and Facebook within 1-2 weeks
  • Chief Rose to bring new police patch design for formal adoption at upcoming trustees meeting
  • Chad Meadows to complete concrete pouring, landscaping, and preparation of columbarium shelter for Memorial Day
  • Plan dedication ceremony for columbarium with plaque recognizing builders, separate from Memorial Day
  • Schedule special meeting on May 18 for township branding/logo discussion and adoption

  • Steve Pegram to prepare research presentation of 100+ Ohio township logos for branding meeting
  • Chad Meadows to trench electric to columbarium for lighting

  • $50 donation from Shannon and Esther Wilder for Police Department

  • $1 lease term for radio tower land to Clermont County

2021-04-07 Special Meeting routine
42m ▶ Vimeo

Special BOT Mtg 4721

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting to consider Zoning Case ZC-2021-001, a series of proposed text amendments to the township zoning resolution regarding accessory structures. The Board adopted the Zoning Commission's recommendations on lot coverage percentages (30/40/50% based on lot size), deletion of the 2,000 sq ft maximum for accessory structures, and removal of maximum square footage limits in all zoning districts. However, the Board rejected the Zoning Commission's recommendation to lower the maximum accessory structure height from 25 feet to 18 feet, instead maintaining the 25-foot maximum while removing the restriction tying it to principal building height.

Zoning text amendments for accessory structures (lot coverage, square footage, height)Lot coverage percentages by lot size tierDeletion of 2,000 sq ft aggregate cap on accessory structuresRemoval of maximum square footage restrictions in all zoning districtsDebate over accessory structure height limit (18 ft vs 25 ft)Impact of height restrictions on common building sizes and RV storageAgricultural exemptions from zoning restrictionsComparison with neighboring township zoning codes30-day referendum/petition period before resolution can be signed

Decisions (9)

  • Adopted agenda unanimously
  • Opened and closed public hearing with no public comment
  • Approved motion to consider Zoning Case ZC-2021-001
  • Approved 701C2A: Maximum lot coverage of 30% for lots half acre and below, 40% for lots over half acre up to one acre, and 50% for lots over one acre (3-0)
  • Approved 701C2B: Deletion of the 2,000 square foot maximum aggregate for accessory structures (3-0)
  • Approved 701C2C: No maximum square footage of accessory buildings or structures in any zoning district (3-0)
  • Denied Zoning Commission's recommended 18-foot height limit; approved modified 701C3: Maximum height of accessory structure shall be 25 feet, removing reference to principal building height (3-0 unanimous)
  • Directed Planning and Zoning Director to prepare resolution and proposed text amendments (3-0)
  • Adjourned at 7:42 PM

Action Items (1)

  • Director Harry Hobart/Holbrook to prepare a resolution and the proposed text amendments as adopted by the Board

Financials

  • 30% lot coverage for lots half acre and below
  • 40% lot coverage for lots over half acre up to one acre
  • 50% lot coverage for lots over one acre
  • 2,000 sq ft previous maximum for accessory structures (now deleted)
  • 25-foot maximum height for accessory structures (maintained, modified from Zoning Commission's proposed 18 feet)
  • Resolution R-92-2020 initiated the text amendment process
  • Clermont County Planning Commission approved 8-0
  • Zoning Commission approved all items 5-0

BZA 1192021

The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals held its January 2021 meeting, electing officers and approving resolutions from prior cases. Ed was re-elected as chairperson and Bob as secretary. The board approved multiple resolutions from BZA cases heard in late 2020, including variances for property construction and one denial with conditions. Discussion also covered the zoning inspector vacancy, BZA member openings, meeting scheduling, and member compensation limits.

Election of BZA chairperson and secretaryApproval of multiple variance resolutions from 2020 casesDenial resolution with conditions for Sean Ryan propertyZoning inspector vacancy and position detailsBZA and zoning commission member recruitmentMeeting scheduling changes from Mondays to Tuesdays for January/FebruaryBZA member compensation and overtime concernsCase load management and legal time requirements for hearingsProperty maintenance budget and enforcementCOVID-19 social distancing practices

  • Ed elected as BZA Chairperson
  • Bob elected as BZA Secretary
  • Minutes from December 21st meeting approved
  • Resolution R-97-2020 approved: Variance for Wenda Taylor at 6342 Telfer Farm Lane for rear yard construction
  • Resolution approved: Variance for property at 1470 with landscaping conditions
  • Resolution R-003-2021 approved: Variance for 576 sq ft construction at 1565 Road
  • Resolution R-99-2020 approved: Denial of variance for Sean Ryan at 6472 Manila Road with conditions (buildings 1, 3, 4 approved; building 2 to be removed)
  • Resolution approved: Variance for CBDG Ocean LLC at 1910 State Route 28
  • Meeting adjourned

Action Items (5)

  • Zoning staff to advertise openings for BZA member and two alternates
  • Applicant at 1470 required to install 30x10 streetscape landscape area with six 20-inch shrubs by April 30, 2021
  • Sean Ryan required to remove building 2 from property at 6472 Manila Road
  • Zoning staff to send email notifications about upcoming meetings and agenda items
  • Staff to continue work on accessory structures text amendment through zoning commission process

Financials

  • $599 per year compensation limit for BZA members
  • 1500 hours per year / 28 hours per week for zoning inspector position
  • 576 sq ft accessory structure (BZA case 2020-010)

2021-03-23 Special Meeting routine
42m ▶ Vimeo

ZC 3-23-21

The Goshen Township Zoning Commission held a public hearing to continue deliberation on text amendment Case ZC 2021-001, which proposes changes to the zoning resolution regarding lot coverage percentages, accessory structure square footage limits, and maximum height of accessory buildings. The commission voted to recommend a tiered lot coverage system (30% for lots under half acre, 40% for half acre to one acre, 50% for lots over one acre), deletion of the 2,000 sq ft accessory structure cap, no maximum square footage for accessory buildings in any zoning district, and an 18-foot maximum height for accessory structures. These recommendations will go to the Board of Trustees for final approval.

Text amendment ZC 2021-001 to Goshen Township zoning resolutionLot coverage percentage changes for residential districtsRemoval of 2,000 sq ft aggregate cap on accessory structuresAccessory structure maximum height reduction from 25 feet to 18 feetClermont County Planning Commission recommendation of approvalImpact on small subdivision lots and concerns about lot coverage on quarter-acre lotsAgricultural use exemptions for accessory building height

Decisions (6)

  • Approved meeting minutes from February 2nd meeting
  • Approved proposed 7.01 lot coverage: 30% for lots under half acre, 40% for half acre to one acre, 50% for lots over one acre including principal building and accessory structures
  • Approved deletion of existing 7.01C2B (2,000 sq ft aggregate cap on accessory structures)
  • Approved proposed 7.01C2C: no maximum square footage of accessory buildings or structures in any zoning district
  • Approved proposed 7.01C3: maximum height of accessory structures shall be 18 feet, measured per Section 5.05A4
  • Motion to adjourn passed

Action Items (2)

  • Staff to forward zoning commission recommendations on text amendment ZC 2021-001 to Board of Trustees for final approval
  • Fill one remaining alternate position on Zoning Commission

Financials

  • 30% maximum lot coverage for lots under half acre (Zoning Commission recommendation)
  • 40% maximum lot coverage for lots half acre to one acre (Zoning Commission recommendation)
  • 50% maximum lot coverage for lots over one acre (Zoning Commission recommendation vs Trustees' 50% across the board)
  • 2,000 square feet accessory structure cap deleted
  • 18 feet maximum height for accessory structures (reduced from existing 25 feet)
  • 6,000 square feet typical lot size in Woodland Streams subdivision discussed
  • 1,400 square feet minimum house size referenced

2021-03-09 Regular Meeting ceremonial
51m ▶ Vimeo

BOT 3.9.2021.mp4

The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting recognizing Detective Steve Maynard as 2020 Police Officer of the Year and Sergeant Cody Collier for a Drug Interdiction Award. The board approved multiple training travel requests, a fire/EMS budget true-up saving $46,975, a mutual aid agreement, the police collective bargaining agreement for 2021-2023, demolition of a township-owned vacant house, engineering for the Charles Snyder Road/SR 28 widening project, and appointed several members to the zoning commission and board of zoning appeals. Community members raised concerns about transportation for low-income residents and provided park board updates.

2020 Police Officer of the Year recognition for Detective Steve MaynardDrug Interdiction Award for Sergeant Cody CollierFire and EMS budget true-up following collective bargaining agreementClermont County mutual aid agreement renewal (Resolution R005-2021)Police collective bargaining agreement 2021-2023Charles Snyder Road and State Route 28 widening project funded by CDBG grantDemolition of township-owned vacant house at 1910 Main StreetCommunity Cleanup Day scheduled for June 5, 2021Transportation needs for low-income and disabled residents raised by community memberPark board update including Easter egg hunt, pancake breakfast, and Ava's Army special needs playgroundPotential transfer of park to Clermont County Park DistrictZoning commission and BZA appointments and reappointmentsPolice extra duty pay rate increaseHoliday schedule for Christmas and New Year's 2021Four new police officers hired: Charlie Saylor, Kyle Haupt, Tyler Schmidt, Louis CarterEmployee anniversaries recognized
